<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**Define automation in the food industry. List any three advantages of automating a dairy processing plant.**

**Model Answer:**

Automation in the food industry refers to the use of mechanical, electronic, sensor-based, and computer-controlled systems to perform food processing, packaging, quality control, and distribution operations **with minimal continuous human intervention**, while ensuring food safety, product uniformity, and traceability.

Three advantages in a dairy plant:

1. **Consistent HTST pasteurization** — temperature held within ±0.5 °C, eliminating human-error in batch pasteurization.
2. **Automated CIP (Clean-In-Place)** — repeatable, validated cleaning cycles that meet FSSAI hygiene norms without manual scrubbing.
3. **Higher OEE** — typical improvement from 55–60% (manual) to **80–85%** (automated), reducing cost per litre.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for each advantage.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**What is a PLC? Why did its introduction in 1968 revolutionize food industry automation?**

**Model Answer:**

A **PLC (Programmable Logic Controller)** is a ruggedized, industrial-grade digital computer that uses programmable memory to store user-defined ladder-logic or function-block instructions for controlling machinery and processes in real time.

Its 1968 introduction revolutionized food automation because it replaced **rigid, hard-wired relay panels** with **software-based logic**, allowing:

1. Recipe changes in minutes instead of rewiring hours of relays.
2. Direct integration of analog sensors (temperature, pH, conductivity) for closed-loop control.
3. Reliable operation in **harsh CIP environments** — high humidity, caustic exposure, temperature swings.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for revolution points.)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – 2024 Model]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **five generations of automation in the food industry** with a representative food industry application for each generation.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Discuss the **ISA-95 automation pyramid**. With a neat diagram, explain the function of each level in the context of a modern automated dairy plant.

---

#### Model Solution for A(a)

**Introduction:** Food industry automation has evolved through five distinct generations — **Mechanization → Electromechanical Control → Digital/PLC → Networked/SCADA-ERP → Industry 4.0/AI**.

**Generation 1 – Mechanization (1780–1920):**
- Control paradigm: Pure mechanical linkages and line shafts driven by steam engines.
- Representative application: **Oliver Evans' automated flour mill (1780s)** — grain elevated, cleaned, ground, cooled, sifted, and packed without direct human handling.
- Limitation: **No feedback** — blockages caused downtime.

**Generation 2 – Electromechanical Control (1920–1960):**
- Control paradigm: Relay logic, analog thermostats, pressure switches.
- Representative application: **Automatic bottle-filling and HTST pasteurization** in dairies.
- Limitation: Relay panels were **rigid** — recipe changes required physical rewiring.

**Generation 3 – Digital / PLC Era (1968–1990):**
- Control paradigm: Programmable ladder-logic on PLCs.
- Representative application: **Automated CIP sequencing** and retort autoclave control.
- Limitation: **Island automation** — one PLC per line, no plant-wide view.

**Generation 4 – Networked / SCADA-ERP Era (1990–2010):**
- Control paradigm: SCADA over Ethernet, MES linkage to ERP.
- Representative application: **Vision-based fruit grading** and RFID-based traceability in juice plants.
- Limitation: SCADA **displayed** but did not **decide**.

**Generation 5 – Industry 4.0 / Cyber-Physical (2010–Present):**
- Control paradigm: IoT + AI/ML + Digital Twin.
- Representative application: **Predictive maintenance** on filling valves, **collaborative robots** in bakeries, **AI-driven recipe optimization** in breweries.
- Limitation: Cybersecurity, high CapEx, and skill gaps.

**Conclusion:** Each generation was driven by the **limitation of the previous one**, making automation a **layered, not replacement, evolution**.

---

#### Model Solution for A(b)

The **ISA-95 automation pyramid** (ANSI/ISA-95 standard) is the international reference model for integrating enterprise and control systems in manufacturing — including food processing.

**Level 0 — Physical Process:** The actual mixing, heating, cooling, homogenization, and packaging operations on the dairy floor.

**Level 1 — Sensors and Actuators:** Temperature probes (RTD/PT100), pH electrodes, conductivity cells, flow meters, control valves, variable-frequency drives (VFDs).

**Level 2 — PLC / DCS:** Real-time control of the process. For example, a PLC executing PID control to hold pasteurization temperature at **72 °C ± 0.5 °C** for 15 seconds.

**Level 3 — SCADA:** Plant-wide supervisory layer. Provides HMIs, trend screens, alarm logs, batch reports.

**Level 4 — MES (Manufacturing Execution System):** Tracks batches, recipes, yield, downtime, and links them to quality and traceability data.

**Level 5 — ERP (Enterprise Resource Planning):** Business layer handling procurement, sales, finance, and supply-chain planning.

In a modern dairy plant, raw milk intake → standardization → pasteurization → homogenization → packaging → cold storage → dispatch is monitored at **Level 3**, managed at **Level 4** (batch traceability), and planned at **Level 5** (sales-driven production schedule).

*(See Section 4.2 for the diagram.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice – KTU University Exam – 2024 Model]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Discuss the **causal chain** that drove each transition between successive generations of food industry automation. Why did the food industry adopt automation later than the automotive industry?

**(b)** [7 Marks] Explain with examples how **SCADA, MES, and ERP** differ in scope and function. Why is the SCADA → MES → ERP hierarchy critical for FSSAI/HACCP traceability?

---

#### Model Solution for B(a)

**Causal Chain Across Generations:**

Each generation was triggered by the **limitation of the previous one**:

- G1 solved the **muscle limit** but introduced a **sensing limit** (no feedback).
- G2 solved the sensing limit through thermostats and relays but introduced a **rigidity limit** (hard-wired panels).
- G3 solved rigidity through programmability (PLC) but introduced an **island-automation limit** (no plant view).
- G4 solved island-automation through networking (SCADA/MES) but introduced a **data-overload limit** (reactive maintenance).
- G5 solves data-overload through predictive AI and digital twins.

**Why the Food Industry Lagged Behind Automotive:**

1. **Raw material variability** — milk fat varies daily; metal parts do not.
2. **Hygiene constraints** — hardware must survive daily CIP with hot caustic and acid.
3. **Regulatory validation** — every automated change must be validated under FSSAI/HACCP/ISO 22000.
4. **Margins** — food margins (3–8%) limit CapEx recovery compared to automotive (15–25%).
5. **SME dominance** — the food sector is dominated by small processors with limited technical budgets.

---

#### Model Solution for B(b)

**SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition):**
- Scope: **A single plant or a few lines.**
- Function: Real-time data acquisition, HMI graphics, alarming, trending.
- Example: A SCADA screen at a dairy shows the live temperature of pasteurizer-2 as 72.3 °C.

**MES (Manufacturing Execution System):**
- Scope: **A site or factory.**
- Function: Batch tracking, recipe management, yield accounting, genealogy, downtime categorization.
- Example: MES records that Batch #4501 of toned milk ran from 09:14 to 11:42, with 0.4% yield loss.

**ERP (Enterprise Resource Planning):**
- Scope: **The entire enterprise — multi-plant, multi-region.**
- Function: Sales orders, procurement, finance, supply-chain planning.
- Example: ERP places a purchase order for 10,000 kg of sugar for next week's milk-bottling schedule.

**Critical Role for FSSAI / HACCP Traceability:**

The **SCADA → MES → ERP** hierarchy is critical because:

1. SCADA captures **real-time CCP data** (e.g., pasteurization temperature logs) required by FSSAI Schedule 4.
2. MES links CCP data to **batch IDs**, enabling forward and backward traceability in minutes, not days.
3. ERP connects batches to **suppliers and customers**, enabling one-up / one-down traceability mandated by ISO 22000.

Without this hierarchy, a food safety recall that should take **30 minutes** could take **30 days** — exactly what happened in several pre-automation food-borne illness outbreaks.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing SCADA with PLC** — PLC is a *controller*; SCADA is a *supervisory viewer*. Marks deducted if used interchangeably.
> 2. **Skipping the limitation column** — each generation's *flaw* is what *triggered* the next. Skipping this loses 2 marks in 14-mark answers.
> 3. **Ignoring food-specific context** — using generic "manufacturing" examples instead of milk, bakery, beverage, or fruit processing loses relevance marks.
> 4. **No diagram in 14-mark answers** — at least one ISA-95 or timeline diagram is mandatory for full marks.
> 5. **Mixing up ISA-95 levels** — Levels 0–5 must be cited in correct order. Reversing Level 3 and Level 4 is a common error.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- Automation in the food industry is the **integrated substitution** of human perception, decision, and action by sensors, controllers, and actuators.
- The evolution follows **five generations**: **M**echanical → **E**lectromechanical → **D**igital/PLC → **N**etworked/SCADA → **I**ntelligent/Industry 4.0.
- The **ISA-95 pyramid** has **six levels**: ERP (L5) → MES (L4) → SCADA (L3) → PLC/DCS (L2) → Sensors/Actuators (L1) → Physical Process (L0).
- **Oliver Evans' 1780s flour mill** is widely cited as the **first fully automated industrial process**.
- The **PLC** (invented **1968**) replaced rigid relay panels with software logic — the **single most important** trigger of digital food automation.
- **OEE** (Overall Equipment Effectiveness) is the **key KPI** for any automated food plant; world-class target ≥ **85%**.
- The food industry lagged automotive due to **raw material variability, hygiene constraints, regulatory validation, low margins, and SME dominance**.
- Modern food plants integrate **SCADA → MES → ERP** to enable FSSAI/HACCP-compliant traceability in minutes.
- **Industry 4.0** adds **IoT, AI/ML, digital twins, and cobots**, shifting automation from *monitoring* to *prediction* and *autonomy*.
- Always include **at least one diagram** and **quote at least two metrics with units** in any KTU 14-mark answer on this topic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Mile Revision Checklist (KTU Module 1):**
> - [ ] Can you list the 5 generations in order with one food example each?
> - [ ] Can you draw the ISA-95 pyramid from memory and label all 6 levels?
> - [ ] Can you explain why the food industry lagged automotive in 5 points?
> - [ ] Can you define SCADA, MES, ERP and place them in correct hierarchy?
> - [ ] Can you quote OEE, MTBF, MTTR with units?
> - [ ] Can you explain the PLC's 1968 significance to food automation?
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Automation Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT864 - AUTOMATION IN FOOD INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Automation Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the food processing industry, **Automation** refers to the controlled use of various technologies (mechanical, hydraulic, pneumatic, electrical, electronic, and computer-based systems) to monitor, regulate, and execute production operations with minimal or no human intervention.

A **Type of Automation System** defines the structural and functional classification of an automated setup, distinguished by its *degree of flexibility, programming capability, production volume suitability*, and *level of integration* between mechanical hardware and intelligent control software.

The KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEFTT864 – Module 1) explicitly categorizes industrial automation into the following canonical classes:

1. **Fixed (Hard) Automation**
2. **Programmable Automation**
3. **Flexible (Soft) Automation**
4. **Integrated Automation / Computer-Integrated Manufacturing (CIM)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Students must be able to *define, classify, and differentiate* between the four principal types of automation, with at least **one real-time food industry example** for each category. This is a frequently tested 14-mark ESE topic.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a small food business that decides to scale up production of its premium mango juice:

- **Fixed Automation** is like the **dedicated bottling machine** installed in a large dairy plant. The machine fills, caps, and labels *only* 1-litre tetra packs of that specific juice. It runs 24/7 at lightning speed. If the company wants to make 200 ml bottles instead, the entire machine must be physically re-tooled. → *High Speed, Zero Flexibility*.

- **Programmable Automation** is like a **bakery's recipe-driven oven controller**. The baker inputs a new temperature profile (bake bread, then cookies) and the PLC executes the sequence. The machine itself isn't physically changed — only its *software instructions* are. → *Medium Volume, Batch-Oriented*.

- **Flexible Automation** is like a **modern cloud-kitchen assembly line** that can switch between packaging Thai curry or Italian pasta within minutes, using the same robotic arms, conveyors, and vision systems. → *High Volume, High Variety*.

- **Integrated Automation** is like the **entire smart factory** — where the order received on the website automatically triggers the warehouse robot, which triggers the processing line, which updates the ERP and the delivery logistics — *all without a single phone call*. → *Total System Intelligence*.

> [!NOTE]
> The progression from Fixed → Programmable → Flexible → Integrated represents a **hierarchy of intelligence and capital investment**. Fixed is the cheapest to run per unit; Integrated is the most expensive to install but the most responsive to market changes.

---

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Standard Industrial Terms)

The following metrics are universally used in food industry automation literature to compare systems:

- **Production Volume** (units/day)
- **Capital Investment** (₹/unit capacity)
- **Flexibility Index** (range of product variants supported)
- **Changeover Time** ($T_c$, in minutes)
- **Overall Equipment Effectiveness** (**OEE** = Availability × Performance × Quality)
- **Return on Automation** (ROA) = $$\text{ROA} = \frac{\text{Annual Savings} - \text{Depreciation}}{\text{Total Investment}} \times 100\%$$

> [!TIP]
> Always quote OEE in **percentage (%)**. World-class food manufacturing plants target an OEE of **≥ 85%**, while the global FMCG average is approximately **60%**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Comparison Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Classification of Automation Systems

### 2.1.1 Fixed (Hard) Automation

**Definition:** A system in which the sequence of processing, assembly, or packaging operations is **fixed by the equipment configuration itself**. The logical steps are hard-wired (or hard-plumbed) into the machine and cannot be easily altered without a physical re-tooling.

**Operational Logic Steps:**

- Step 1: Product specification is **frozen** during plant design.
- Step 2: Custom tooling, cams, limit switches, and pneumatic actuators are installed.
- Step 3: Material flows in a strictly linear path (e.g., conveyor → filler → capper → labeler).
- Step 4: The line runs at a **single optimized cycle time** ($T_c$).
- Step 5: Any product change requires **physical modification** of the line.

**Why and How:**
- *Why used?* Lowest per-unit cost when product volume is very high and product variety is low.
- *How does it work?* Through mechanical synchronization — gears, sprockets, and timing belts ensure each station completes its task in a fixed window.

**Food Industry Examples:**
- High-speed **Coca-Cola / Pepsi bottling lines** (60,000 bottles/hour).
- **Maggi noodle packaging** lines.
- **Tetra Pak aseptic filling** for UHT milk.

---

### 2.1.2 Programmable Automation

**Definition:** A system in which the sequence of operations can be **changed by reprogramming** the controller, but the physical hardware remains the same. Changeover involves writing new code, not re-building the machine.

**Operational Logic Steps:**

- Step 1: Operations are defined as a **set of instructions** (a program).
- Step 2: The program is loaded into a **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)**.
- Step 3: Sensors and actuators are wired to the PLC's I/O modules.
- Step 4: When a new product is needed, the operator uploads a new program via an **HMI (Human-Machine Interface)**.
- Step 5: The PLC executes the new logic in **scan cycles** (typically 10–50 ms).

**Why and How:**
- *Why used?* Suited for **batch production** (e.g., a dairy producing 10 different flavored yogurts in 8-hour campaigns).
- *How does it work?* The PLC follows a **ladder logic** or **structured text** program that defines actuator states as functions of sensor inputs.

**Food Industry Examples:**
- **Batch pasteurization** of milk and cheese.
- **Recipe management** in a brewery (mashing → boiling → fermentation → packaging).
- **PLC-controlled retort autoclaves** for canned food sterilization.

---

### 2.1.3 Flexible (Soft) Automation

**Definition:** An extension of programmable automation in which the system can be **reconfigured online** (i.e., without stopping production) to produce a variety of products or product mixes. It is the intersection of high volume and high variety.

**Operational Logic Steps:**

- Step 1: A **distributed network** of PLCs / CNCs / robotic controllers is implemented.
- Step 2: **Vision systems** and **RFID tags** identify incoming product variants.
- Step 3: A **Manufacturing Execution System (MES)** re-routes material flow dynamically.
- Step 4: **Industrial robots** with quick-change end-effectors (grippers) handle multiple SKUs.
- Step 5: The system is governed by an **SCADA / DCS layer** for supervisory control.

**Why and How:**
- *Why used?* Modern consumer demand requires *mass customization* (e.g., 50 SKUs of ready-to-eat meals on the same line).
- *How does it work?* Through **distributed intelligence** — no single controller owns the entire process; instead, controllers negotiate via industrial protocols like **Profinet, EtherCAT, or OPC-UA**.

**Food Industry Examples:**
- **Multi-SKU snack packaging** lines (Lays, Kurkure) with robot pick-and-place.
- **Automated cheese slicing and portioning** with vision-guided cutters.
- **Coffee capsule filling** machines that handle multiple flavors.

---

### 2.1.4 Integrated Automation (CIM)

**Definition:** The **highest tier** of automation, in which the entire enterprise — from procurement to dispatch — is interconnected through a unified digital backbone. Hardware automation is fused with Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP), Manufacturing Execution Systems (MES), and Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA).

**Operational Logic Steps:**

- Step 1: A **customer order** enters the ERP system (e.g., SAP, Oracle).
- Step 2: The ERP **schedules** raw material requirements and triggers the **warehouse automation** (AS/RS, AGVs).
- Step 3: The MES **dispatches** the job to the appropriate production line.
- Step 4: **SCADA** monitors real-time process variables (temperature, pressure, flow).
- Step 5: **Quality data** is fed back to ERP for **regulatory compliance** (FSSAI, HACCP, ISO 22000).
- Step 6: Finished goods are **automatically dispatched** via conveyor or AGV to the dispatch bay.

**Why and How:**
- *Why used?* Traceability, regulatory compliance, and global competitiveness demand a *single source of truth*.
- *How does it work?* Through **ISA-95 layered architecture** (Levels 0–4: Field → Control → Supervisory → Operations → Enterprise).

**Food Industry Examples:**
- **Nestlé's fully automated instant coffee plants** in Switzerland.
- **Amul's integrated dairy cooperatives** with real-time milk procurement.
- **Smart cold-chain warehouses** for frozen foods.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Comparison Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Feature | Fixed Automation | Programmable Automation | Flexible Automation | Integrated Automation (CIM) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Product Variety** | Very Low (Single SKU) | Moderate (Batch-based) | High (Multi-SKU) | Very High (Mass Customization) |
| **Production Volume** | Very High | Moderate | High | High to Very High |
| **Capital Investment** | Very High | Moderate | High | Very High |
| **Changeover Time ($T_c$)** | Hours to Days | Minutes to Hours | Seconds to Minutes | Real-time (Auto) |
| **Flexibility Index** | 1 (Locked) | 3 (Software-driven) | 7 (Hardware + Software) | 10 (Fully Adaptive) |
| **Typical Controller** | Relays, Cam Timers | PLC | PLC + CNC + Robot + SCADA | ERP + MES + SCADA + PLC |
| **Programming Effort** | None (Hard-wired) | High (Per batch) | Medium (Recipe-based) | Low (Self-optimizing) |
| **Human Intervention** | Minimal (Monitoring) | Moderate (Programming) | Low (Supervisory) | Negligible (Strategic) |
| **Per-Unit Cost** | **Lowest** | Medium | Low to Medium | Lowest (at scale) |
| **Lead Time for New Product** | Very Long | Medium | Short | Shortest |
| **Best Suited For** | Bottling, Canning | Batch Pasteurization | Snack Packaging | Entire Factory |
| **Food Industry Example** | Coke Bottling Line | Brewery Fermentation | Lays Packaging Plant | Nestlé Smart Plant |
| **OEE Achievable** | 60–75% | 65–80% | 75–85% | **85–95%** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Pearl:** When KTU asks *"Which automation is best for a new product introduction?"* the answer is **Flexible Automation** — *not* Integrated, because the high capital of CIM is justified only when product, volume, and traceability requirements are *all* complex.

---

## 2.3 Engineering Utility of These Classifications

Choosing the correct automation type is a **strategic engineering decision** that affects:

- **Production Economics:** Fixed automation wins when $V \geq 10^6$ units/year of a single SKU.
- **Time-to-Market:** Flexible automation reduces *new product introduction (NPI)* lead time by 40–60%.
- **Food Safety Compliance:** Integrated automation provides **end-to-end traceability** required by **FSSAI's 2024 traceability mandate** for high-risk foods.
- **Energy Efficiency:** Integrated systems can reduce specific energy consumption (kWh/tonne) by **15–25%** through demand-based scheduling.

# SECTION 3: Analytical Matrix — Selection Logic and Quantitative Justification

## 3.1 The Automation Selection Decision Matrix

The choice of automation type is governed by the **intersection of three engineering parameters**: *Annual Volume ($V$)*, *Product Variety ($N$)*, and *Process Complexity ($C$)*.

### 3.1.1 Defining the Selection Variables

Let:
- $V$ = Annual production volume (units/year)
- $N$ = Number of distinct SKUs (Stock Keeping Units)
- $C$ = Process complexity index (1 = simple mixing, 10 = multi-stage aseptic processing)

### 3.1.2 Decision Boundaries (Analytical)

The boundaries are not arbitrary — they are derived from industry-validated case studies (Groover, *Automation, Production Systems, and CIM*, Pearson — referenced conceptually, not quoted).

**Case 1: Low Volume + Low Variety ($V < 10^4$ and $N \leq 3$)**
$$\text{Recommendation} = \text{Manual} + \text{Mechanized Tools}$$

**Case 2: High Volume + Low Variety ($V > 10^6$ and $N \leq 5$)**
$$\text{Recommendation} = \text{Fixed (Hard) Automation}$$

**Case 3: Medium Volume + Medium Variety ($10^4 \leq V \leq 10^6$ and $5 < N \leq 20$)**
$$\text{Recommendation} = \text{Programmable Automation}$$

**Case 4: High Volume + High Variety ($V > 10^6$ and $N > 20$)**
$$\text{Recommendation} = \text{Flexible Automation}$$

**Case 5: Multi-Plant / Regulated / Strategic ($C \geq 7$ and full traceability required)**
$$\text{Recommendation} = \text{Integrated Automation (CIM)}$$

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Selection Logic

Consider the following worked example:

> *A startup produces three variants of cold-pressed olive oil (Extra Virgin, Lemon-Infused, Chili-Infused) in 500 ml glass bottles. The expected demand is 50,000 bottles/year per variant. What automation type is most appropriate?*

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters:**
- $N = 3$ SKUs
- $V = 50{,}000 \times 3 = 150{,}000$ bottles/year
- $C \approx 5$ (filling, capping, labeling, light filtration)

**Step 2 — Map to the decision matrix:**
- $V$ falls in the range $10^4 \leq V \leq 10^6$ → Case 3
- $N = 3$ is on the boundary; however, the *campaign-based* production model is standard.

**Step 3 — Conclusion:**
$$\text{Selected System} = \text{Programmable Automation (PLC-controlled filling line)}$$

**Step 4 — Justification:**
- The line can be reprogrammed to switch between the three SKUs in < 30 minutes.
- Capital cost is recoverable in 2.5 years (vs. 5+ years for flexible).
- Fixed automation would *fail* because $N = 3$ would require three separate lines.

---

## 3.2 Worked Quantitative Example: Calculating the Threshold Volume for Fixed Automation

A **break-even analysis** determines the minimum annual volume $V^*$ above which fixed automation becomes economically superior to programmable automation.

### 3.2.1 Cost Model

Let:
- $C_f$ = Fixed cost of dedicated (hard) automation line = **₹ 50,00,000**
- $C_p$ = Fixed cost of programmable automation line = **₹ 20,00,000**
- $c_f$ = Variable cost per unit (fixed line) = **₹ 2.00**
- $c_p$ = Variable cost per unit (programmable line) = **₹ 4.00**
- $V$ = Annual volume in units

### 3.2.2 Total Cost Equations

The total annual cost for each system is:

$$TC_f = C_f + c_f \cdot V$$

$$TC_p = C_p + c_p \cdot V$$

### 3.2.3 Break-Even Condition

At the threshold volume $V^*$, both systems cost the same:

$$C_f + c_f \cdot V^* = C_p + c_p \cdot V^*$$

### 3.2.4 Solving for $V^*$

Rearranging:

$$C_f - C_p = (c_p - c_f) \cdot V^*$$

$$V^* = \frac{C_f - C_p}{c_p - c_f}$$

### 3.2.5 Substituting Numerical Values

$$V^* = \frac{50{,}00{,}000 - 20{,}00{,}000}{4.00 - 2.00}$$

$$V^* = \frac{30{,}00{,}000}{2.00}$$

$$\boxed{V^* = 1{,}50{,}00{,}000 \text{ units} = 1.5 \text{ Crore units/year}}$$

### 3.2.6 Engineering Interpretation

- If $V > 1.5 \times 10^7$ units/year → **Choose Fixed Automation** (lower per-unit cost dominates).
- If $V < 1.5 \times 10^7$ units/year → **Choose Programmable Automation** (lower capital cost dominates).
- The **per-unit cost savings** beyond $V^*$ is:

$$\Delta c = c_p - c_f = \text{₹ } 2.00 \text{ per unit}$$

For $V = 2 \times 10^7$ units/year:

$$\text{Annual Savings} = \Delta c \cdot (V - V^*) = 2.00 \times (2 \times 10^7 - 1.5 \times 10^7) = \text{₹ } 1 \text{ Crore/year}$$

> [!TIP]
> This $V^*$ calculation is a **favourite 14-mark KTU question**. Always state the assumption: *per-unit variable costs are constant over the analysis horizon* (no economies of scale in the variable term).

---

## 3.3 Comparison Matrix: Quantitative vs. Qualitative Aspects

| Parameter | Fixed | Programmable | Flexible | Integrated |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Typical $V^*$ threshold** | $> 10^6$ | $10^4$–$10^6$ | $> 10^6$ + variety | Enterprise-wide |
| **Relative CapEx Ratio** | 1.0 (baseline) | 0.4 | 1.2 | 2.5–4.0 |
| **Relative OpEx Ratio** | 0.6 | 0.8 | 0.7 | 0.5 |
| **Payback Period (years)** | 3–5 | 2–3 | 4–6 | 5–8 |
| **Skill Level of Operators** | Low | Medium | High | Very High |
| **Maintenance Cost (% of CapEx)** | 2–4% | 4–6% | 6–8% | 8–12% |
| **Scalability** | Poor | Moderate | Good | Excellent |

> [!NOTE]
> The CapEx ratios are *relative* to fixed automation. They represent typical food-industry values from pilot-plant data. They are intended for **comparative reasoning**, not for absolute costing.
