# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Flexible Automation. Give a food industry example. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Flexible (or Soft) Automation is a class of automated manufacturing system in which the production equipment can be **reconfigured online** — without stopping the line — to manufacture a variety of products or product variants on the same physical hardware.

It is achieved through a combination of **PLC/CNC controllers, industrial robots, vision systems, and SCADA-based recipe management**, all communicating over industrial fieldbuses.

**Food Industry Example:** A multi-SKU snack packaging line (e.g., Lays / Kurkure plant) where the same robotic pick-and-place arm and multi-head weigher can switch between 50 g, 100 g, and 200 g pouches of different flavors in under 5 minutes, driven by a vision system that identifies the SKU on the incoming conveyor.

*(Word count optimised for 3-mark answer; bullet points recommended in exam.)*

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Fixed Automation and Programmable Automation. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | Fixed Automation | Programmable Automation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Definition** | Sequence of operations is *physically built* into the machine via cams, gears, hard-wired relays. | Sequence of operations is *software-defined* and loaded into a PLC. |
| **Product Change** | Requires physical re-tooling (hours to days). | Requires only reprogramming (minutes to hours). |
| **Production Type** | Continuous, high-volume, single SKU. | Batch, medium-volume, multiple SKUs. |
| **Flexibility** | None / Very low. | Moderate (limited by program complexity). |
| **Food Example** | Tetra Pak aseptic milk line. | Brewery batch fermentation. |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question with Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Part B questions carry **14 marks** and offer an **internal choice** (either attempt the same question or its alternative). The KTU Premier Engine generates **two independent alternatives** below — students may attempt either.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): Comprehensive Analysis of Automation Types in a Beverage Plant `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** *A beverage company plans to produce 8 variants of fruit juice in 200 ml and 500 ml Tetra Pak cartons. Annual demand is 2 crore units. Compare and recommend the most suitable type of automation with justification.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Parameter Identification:**
- Number of SKUs: $N = 8 \text{ variants} \times 2 \text{ sizes} = 16$ SKUs
- Annual volume: $V = 2 \times 10^7$ units/year
- Process complexity: Aseptic filling → $C \approx 7$ (sterile environment, H₂O₂ bath, sealing)

**Step 2 — Mapping to Decision Matrix:**
- $V = 2 \times 10^7 > 10^6$ → candidate for Fixed or Flexible.
- $N = 16$ is moderate, but **$V$ is high** → mass customization is feasible.
- $C = 7$ suggests **process automation** is mandatory (HMI-based aseptic control).

**Step 3 — Trade-off Analysis:**

| Criterion | Fixed | Flexible |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Capital for 16 SKUs | 16 dedicated lines required (₹80 Cr) | 1 line with quick-change parts (₹12 Cr) |
| Per-unit cost | ₹ 1.50 | ₹ 2.20 |
| Changeover time | 8 hours | < 10 minutes |
| Floor space | 4× higher | Baseline |

**Step 4 — Recommendation:**
$$\boxed{\text{Recommended: Flexible Automation with PLC + SCADA + Vision}}$$

**Justification (for 7 marks):**
- $N = 16$ rules out pure Fixed Automation (would need 16 lines).
- High $V$ rules out pure Programmable (reprogramming 16 SKUs hourly is impractical).
- The line uses **robotic carton erecting, vision-guided filling, and a CIP (Clean-in-Place) system** automatically selected by SKU.
- OEE target: **85%** (achievable in flexible systems).
- **Compliance:** SCADA layer provides FSSAI-traceable batch records.

---

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** *Explain the ISA-95 architecture used in Integrated Automation. List the levels and the function of each level.*

**Model Solution:**

The **ISA-95 (International Society of Automation – 95)** standard defines a **5-level hierarchical model** for integrating enterprise and control systems in manufacturing, including food processing.

| Level | Name | Function | Food Industry Component |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Level 4** | Enterprise Planning | Business planning, finance, procurement, HR. | ERP (SAP), FSSAI compliance module. |
| **Level 3** | Manufacturing Execution System (MES) | Job scheduling, recipe management, batch records, genealogy. | Batch execution, raw material traceability. |
| **Level 2** | Supervisory Control | Real-time monitoring, trends, alarms, HMI. | SCADA server, operator dashboards. |
| **Level 1** | Basic Control | Closed-loop control of process variables. | PLC / DCS controlling temperature, flow, pressure. |
| **Level 0** | Physical Process | Actual sensing and actuation. | RTDs, pH probes, control valves, motors, conveyors. |

**Data Flow:**
- **Downward:** Business plans (L4) → Production orders (L3) → Recipes and setpoints (L2) → Control signals (L1) → Physical actions (L0).
- **Upward:** Sensor data (L0) → Status (L1) → Alarms and trends (L2) → Production KPIs (L3) → Financial reports (L4).

**Engineering Utility in Food Industry:**
- **Traceability:** A quality incident (e.g., *E. coli* in a spinach batch) can be traced from L4 invoice data down to L0 sensor logs within minutes.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** FSSAI's 2024 traceability mandate and ISO 22000 require L0–L4 data integration.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Alternative Comprehensive Question `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** *A dairy cooperative produces 5 types of cheese in batches of 2 tonnes each. Each batch takes 6 hours. Discuss the type of automation suitable for this process and draw the control loop.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Parameter Identification:**
- $N = 5$ SKUs
- $V = 5 \text{ batches} \times 2 \text{ tonnes} \times 365 \text{ days} \approx 3{,}650$ tonnes/year → moderate volume.
- Process: Pasteurization → Coagulation → Curd cutting → Pressing → Aging → Packaging.
- $C \approx 6$ (multi-stage, time-temperature sensitive).

**Step 2 — Decision:**
- Fixed automation is **inappropriate** — 5 SKUs with varying aging times.
- Flexible automation is **overkill** — annual volume is not extremely high.
- **Recommendation: Programmable Automation using a PLC-based batch controller.**

**Step 3 — Justification (7 marks):**

- **Programmability:** Each cheese has a different recipe (temperature ramp, pH curve, coagulation time). The PLC stores 5 recipes.
- **Cost-effectiveness:** Capital cost is moderate (₹ 30–40 lakh) and easily recoverable.
- **Clean-in-Place (CIP):** A PLC-controlled CIP sequence automatically cleans the vat between batches, reducing cross-contamination — a *critical* food safety requirement.
- **Data Logging:** The PLC records all process variables for **HACCP compliance**.

**Step 4 — Control Loop Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP["Setpoint - Recipe from HMI"] --> CTRL["PLC Controller - PID Algorithm"]
    CTRL --> VLV["Steam Valve Actuator"]
    VLV --> VAT["Cheese Vat - Process"]
    VAT --> T1["Temperature Sensor RTD"]
    T1 --> CTRL
```

**Explanation:**
- The PLC compares the measured temperature (T1) with the setpoint (SP) and modulates the steam valve (VLV) to maintain the desired curd-coagulation temperature.
- This is a **closed-loop PID control** — the most fundamental building block of automation.

---

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** *With the help of a block diagram, explain how a Computer-Integrated Manufacturing (CIM) framework operates in a modern ready-to-eat meal factory producing 50+ SKUs.*

**Model Solution:**

A CIM framework in an RTE meal factory operates on the **ISA-95 layered model**, with the following flow:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Customer Order via App or B2B Portal"] --> B["ERP - Order Entry Inventory Check"]
    B --> C["MES - Recipe Selection and Line Allocation"]
    C --> D["SCADA - Line Status Real Time Monitoring"]
    D --> E["PLC - Cooking Mixing Chilling Portioning Control"]
    E --> F["Vision System - SKU Verification"]
    F --> G["Robotic Palletizer and AGV to Cold Store"]
    G --> H["Auto Dispatch with E Invoice"]
    H -. "Customer Feedback Loop" .-> B
```

**Explanation (7 marks):**

- **ERP (L4):** Captures the order, checks raw material stock, generates a work order.
- **MES (L3):** Selects the appropriate recipe (e.g., "Paneer Butter Masala, 250 g tray") and routes it to a free production line.
- **SCADA (L2):** Monitors cooking temperature, chilling rate, line speed; raises alarms on deviation.
- **PLC (L1):** Executes the actual sequence — agitator speed, steam injection, conveyor timing.
- **Field devices (L0):** RTDs, flow meters, load cells, vision cameras verify portion weight and SKU.
- **Dispatch:** Finished trays are robotically palletized and AGV-transported to a temperature-controlled store.
- **Feedback:** The customer's digital feedback updates the ERP's demand forecasting model.

**CIM Justification:**
- With **50+ SKUs** and **mass customization** as the business model, neither Fixed nor Programmable alone is sufficient.
- The **integrated nature** of CIM provides end-to-end traceability demanded by FSSAI 2024 norms.
- OEE achievable: **85–92%** in such plants.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Reasons Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Programmable" with "Flexible"** — they are *not* synonyms. Programmable requires line stoppage for new programs; Flexible reconfigures *online*.
> 2. **Omitting numerical justification** — KTU examiners award the 7-mark part *only* if the student computes or quantifies a metric (volume, $V^*$, OEE, payback).
> 3. **Wrong food industry example** — using non-food examples (e.g., automotive welding) is a partial-mark loss. Always cite a **food processing scenario**.
> 4. **Ignoring OEE and CapEx** — modern KTU questions expect at least *one* quantitative metric in the answer.
> 5. **Skipping the block diagram** — Part B (7 marks) without a labeled block diagram typically receives only 4–5 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read This the Night Before the Exam:**

- **Four Types of Automation:** Fixed → Programmable → Flexible → Integrated (in order of increasing flexibility and CapEx).
- **Fixed Automation:** Hard-wired, single SKU, very high volume. *Example:* Coca-Cola bottling.
- **Programmable Automation:** PLC-driven, batch-based, multiple SKUs. *Example:* Brewery batch process.
- **Flexible Automation:** Online reconfiguration, mass customization, vision + robotics. *Example:* Multi-SKU snack packaging.
- **Integrated Automation / CIM:** Full enterprise integration via ERP–MES–SCADA–PLC layers.
- **ISA-95 Model:** 5 levels (L0 → L4) — Field, Control, Supervisory, Operations, Enterprise.
- **Selection Logic:** Volume + Variety + Complexity → Choice of automation.
- **Key Metric:** **OEE = Availability × Performance × Quality**; world-class target ≥ 85%.
- **Break-Even Volume Formula:** $V^* = \dfrac{C_f - C_p}{c_p - c_f}$
- **Food Safety Link:** Higher automation tier → better traceability → better FSSAI/HACCP compliance.
- **CapEx Ratio (Relative to Fixed):** Fixed = 1.0; Programmable = 0.4; Flexible = 1.2; CIM = 2.5–4.0.
- **Payback Period:** Programmable (2–3 yr) is the *fastest*; CIM (5–8 yr) is the *slowest*.
- **Critical Distinction:** A **PLC** is a *device*; **SCADA** is a *system*; **MES/ERP** are *software platforms*.
- **Common Confusions:** DCS vs. SCADA; PLC vs. CNC; Hard automation vs. Mechatronics.
- **Sensors Used in Food Automation:** RTD (temperature), pH probe, load cell (weight), flow meter, vision camera (SKU), turbidity sensor (clarity).
- **CIP (Clean-in-Place):** A *mandatory* automated system in dairy and beverage plants for hygienic operation.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Types of Automation Systems | PEFTT864**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hard and Soft Automation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT864 - AUTOMATION IN FOOD INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hard and Soft Automation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Automation in Food Industry

**Automation** in the food industry refers to the use of control systems (such as computers, PLCs, sensors, and actuators) and information technologies to handle different processes and machinery in place of human intervention. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEFTT864)**, automation is classified broadly into two principal paradigms based on flexibility, capital intensity, and product variety: **Hard Automation** and **Soft Automation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 1, PEFTT864):** The first module of *Automation in Food Industry* lays the conceptual foundation by differentiating between **Hard (Fixed/Rigid) Automation** and **Soft (Flexible/Programmable) Automation**. This distinction is critical for selecting the right automation strategy for a given food processing plant.

---

## 1.2 Definition: Hard Automation

**Hard Automation**, also known as **Fixed Automation** or **Rigid Automation**, is a type of automation system in which the processing equipment is specifically designed and dedicated to produce a single product (or a very narrow family of products) in a high-volume, standardized manner. The sequence of operations is fixed by the physical configuration of the equipment itself, and reconfiguration to produce a different product is either impossible or extremely uneconomical.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *Hard Automation is an automation strategy where the production equipment is hard-wired or mechanically configured to perform a specific, unchanging sequence of operations on a dedicated product. The control logic, tooling, and material handling paths are physically built into the system and cannot be easily altered without substantial re-engineering.*

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Egg Boiler"

Imagine a small roadside tea stall that uses a **dedicated egg-boiling machine**. The machine is built with one purpose: to boil exactly six eggs at a time for exactly seven minutes. The slots are fixed, the timer is fixed, and the water capacity is fixed. If you suddenly wanted to boil ten eggs or steam an idli, the machine would be useless — you'd have to buy a new machine.

That is **Hard Automation** in essence. The machine is brilliant at doing *one thing only* at extremely high speed and very low cost per egg, but it has **zero flexibility**.

---

## 1.3 Definition: Soft Automation

**Soft Automation**, also called **Flexible Automation** or **Programmable Automation**, is a system in which the production equipment is controlled by a programmable device (PLC, microcontroller, industrial PC) and can be reconfigured — through software changes, tooling swaps, or recipe parameters — to manufacture different products or product variants on the same physical line. The underlying hardware remains largely the same; what changes is the **program (recipe)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *Soft Automation is an automation approach in which the processing equipment is controlled by programmable logic (e.g., PLC, SCADA, DCS) and can be rapidly reconfigured, via software or minor mechanical adjustments, to produce a variety of products in variable batch sizes.*

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Smartphone Oven"

Compare the egg boiler to a **modern smart convection oven with presets**. The same oven can bake a cake, grill chicken, dehydrate mango slices, ferment dough, or steam idlis — all because you press a different button and the embedded controller changes the temperature curve, fan speed, and timer. The *hardware* (the oven box) is fixed, but the *behaviour* is infinitely re-programmable.

That is **Soft Automation**. You sacrifice a little speed and pay a higher upfront cost, but you gain the freedom to manufacture *multiple SKUs* (Stock Keeping Units) on the same line.

---

## 1.4 Quick Comparative Snapshot

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic Anchor:** **"Hard = Hammered-in-place, Soft = Software-driven"**

| Parameter | Hard Automation | Soft Automation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Alternate Name | Fixed / Rigid Automation | Flexible / Programmable Automation |
| Product Variety | **Single / Very Narrow** | **Wide / Variable** |
| Production Volume | **Very High** | **Medium to High** |
| Initial Investment | **Very High** | **Moderate to High** |
| Per-Unit Cost | **Very Low** | **Moderate** |
| Reconfiguration | **Not Feasible / Very Costly** | **Easy via Software** |
| Changeover Time | **None (dedicated)** | **Minutes to Hours** |
| Typical Controller | Relays, Cam Timers, Mechanicals | **PLC, DCS, SCADA, Microcontroller** |
| Food Industry Example | Soft-drink canning line | CIP skid for multi-product dairy |

---

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

In automation engineering, the following **standard industrial metrics** are used to classify systems:

- **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness):** World-class benchmark is **≥ 85%**.
- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** Target for food lines is typically **≥ 2,000 hours**.
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair):** Target is **≤ 30 minutes** for critical food lines.
- **Cycle Time ($t_c$):** Measured in **seconds per unit**; lower is better.
- **Changeover Time ($t_{co}$):** Measured in **minutes**; the **SMED** (Single Minute Exchange of Die) target is **< 10 minutes**.
- **Flexibility Index ($F_i$):** A dimensionless ratio of product variants handled by the system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These metrics are not specific to one topic — they are the **language of automation valuation** and frequently appear in KTU Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hard Automation — Theoretical Deep Dive

### 2.1.1 Operational Logic

Hard automation systems are built around a **fixed sequence of operations**. Each station in the line performs a specific mechanical action (filling, capping, labelling, sealing) and the product moves from one station to the next via a **synchronous transfer mechanism** (e.g., rotary indexing table, linear conveyor with mechanical stops).

The core 'Why' and 'How' behind hard automation can be broken into the following logic steps:

1. **Product Standardization:** The product must be highly standardized in size, shape, weight, and material. Example: A 330 mL aluminium beverage can.
2. **High-Volume Demand:** Production runs typically exceed **1,00,000 units/day** to justify capital cost.
3. **Mechanical / Cam-Based Sequencing:** The control is achieved through **cams, limit switches, mechanical interlocks, and relay logic** — no software.
4. **Synchronous Transfer:** All stations operate in lock-step with a master clock (e.g., a single motor driving a Geneva mechanism).
5. **Dedicated Tooling:** Each station has product-specific fixtures, dies, and grippers.

### 2.1.2 Advantages of Hard Automation

- **Extremely High Production Rate** (e.g., 1,200 cans/minute on a beer canning line).
- **Very Low Per-Unit Operating Cost** (often less than ₹0.50 per unit at scale).
- **High Repeatability & Precision** (tolerances of ± 0.1 mm are routine).
- **Minimal Software Dependency** — robust against cyber threats and software bugs.
- **Lower Skilled-Labour Requirement** at the station level.

### 2.1.3 Limitations of Hard Automation

- **Poor Flexibility:** Cannot adapt to new product sizes without major retooling.
- **High Capital Expenditure (CapEx):** Initial investment often runs into **crores of rupees**.
- **High Downtime Cost:** Any single station failure halts the entire line.
- **Obsolescence Risk:** When the product is discontinued, the entire line may become a sunk cost.
- **Long Payback Period** when production volumes drop.

### 2.1.4 Real-World Food Industry Examples of Hard Automation

- **Bottling Lines for Soft Drinks** (Pepsi, Coca-Cola plants) — 30,000+ BPH (Bottles Per Hour).
- **Canning Lines for Soups, Soups, Vegetables** (e.g., tomato paste, baked beans).
- **Bread Slicing & Wrapping Lines** in industrial bakeries.
- **Milk Pouch Filling Machines** for standardized 500 mL pouches.
- **Boiler Steam Sterilization Retorts** for fixed can dimensions.

---

## 2.2 Soft Automation — Theoretical Deep Dive

### 2.2.1 Operational Logic

Soft automation is built around a **programmable controller** that issues commands to actuators based on sensor feedback. The hardware is generic; the *behaviour* is defined by a **program or recipe**.

The core 'Why' and 'How' behind soft automation:

1. **Programmable Logic:** A **PLC (Programmable Logic Controller)** executes Ladder Logic or Function Block code, allowing easy modification of sequence, timing, and setpoints.
2. **Recipe Management:** Different product variants (e.g., chocolate milk vs. strawberry milk) are stored as **recipes** in HMI/SCADA memory and recalled on demand.
3. **Sensor-Driven Feedback:** Sensors (temperature, flow, level, pH, conductivity) provide real-time data for closed-loop control (e.g., PID control of a pasteurizer).
4. **Modular Hardware:** Equipment is built from standard modules (VFDs, servo drives, pneumatic valves) that can be repurposed.
5. **Asynchronous / Distributed Operation:** Stations can operate semi-independently using industrial communication protocols (PROFIBUS, Modbus, EtherNet/IP, OPC-UA).

### 2.2.2 Advantages of Soft Automation

- **High Flexibility** — same line can produce 5–10 product variants.
- **Quick Changeover** — recipe selection takes **seconds to minutes**.
- **Easier Upgrades** — new features added via software patches, not new hardware.
- **Better Data Acquisition** — for HACCP, FSSC 22000, and regulatory traceability.
- **Lower Mechanical Complexity** — fewer cams, gears, limit switches.

### 2.2.3 Limitations of Soft Automation

- **Higher Per-Unit Cost** due to controller hardware and software.
- **Slower Cycle Times** compared to hard automation (e.g., 6,000 BPH vs. 30,000 BPH).
- **Cybersecurity Vulnerability** — networked systems need firewalls, OT/IT segregation.
- **Requires Skilled Workforce** (PLC programmers, instrumentation engineers).
- **Software Validation Burden** (critical in food safety — GAMP 5 guidelines).

### 2.2.4 Real-World Food Industry Examples of Soft Automation

- **CIP (Clean-In-Place) Skids** in dairy and breweries — multi-recipe cleaning cycles.
- **PLC-Controlled Batch Pasteurizers** in ice cream plants.
- **Robotic Pick-and-Place** for delicate bakery items or chocolates.
- **Computer-Controlled Retort Sterilization** (e.g., Stock Rotamat).
- **SCADA-Monitored Fermentation Tanks** in wineries and breweries.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** The following table contains the **high-yield formulas** and **decision criteria** a KTU student must memorize for this module. Many of these appear directly in numerical questions worth 7–14 marks.

| # | Concept / Formula | Symbol | Description | Typical Units / Value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Production Rate | $R$ | Units produced per unit time | units/hour, kg/hour |
| 2 | Cycle Time | $t_c$ | Time between two successive units | seconds/unit |
| 3 | Relationship | $t_c = \dfrac{3600}{R}$ | Cycle time from production rate | seconds/unit |
| 4 | Overall Equipment Effectiveness | $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | Availability $\times$ Performance $\times$ Quality | dimensionless, **0–1** (or **%**) |
| 5 | Availability | $A = \dfrac{\text{Uptime}}{\text{Planned Production Time}}$ | Uptime fraction | dimensionless |
| 6 | Performance | $P = \dfrac{t_c^{\text{ideal}}}{t_c^{\text{actual}}}$ | Speed efficiency | dimensionless |
| 7 | Quality | $Q = \dfrac{\text{Good Units}}{\text{Total Units}}$ | Yield fraction | dimensionless |
| 8 | MTBF | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{\text{Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ | Mean Time Between Failures | hours |
| 9 | MTTR | $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{\text{Total Repair Time}}{\text{Number of Repairs}}$ | Mean Time To Repair | hours |
| 10 | System Availability | $A_s = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Inherent availability | dimensionless |
| 11 | Flexibility Index | $F_i = \dfrac{N_v}{t_{co}}$ | $N_v$ = number of variants; $t_{co}$ = changeover time | variants/minute |
| 12 | Payback Period | $T_{pb} = \dfrac{C_{\text{capex}}}{\text{Annual Savings}}$ | Time to recover investment | years |
| 13 | Net Present Value | $\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_t}{(1+r)^t} - C_0$ | $C_t$ = cash flow, $r$ = discount rate, $C_0$ = initial cost | ₹ (currency) |
| 14 | ROI | $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{\text{Net Profit}}{\text{Cost of Investment}} \times 100$ | Return on Investment | **%** |
| 15 | Break-Even Volume | $V_{be} = \dfrac{C_{\text{fixed}}}{p - v}$ | $C_{\text{fixed}}$ = fixed cost; $p$ = price; $v$ = variable cost | units/year |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE (per KTU Engine Rule):** All absolute-value and conditional notations in equations use **$\vert$** or **$\mid$** rather than the pipe symbol to preserve markdown table integrity.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility — Why Does This Matter?

The choice between **hard** and **soft** automation is not academic — it has direct **Capital Expenditure (CapEx)** and **Operational Expenditure (OpEx)** consequences:

- A **hard automation line** for biscuits may cost **₹ 8–15 crores** but produce **5,000 kg/hour** for a single SKU.
- A **soft automation line** with PLC + robots may cost **₹ 3–6 crores** for the same throughput, but can switch between 4–6 SKUs.

For a **multi-SKU, mid-volume** food manufacturer (e.g., a private-label sauce producer making ketchup, mustard, and BBQ sauce on the same line), **soft automation is economically rational**. For a **monoproduct, ultra-high-volume** manufacturer (e.g., a regional dairy producing only 1-litre milk pouches), **hard automation delivers the lowest cost per litre**.

This is exactly the kind of **design decision** KTU expects you to defend in a 14-mark Part B question.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Choose Hard vs. Soft Automation

The selection of an automation strategy is governed by a structured **decision matrix** that KTU students are routinely tested on. Below is the **step-by-step analytical derivation** of the decision logic, which you can reproduce verbatim in an exam.

### Step 1 — Quantify Annual Production Volume ($V$)

The first parameter is the **annual production volume**, measured in units or kg/year:

$$V = R \times H_{\text{op}} \times D_{\text{op}} \times \eta$$

where:
- $R$ = Rated production rate of the equipment (units/hour or kg/hour)
- $H_{\text{op}}$ = Operating hours per day (typically 8, 16, or 24 hours)
- $D_{\text{op}}$ = Operating days per year (typically 300 days, accounting for maintenance shutdowns)
- $\eta$ = Overall efficiency factor (OEE, typically 0.60 – 0.85 for food lines)

> **Logic:** This equation gives the **effective annual output** the line must deliver. The higher the volume, the stronger the economic case for **hard automation** (since its per-unit cost advantage scales with volume).

### Step 2 — Compute Per-Unit Cost for Each Strategy

For **Hard Automation** (HA):

$$C_{\text{HA}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{capex,HA}} / T_{\text{life}} + C_{\text{opex,HA}}}{V}$$

For **Soft Automation** (SA):

$$C_{\text{SA}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{capex,SA}} / T_{\text{life}} + C_{\text{opex,SA}}}{V} + C_{\text{changeover}} \cdot N_{co}$$

where:
- $C_{\text{capex}}$ = Initial capital cost (₹)
- $T_{\text{life}}$ = Useful life of the equipment (typically 7–10 years)
- $C_{\text{opex}}$ = Annual operating cost (₹/year) including labour, energy, spares
- $C_{\text{changeover}}$ = Cost of a single changeover event (₹)
- $N_{co}$ = Number of changeovers per year
- $V$ = Annual production volume (units/year)

> **Logic:** The numerator amortizes capital across the equipment life, the denominator distributes the cost over the volume. Soft automation carries an **extra changeover penalty** that hard automation does not.

### Step 3 — Apply the Decision Rule

The selection criterion is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Choose Hard Automation} &\iff V \geq V_{\text{critical}} \;\text{AND}\; N_v = 1 \\
\text{Choose Soft Automation} &\iff N_v \geq 2 \;\text{OR}\; V < V_{\text{critical}}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $V_{\text{critical}}$ is the **break-even production volume** at which both options yield identical per-unit cost.

### Step 4 — Solve for $V_{\text{critical}}$ Analytically

Setting $C_{\text{HA}} = C_{\text{SA}}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{C_{\text{capex,HA}} / T_{\text{life}} + C_{\text{opex,HA}}}{V} &= \dfrac{C_{\text{capex,SA}} / T_{\text{life}} + C_{\text{opex,SA}}}{V} + C_{\text{changeover}} \cdot N_{co} \\
\dfrac{(C_{\text{capex,HA}} - C_{\text{capex,SA}})}{T_{\text{life}}} + (C_{\text{opex,HA}} - C_{\text{opex,SA}}) &= V \cdot C_{\text{changeover}} \cdot N_{co} \\
V_{\text{critical}} &= \dfrac{(C_{\text{capex,HA}} - C_{\text{capex,SA}}) / T_{\text{life}} + (C_{\text{opex,HA}} - C_{\text{opex,SA}})}{C_{\text{changeover}} \cdot N_{co}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Any annual volume *above* $V_{\text{critical}}$ — combined with a single product variant — favours hard automation. Any annual volume *below* $V_{\text{critical}}$ — or any need for more than one product variant — favours soft automation.

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example (Food Industry Case)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked-Out Case:** A Kerala-based coconut oil bottling company is evaluating automation. Use the values below to determine which strategy to adopt.

**Given Data:**

- Hard automation: $C_{\text{capex,HA}} = \text{₹ 1,20,00,000}$, $C_{\text{opex,HA}} = \text{₹ 8,00,000/year}$
- Soft automation: $C_{\text{capex,SA}} = \text{₹ 60,00,000}$, $C_{\text{opex,SA}} = \text{₹ 12,00,000/year}$
- $T_{\text{life}} = 10 \text{ years}$
- $C_{\text{changeover}} = \text{₹ 5,000 per event}$, $N_{co} = 24 \text{ events/year}$

**Step 5a — Compute Numerator Terms**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{C_{\text{capex,HA}} - C_{\text{capex,SA}}}{T_{\text{life}}} &= \dfrac{1,20,00,000 - 60,00,000}{10} = \dfrac{60,00,000}{10} = 6,00,000 \;\text{₹/year} \\
C_{\text{opex,HA}} - C_{\text{opex,SA}} &= 8,00,000 - 12,00,000 = -4,00,000 \;\text{₹/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5b — Compute Denominator**

$$
C_{\text{changeover}} \cdot N_{co} = 5,000 \times 24 = 1,20,000 \;\text{₹/year per unit/year}
$$

**Step 5c — Compute $V_{\text{critical}}$**

$$
V_{\text{critical}} = \dfrac{6,00,000 + (-4,00,000)}{1,20,000} = \dfrac{2,00,000}{1,20,000} \approx 1.67 \;\text{units/year}
$$

**Step 5d — Interpretation**

Since $V_{\text{critical}} \approx 1.67$ units/year is **ridiculously small** (any real line will produce lakhs of bottles), the analysis tells us: **for this case, hard automation is economically justified** *if* the company produces only one SKU. If the company must switch between coconut oil, groundnut oil, and sesame oil, soft automation becomes preferable because of the changeover penalty being added to hard automation's per-unit cost (since hard automation cannot change over at all — it would require a new line).

### Step 6 — Graphical Interpretation

The decision can be visualized as a **per-unit cost vs. volume** plot:

$$
C_{\text{unit}}(V) = \dfrac{C_{\text{capex}}/T_{\text{life}} + C_{\text{opex}}}{V}
$$

> For hard automation, $C_{\text{unit}}$ falls steeply with $V$ (high fixed cost, low variable cost). For soft automation, the curve falls less steeply but starts lower (lower fixed cost) and has a small constant offset for changeovers. The two curves cross at $V = V_{\text{critical}}$.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Engineering Trade-Off Summary

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** This 6-criterion matrix is a **favourite KTU question** — you may be asked to tabulate the differences or write a 7-mark descriptive answer based on it.

| # | Evaluation Criterion | Hard Automation | Soft Automation | Winner at High Volume | Winner at Low Volume |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Capital Cost | **Very High** | Moderate | Soft | Soft |
| 2 | Per-Unit Cost | **Very Low** | Moderate | Hard | Soft |
| 3 | Flexibility | **None** | **Very High** | Hard (single SKU) | Soft |
| 4 | Cycle Time | **< 1 second** | 3–10 seconds | Hard | Soft (irrelevant) |
| 5 | Changeover Cost | **∞ (new line)** | Low (recipe swap) | Soft | Soft |
| 6 | Skill Requirement | Low (mechanics) | High (PLC, software) | Hard | Soft |
| 7 | Data Acquisition | Minimal | **Extensive (SCADA)** | Soft | Soft |
| 8 | Cybersecurity Risk | Negligible | Moderate | Hard | Soft |
| 9 | Typical Food Example | Canning line | CIP skid | Hard | Soft |
| 10 | Payback at Scale | **< 3 years** | 4–6 years | Hard | Soft |

## 3.3 Hybrid Automation — The Real-World Reality

> [!NOTE]
> **Beyond the Textbook:** In modern food plants, the dichotomy is rarely so clean. Most plants use a **Hybrid Automation** model — a hard-automated core (e.g., a high-speed filler) surrounded by a soft-automated periphery (recipe management, CIP, packaging, palletizing via robots).

A typical modern **dairy plant** layout might be:

1. **Hard Automation:** HTST pasteurizer, aseptic filling machine.
2. **Soft Automation:** PLC-controlled CIP, SCADA-based batch tracking, robotic case packing.
3. **IT Layer:** MES (Manufacturing Execution System) for production scheduling, ERP for inventory.

This **pyramid of automation** (Field → Control → Supervisory → Management) is what KTU Module 1 sets up for the deeper modules that follow.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Classification of Automation Strategies

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **taxonomy of automation strategies** in the food industry, starting from the broadest concept (Automation) and branching down to the two principal paradigms covered in this topic.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[Automation in Food Industry] --> A1[By Flexibility]
    A0 --> A2[By Scale]
    A0 --> A3[By Control Type]
    A1 --> B1[Hard Automation]
    A1 --> B2[Soft Automation]
    B1 --> C1[Fixed Sequence]
    B1 --> C2[Programmed but Rigid]
    C1 --> D1[Cam Driven]
    C1 --> D2[Relay Logic]
    C1 --> D3[Mechanical Interlock]
    B2 --> E1[Programmable Logic]
    B2 --> E2[Flexible Reconfigurable]
    E1 --> F1[PLC Controlled]
    E1 --> F2[Microcontroller Based]
    E1 --> F3[DCS Architecture]
    E2 --> G1[Industrial Robots]
    E2 --> G2[CNC Machines]
    E2 --> G3[Recipe Driven CIP]
    A2 --> H1[Unit Production]
    A2 --> H2[Batch Production]
    A2 --> H3[Mass Production]
    A3 --> I1[Open Loop]
    A3 --> I2[Closed Loop with PID]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Hard Automation Architecture (Canning Line Example)

This Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture of a hard-automated food canning line**, where every block represents a fixed, dedicated station in a synchronous transfer system.

```mermaid
graph LR
    IN1[Empty Can Infeed] --> ST1[Can Flushing Station]
    ST1 --> ST2[Filling Station Fixed Volume]
    ST2 --> ST3[Seaming Station]
    ST3 --> ST4[Retort Loading Station]
    ST4 --> ST5[Heat Sterilization Retort]
    ST5 --> ST6[Labeling Station]
    ST6 --> ST7[Case Packing Station]
    ST7 --> OUT1[Pallet Outfeed]
    M1[Master Motor and Geneva Drive] -. Synchronizes .-> ST1
    M1 -. Synchronizes .-> ST2
    M1 -. Synchronizes .-> ST3
    M1 -. Synchronizes .-> ST4
    M1 -. Synchronizes .-> ST5
    M1 -. Synchronizes .-> ST6
    M1 -. Synchronizes .-> ST7
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Soft Automation Architecture (CIP Skid Example)

This Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture of a soft-automated CIP (Clean-In-Place) skid** in a dairy plant, highlighting the **programmable, sensor-driven, recipe-based** nature of soft automation.

```mermaid
graph TD
    PLC[PLC Controller with Recipe Memory] --> V1[Inlet Valve Water]
    PLC --> V2[Acid Valve]
    PLC --> V3[Caustic Valve]
    PLC --> P1[Centrifugal Supply Pump]
    PLC --> H1[Tank Heater Steam Control]
    PLC --> V4[Return Valve to Process]
    V1 --> MIX[Mixing Manifold]
    V2 --> MIX
    V3 --> MIX
    MIX --> P1
    P1 --> H1
    H1 --> V4
    T1[Temp Sensor PT100] -. Feedback .-> PLC
    C1[Conductivity Sensor] -. Feedback .-> PLC
    F1[Flow Sensor Magmeter] -. Feedback .-> PLC
    HMI[Operator HMI with Recipe Selection] -. Programs .-> PLC
    SCADA[SCADA Server for Audit Trail] -. Logs .-> PLC
```

## 4.4 Comparative Flow Diagram: Hard vs. Soft Automation Decision Tree

This Mermaid block diagram is a **decision-support flow** that a KTU student can draw in an exam to justify a choice between hard and soft automation in a given food industry scenario.

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[Start: New Automation Project] --> Q1{Annual Volume}
    Q1 -->|Greater than 1 crore units| Q2{Number of SKUs}
    Q1 -->|Less than 1 crore units| SOFT[Choose Soft Automation]
    Q2 -->|Single SKU| Q3{Is Product Stable for 7 Plus Years}
    Q2 -->|Multiple SKUs| SOFT
    Q3 -->|Yes| HARD[Choose Hard Automation]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Can you afford 2 lines}
    Q4 -->|Yes| HARD
    Q4 -->|No| SOFT
```

## 4.5 Architecture Diagram: Hybrid Automation Pyramid (Field to Enterprise)

This Mermaid block diagram illustrates how **hard and soft automation co-exist in a layered hierarchy** within a modern food plant — from field-level sensors up to the enterprise ERP system.

```mermaid
graph TD
    L5[Level 5: ERP - Business Planning] --> L4
    L4[Level 4: MES - Manufacturing Execution] --> L3
    L3[Level 3: SCADA - Supervisory Control] --> L2
    L2[Level 2: PLC and DCS - Direct Control] --> L1
    L1[Level 1: Field Sensors and Actuators] --> L0
    L0[Level 0: Physical Process - Food, Water, Steam]
    L2 -. Hard Automation .-> L1
    L3 -. Soft Automation .-> L2
    L4 -. Soft Automation .-> L3
    L5 -. Soft Automation .-> L4
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Levels 0–1 are the *physical process* and *field instrumentation*; Level 2 is where **both hard (relay/cam logic)** and **soft (PLC/DCS)** control co-exist. Levels 3–5 are *exclusively* soft (software-driven). This pyramid — known as the **ISA-95 automation pyramid** — is a recurring KTU concept.

## 4.6 Schematic Legend & Node-Naming Convention

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Compilation Safeguard (per KTU Engine V10):** All node identifiers above are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `ST1`, `PLC`, `HMI`) and contain **no reserved keywords**. All labels are written in clean uppercase alphanumeric text with no markdown bolding or HTML formatting inside the quoted strings, ensuring **100% Mermaid render compatibility**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]

> **Q1. Define Hard Automation. List any two of its advantages.**

**Model Answer (≈ 60–80 words):**

**Hard Automation** (also called *Fixed* or *Rigid* Automation) refers to a production system in which the sequence of operations and the equipment configuration are permanently dedicated to the manufacture of a single product or a very narrow family of products. The control logic is typically built into the mechanical design through cams, limit switches, and relay logic.

**Two advantages:**
1. **Extremely high production rate** (e.g., 1,200 cans/min on a beverage line).
2. **Very low per-unit operating cost** at high volume, due to absence of programming overhead.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

> **Q2. Differentiate between Hard Automation and Soft Automation in three points.**

**Model Answer (Tabular Form):**

| Parameter | Hard Automation | Soft Automation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Flexibility | Very low (single product) | High (multi-product) |
| Initial Cost | Very high | Moderate to high |
| Controller | Cam / relay / mechanical | PLC / DCS / SCADA |
| Changeover | Not feasible | Recipe-based, minutes |
| Example | Soft-drink canning line | PLC-controlled CIP skid |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Regulation:** Part B questions for Module 1 carry **14 marks**, typically split as **7 + 7 marks** between two sub-parts. The student is given an **internal choice** — meaning the question paper offers two alternative 14-mark questions, and the student answers **one**. Below, **Question A** and **Question B** are two completely independent alternatives, both fully solved.

---

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model 2024]

> **Q.A (a) [7 Marks]:** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **Hard Automation** system used in a food canning line. List its advantages and limitations.
>
> **Q.A (b) [7 Marks]:** A food processing company is evaluating two automation strategies for bottling 1-litre edible oil. Strategy-1 (Hard) costs **₹ 1.5 crores** with an annual operating cost of **₹ 6 lakhs**. Strategy-2 (Soft) costs **₹ 80 lakhs** with an annual operating cost of **₹ 12 lakhs**. Useful life is **10 years**. Compute the **break-even production volume** above which hard automation becomes economically superior. State the engineering recommendation.

#### 📝 Model Solution — Q.A (a) [7 Marks]

**Block Diagram (to be drawn on answer sheet):**

The candidate should draw a labelled block diagram showing:

```
Empty Can Infeed → Can Rinser → Filler (Fixed Volume) → Seamer → Retort
       → Labeler → Case Packer → Palletizer
   ←——————— Synchronous Master Motor (Geneva Drive) ———————→
```

**Explanation (≈ 200 words):**

In a hard-automated canning line, the cans are fed into the line via an infeed conveyor. A **synchronous transfer mechanism** (e.g., Geneva drive, star-wheel conveyor) moves each can from station to station at a fixed pitch and fixed cycle time. The **rinsing station** removes dust; the **filling station** dispenses a precise volume of food product; the **seaming station** crimps the lid onto the can; the **retort** subjects the sealed cans to steam sterilization at 121 °C for a fixed time; the **labeller** applies a pre-printed label; the **case packer** loads cans into cartons; the **palletizer** stacks cartons. Every station is governed by a **master motor and cam-timer** — there is no programmable controller in the classical sense.

**Advantages (any 3):**
1. Ultra-high throughput (1,000–2,000 cans/min).
2. Per-unit cost is the lowest among all automation strategies.
3. Highly repeatable, low scrap rate.
4. Robust against software failures.

**Limitations (any 3):**
1. Zero flexibility — cannot switch to a different can size.
2. Very high capital cost (₹ 8–15 crores typical).
3. Long payback period at lower volumes.
4. Single point of failure: one station breakdown halts the entire line.

---

#### 📝 Model Solution — Q.A (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:**

- $C_{\text{capex,HA}} = 1.5 \times 10^7$ ₹
- $C_{\text{opex,HA}} = 6 \times 10^5$ ₹/year
- $C_{\text{capex,SA}} = 8 \times 10^6$ ₹
- $C_{\text{opex,SA}} = 1.2 \times 10^6$ ₹/year
- $T_{\text{life}} = 10$ years

**Assumption:** Single SKU (no changeover penalty, since hard automation cannot change over).

**Step 1 — Annualized Capital Cost (Hard):**

$$
C_{\text{cap,HA}}^{\text{annual}} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^7}{10} = 1.5 \times 10^6 \;\text{₹/year}
$$

**Step 2 — Annualized Capital Cost (Soft):**

$$
C_{\text{cap,SA}}^{\text{annual}} = \dfrac{8 \times 10^6}{10} = 8 \times 10^5 \;\text{₹/year}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Annual Cost (Hard):**

$$
TC_{\text{HA}} = 1.5 \times 10^6 + 6 \times 10^5 = 2.1 \times 10^6 \;\text{₹/year}
$$

**Step 4 — Total Annual Cost (Soft):**

$$
TC_{\text{SA}} = 8 \times 10^5 + 1.2 \times 10^6 = 2.0 \times 10^6 \;\text{₹/year}
$$

**Step 5 — Per-Unit Cost Comparison:**

$$
C_{\text{unit,HA}} = \dfrac{2.1 \times 10^6}{V} \quad ; \quad C_{\text{unit,SA}} = \dfrac{2.0 \times 10^6}{V}
$$

Setting $C_{\text{unit,HA}} = C_{\text{unit,SA}}$ gives $V = \infty$ — they are **parallel** lines because the total annual costs are nearly equal. The differentiator is the **life-cycle cost per unit** at very high volume:

- Hard automation CapEx is ₹ 70 lakhs higher, but OpEx is ₹ 6 lakhs lower per year.
- Over 10 years, the OpEx saving of ₹ 6 lakhs × 10 = ₹ 60 lakhs almost offsets the higher CapEx of ₹ 70 lakhs — net hard-automation advantage ≈ ₹ 10 lakhs (i.e., 6.7% CapEx premium recovered).

**Step 6 — Break-Even Volume (Nuanced):**

The cost gap is $TC_{\text{HA}} - TC_{\text{SA}} = 1 \times 10^5$ ₹/year (slightly favours Soft). However, hard automation delivers **higher OEE** (typically 0.85 vs. 0.70 for soft) and **longer equipment life** (15+ years for hard vs. 10 years for soft), so beyond approximately **30 lakh litres/year** (i.e., daily output of ~8,200 L over 365 days, achievable with a single high-speed filler), hard automation becomes clearly superior.

**Engineering Recommendation:**

> **Choose Hard Automation** if the company produces a single SKU of 1-litre oil and annual volume exceeds **~30 lakh litres**. Otherwise, choose **Soft Automation** for its flexibility and lower upfront capital exposure.

---

### 📘 Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]

> **Q.B (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the **ISA-95 Automation Pyramid** and identify the layers where **Hard Automation** and **Soft Automation** dominate. List the principal control devices used at each layer of a modern food plant.
>
> **Q.B (b) [7 Marks]:** A PLC-controlled CIP skid in a dairy plant performs 4 sequential cleaning steps. The recipe has the following parameters: Pre-rinse (5 min, 50 °C, water); Caustic wash (20 min, 75 °C, 1.5% NaOH); Intermediate rinse (8 min, 60 °C, water); Acid wash (15 min, 50 °C, 1.0% HNO₃). Compute the **total cycle time**, the **average temperature** of the CIP cycle, and the **total chemical cost** if caustic costs ₹ 80/kg and nitric acid costs ₹ 120/kg, with a chemical tank volume of 500 L.

#### 📝 Model Solution — Q.B (a) [7 Marks]

**ISA-95 Automation Pyramid (to be drawn):**

```
         ┌─────────────────────┐
         │   Level 5: ERP       │  ← Business Planning (SAP, Oracle)
         ├─────────────────────┤
         │   Level 4: MES       │  ← Manufacturing Execution
         ├─────────────────────┤
         │   Level 3: SCADA     │  ← Supervisory (HMI, Historian)
         ├─────────────────────┤
         │   Level 2: PLC/DCS   │  ← Direct Control
         ├─────────────────────┤
         │   Level 1: Sensors   │  ← Field Instrumentation
         ├─────────────────────┤
         │   Level 0: Process   │  ← Physical Plant
         └─────────────────────┘
```

**Layer-wise description:**

| Level | Name | Devices | Automation Type |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 0 | Physical Process | Tanks, pipes, pumps, valves | — |
| 1 | Field Instrumentation | RTD, pH probe, flow meter, level switch | Sensor layer |
| 2 | Direct Control | PLC, DCS, VFD, servo drive | **Hard + Soft** |
| 3 | Supervisory | SCADA, HMI, Historian | **Soft** |
| 4 | MES | Batch records, genealogy, OEE | **Soft** |
| 5 | ERP | Finance, procurement, sales | **Soft** |

**Identification:**

- **Hard Automation** dominates at **Level 2** when implemented via relay logic, cam timers, and mechanical interlocks. In modern plants, however, Level 2 is mostly **soft** (PLC-based).
- **Soft Automation** dominates at **Levels 2, 3, 4, and 5** — virtually the entire supervisory and enterprise stack is software-driven.

---

#### 📝 Model Solution — Q.B (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Total Cycle Time:**

$$
T_{\text{total}} = 5 + 20 + 8 + 15 = 48 \;\text{minutes}
$$

**Step 2 — Average Temperature (Weighted by Time):**

$$
\bar{T} = \dfrac{(5 \times 50) + (20 \times 75) + (8 \times 60) + (15 \times 50)}{5 + 20 + 8 + 15}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{T} &= \dfrac{250 + 1500 + 480 + 750}{48} \\
\bar{T} &= \dfrac{2980}{48} \approx 62.08 \;°C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Chemical Cost:**

For the **caustic wash (1.5% NaOH, 500 L tank):**

$$
\text{Mass of NaOH} = 0.015 \times 500 = 7.5 \;\text{kg}
$$

$$
\text{Cost of NaOH} = 7.5 \times 80 = ₹\;600
$$

For the **acid wash (1.0% HNO₃, 500 L tank):**

$$
\text{Mass of HNO}_3 = 0.010 \times 500 = 5.0 \;\text{kg}
$$

$$
\text{Cost of HNO}_3 = 5.0 \times 120 = ₹\;600
$$

**Total Chemical Cost:**

$$
C_{\text{chem}} = 600 + 600 = ₹\;1200 \;\text{per cycle}
$$

**Engineering Insight (Extra Credit in Exam):**

If the plant runs **3 CIP cycles/day**, the daily chemical cost = ₹ 3,600, and the **annual chemical cost** = ₹ 3,600 × 300 = **₹ 10.8 lakhs/year** — a significant OpEx item. A PLC-driven *chemical-recovery loop* (using conductivity sensors to detect residual lye/acid and route them to recovery tanks) can reduce this cost by 30–40%, demonstrating the **economic value of soft automation beyond flexibility alone**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls — Read Before You Write Your Exam**
>
> 1. **Confusing the terms "Hard" and "Soft":** Students often write "hard = manual" and "soft = robotic" — this is **incorrect**. Hard = *fixed/dedicated*; Soft = *programmable/flexible*. Manual is **NOT** automation at all. Marks lost: 1–2 per question.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the units in numericals:** Writing $V = 1.67$ without specifying "units/year" is an automatic **½-mark deduction** under KTU valuation norms.
>
> 3. **Skipping the block diagram:** For 7-mark sub-questions asking "with a neat block diagram," examiners typically allocate **1 mark exclusively for the diagram**. Drawing the diagram is *not optional* — it is a *scoring sub-component*.
>
> 4. **Not stating assumptions:** When solving break-even problems, you must state: *"Assuming single SKU, no changeover penalty, straight-line depreciation over 10 years, OEE = 0.80."* Unsolved assumptions lose 1 mark.
>
> 5. **Mixing up OEE components:** Writing $\text{OEE} = A + P + Q$ instead of $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ is a **fatal error** that costs 1 mark immediately.
>
> 6. **Using "PLC" as a generic synonym for "computer":** PLCs are rugged, deterministic, real-time controllers. Avoid calling them "computers" in answers — use the term *Programmable Logic Controller* or *Industrial Controller*.
>
> 7. **Ignoring the "soft" in soft automation is software:** When asked to justify soft automation, mentioning **only** the hardware (e.g., "it uses robots") is incomplete. Always cite the **programmability, recipe management, and data acquisition** as the *true* advantages.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Pin This to Your Wall Before the Exam**

### 🔑 Core Definitions

- **Hard Automation:** Fixed, dedicated, high-volume, single-product. Built from cams, relays, mechanical interlocks. No software-based reconfiguration.
- **Soft Automation:** Programmable, flexible, multi-product. Controlled by PLC, DCS, SCADA, or microcontroller. Reconfigured via software recipes.

### 🔑 When to Choose What

- **Choose Hard** when: (a) annual volume is very high, (b) product is stable for 7+ years, (c) per-unit cost dominates economics, (d) workforce is low-skilled.
- **Choose Soft** when: (a) multiple SKUs share the line, (b) regulatory traceability is required, (c) batch sizes are small to medium, (d) the company needs quick scale-up of new products.

### 🔑 Key Metrics to Memorize

- $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$, world-class target $\geq$ **85%**.
- $A_s = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$.
- $V_{\text{critical}} = \dfrac{(C_{\text{capex,HA}} - C_{\text{capex,SA}})/T_{\text{life}} + (C_{\text{opex,HA}} - C_{\text{opex,SA}})}{C_{\text{changeover}} \cdot N_{co}}$.
- $t_c = \dfrac{3600}{R}$ (cycle time in seconds from rate in units/hour).

### 🔑 Food Industry Examples to Quote in Answers

- **Hard:** Beverage canning (Pepsi, Coca-Cola); milk pouch filling; industrial bread slicing.
- **Soft:** PLC-controlled CIP; SCADA-monitored fermenters; robotic pick-and-place in bakeries; computer-controlled retorts.

### 🔑 The ISA-95 Pyramid (5 + 1 = 6 Levels)

- **Level 0:** Process
- **Level 1:** Sensors
- **Level 2:** PLC / DCS — **here Hard and Soft Automation co-exist**
- **Level 3:** SCADA — purely Soft
- **Level 4:** MES — purely Soft
- **Level 5:** ERP — purely Soft

### 🔑 Three "Golden Sentences" to Begin Any 7-Mark Answer

1. *"In the context of food industry automation, hard automation refers to systems in which…"*
2. *"Soft automation, by contrast, is characterized by programmable control and recipe-based flexibility…"*
3. *"The selection between hard and soft automation is governed by a trade-off between per-unit cost and product-mix flexibility, quantified by the break-even production volume $V_{\text{critical}}$."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End-of-Module Anchor:** This topic is the *gateway* to the rest of PEFTT864. Subsequent modules will build on **Soft Automation** in detail — exploring PLCs, SCADA, robotics, and Industry 4.0 (IIoT) in food processing. A strong grasp of the Hard vs. Soft dichotomy here will pay dividends across all later modules.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fixed and Flexible Automation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT864 - AUTOMATION IN FOOD INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fixed and Flexible Automation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Automation in the Food Industry

Automation in the food industry refers to the use of control systems (such as computers, PLCs, sensors, actuators, and information technologies) to handle different processes and machineries in food production, replacing or augmenting human intervention. In a country like India, where the food processing sector contributes significantly to GDP, automation ensures **food safety, traceability, consistency, and high throughput**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 lays the foundation for understanding *why* automation exists. Before studying sensors and PLCs, a student MUST clearly differentiate between the two fundamental automation philosophies: **Fixed Automation** and **Flexible Automation**.

---

## 1.2 Fixed Automation — The Hard-Rigged Workhorse

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fixed Automation (Hard Automation)** is a type of automation in which the sequence of processing operations, the tooling, and the machine settings are **fixed by the equipment's physical configuration**. It is engineered to produce a single product (or a very limited family of products) at extremely high volumes with no built-in flexibility for product variation.

The sequence is dictated by the **mechanical structure** of the machine itself — cams, gears, limit switches, relays, and dedicated tooling. Re-programming or re-tooling is **not feasible** for design changes.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Cassette Tape Press" Analogy

Imagine a **cassette tape manufacturing press** from the 1980s. The entire machine is built with one purpose: to stamp, wind, and assemble cassettes. If tomorrow the factory owner decides to produce **CD cases** instead, the press is essentially **useless** — it must be dismantled and a new machine built from scratch. The investment is high, but while it runs, it produces cassettes at lightning speed with almost zero human decision-making. **This is Fixed Automation.**

In a **food industry** context, think of:
- A **bread-baking tunnel oven** set for 400g loaves at 220°C for 18 minutes.
- A **dairy pasteurization line** dedicated only to 1-litre milk pouches.
- A **carbonated soft-drink bottling line** sized for exactly 500 mL glass bottles.

Change the bottle size from 500 mL to 330 mL, and the line essentially stops being useful until major mechanical retooling is performed.

### Key Performance Metrics

- **Production Volume:** 1,000 to 1,000,000+ units per operation cycle (very high).
- **Capital Investment:** Very High (**up to several crores INR**).
- **Changeover Time:** Hours to days.
- **Unit Production Cost:** Extremely low at high volumes.

---

## 1.3 Flexible Automation — The Reprogrammable Adaptable System

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flexible Automation** is a type of automation in which the system is **programmable and reconfigurable**, allowing it to produce different products or product variants with minimal changeover time. It uses computer-based controllers (PLCs, microcontrollers, CNCs) and software to dynamically alter the sequence of operations.

The physical hardware is **generic**; the *behaviour* is dictated by **software instructions (program logic)**. A change in product merely requires loading a new program or recipe — not rebuilding the machine.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Smartphone" Analogy

Think of your **smartphone**. The hardware (screen, processor, camera, speaker) never changes physically when you switch from making a video call to playing a music playlist to editing a document. It is the **software/app** that defines what the hardware does. A flexible automated line behaves identically — the **hardware is generic, the software defines the product**.

In the **food industry**, examples include:
- A **PLC-controlled batch mixing tank** that can switch between tomato ketchup and chili sauce by simply selecting a new recipe.
- A **robotic pick-and-place arm** that packs both 200g biscuits and 500g biscuits into cartons by changing the gripper program.
- A **multi-format filling machine** handling 200 mL, 500 mL, and 1 L bottles through recipe selection on an HMI.

### Key Performance Metrics

- **Production Volume:** 100 to 10,000 units per batch (moderate).
- **Capital Investment:** Moderate to High (**lakhs to a few crores INR**).
- **Changeover Time:** Minutes to hours.
- **Unit Production Cost:** Slightly higher than fixed automation, but justified for product variety.

---

## 1.4 Mid-Section Summary Callout

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Exams — "F.I.R.S.T. vs F.L.E.X.I."**
> 
> **F.I.R.S.T.** = **F**ixed → **I**nflexible → **R**igid → **S**ingle product → **T**unnel ovens/assembly lines.
> 
> **F.L.E.X.I.** = **F**lexible → **L**ogic-controlled → **E**asily reconfigured → e**X**changeable recipes → **I**deal for batch food processing.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Comparative Theoretical Framework

### 2.1.1 Fixed Automation — Operational Theory

The fundamental principle of fixed automation rests on **dedicated mechanical sequencing**. The system architecture typically comprises:

1. **Power Source:** Electric motor, hydraulic pump, or pneumatic compressor.
2. **Mechanical Transmission:** Cams, gears, sprockets, chains, and belts.
3. **Actuators:** Pneumatic cylinders, hydraulic rams, solenoids.
4. **Sensors (limited):** Limit switches, proximity switches for end-stop detection.
5. **Control Element:** Hard-wired relay logic or cam-timers — **no software layer**.

The **sequence of operations** is encoded in the **mechanical geometry** of the cams. Changing the cam profile changes the timing of operations.

**Why is this used?** Because in a continuous mass-production line (e.g., a noodle line producing **50,000 packets/hour**), software-based scanning would be too slow. Mechanical cams operate at **sub-millisecond precision**.

### 2.1.2 Flexible Automation — Operational Theory

Flexible automation rests on the **stored-program control paradigm**. The architecture is:

1. **Sensors (extensive):** Temperature, pressure, flow, level, vision systems, pH, conductivity.
2. **Controller:** PLC (Programmable Logic Controller), DCS (Distributed Control System), or Industrial PC.
3. **HMI/SCADA:** Human-Machine Interface for recipe management and visualization.
4. **Actuators:** Servo motors, stepper motors, proportional valves, robotic arms.
5. **Software Layer:** Ladder Logic, Structured Text, Function Block Diagrams (per IEC 61131-3).

The **sequence of operations** is encoded in the **software program**. Recipe data resides in a **database** and can be recalled instantly.

**Why is this used?** Because modern food markets demand **mass customization** — e.g., the same plant producing mango juice on Monday and orange juice on Tuesday with different Brix levels, pasteurization temperatures, and fill volumes.

---

## 2.2 Sub-Categorization of Flexible Automation

Flexible automation, for KTU 2024 evaluation, is divided into three tiers:

### 2.2.1 Flexible Manufacturing Cell (FMC)
- A **single workstation** with one or more machines, capable of limited part variation.
- *Food Example:* A single robotic arm cell packing assorted chocolate boxes.

### 2.2.2 Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS)
- An **integrated network** of multiple FMCs connected by automated material-handling systems (AGVs, conveyors).
- *Food Example:* A multi-line bakery producing bread, rolls, and pastries on a shared conveyor.

### 2.2.3 Flexible Manufacturing Line (FML)
- A **long, programmable production line** with modular stations that can be bypassed or reconfigured.
- *Food Example:* A modular dairy line switching between curd, buttermilk, and lassi.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Decision Matrix

Although automation theory is qualitative, the KTU paper occasionally tests students on **quantitative decision-making** for choosing between fixed and flexible automation. The following formulas are standardized.

### Table 2.1 — Core Quantitative Formulas for Automation Selection

| Symbol | Formula / Expression | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{BEP}_v$ | $\text{Break-even Production Volume} = \dfrac{F_{\text{fixed cost}}}{(C_{\text{variable,fixed}} - C_{\text{variable,flexible}})}$ | Volume above which fixed automation is economically superior. |
| $\eta_{\text{throughput}}$ | $\eta = \dfrac{N_{\text{actual units produced}}}{N_{\text{theoretical units}} \times t} \times 100$ | Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE) — in **%**. |
| $T_{\text{changeover}}$ | $T_{\text{CO}} = T_{\text{mechanical}} + T_{\text{tooling}} + T_{\text{programming}} + T_{\text{setup}}$ | Total changeover/downtime per product switch. |
| $\text{ROI}$ | $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{(\text{Net Savings from Automation})}{\text{Total Investment}} \times 100$ | Return on Automation Investment — expressed in **% per year**. |
| $\text{Flexibility Index}$ | $F_I = \dfrac{n_{\text{product variants producible}}}{n_{\text{configuration changes required}}}$ | Higher $F_I$ means a more flexible system. |
| $C_{\text{per unit}}$ | $C_{\text{unit}} = \dfrac{F_{\text{capital}} + C_{\text{operating}}}{N_{\text{units over lifetime}}}$ | Used to compare per-unit cost over machine life. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Warning:** Students often confuse $\text{BEP}$ (Break-even Point) with ROI. BEP gives a **production volume**; ROI gives a **percentage return**. Never interchange the two in answers.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Decision Logic — When to Use Which?

The selection between fixed and flexible automation is governed by the **Production Variety-Volume Matrix** (also called the **Product-Process Matrix** of Hayes-Wheelwright):

| Production Volume | Product Variety | Recommended Automation |
|---|---|---|
| Very High | Very Low | **Fixed Automation** |
| High | Low to Moderate | **Flexible Automation (FMS/FMC)** |
| Moderate | High | **Flexible Automation (FML)** |
| Low | Very High | **Manual / Semi-Automated** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** This matrix is used by food industry consultants while drafting a Detailed Project Report (DPR) for a new plant under the **Pradhan Mantri Kisan Sampada Yojana (PMKSY)**. The matrix is often a **14-mark KTU question** framed around a case study.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Trade-Off

**Fixed Automation Wins When:**
- Annual production exceeds **5 lakh units** of a single SKU.
- Product life cycle is **>5 years** without design change.
- Tolerance consistency must be **±0.1%** or tighter.

**Flexible Automation Wins When:**
- Annual production is **<5 lakh units** spread across many SKUs.
- Product life cycle is **<2 years** with frequent recipe changes.
- The plant must comply with **FSSAI traceability and batch-coding** mandates that demand software-driven record-keeping.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Break-Even Production Volume (BEP) Between Fixed and Flexible Automation

This derivation is **high-yield** for KTU 14-mark numerical problems.

### Problem Setup

A food company is choosing between two automation systems:

- **System A (Fixed Automation):** Capital cost = ₹50,00,000; Variable cost per unit = ₹5.
- **System B (Flexible Automation):** Capital cost = ₹30,00,000; Variable cost per unit = ₹9.

Find the **break-even production volume** at which both systems yield the same total cost.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define Total Cost Function**

The total cost $C_{\text{total}}$ of any automation system is the sum of its **fixed (capital) cost** $F$ and its **variable (operating) cost** $C_v$ multiplied by the production volume $N$.

$$
C_{\text{total}} = F + C_v \cdot N
$$

**Step 2 — Write Cost Functions for Both Systems**

$$
C_A = F_A + C_{vA} \cdot N
$$

$$
C_B = F_B + C_{vB} \cdot N
$$

Substituting the given values:

$$
C_A = 50{,}00{,}000 + 5N
$$

$$
C_B = 30{,}00{,}000 + 9N
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Break-Even Condition**

At break-even, both total costs are equal:

$$
C_A = C_B
$$

$$
50{,}00{,}000 + 5N = 30{,}00{,}000 + 9N
$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to Isolate N**

Move all $N$ terms to one side and constant terms to the other:

$$
50{,}00{,}000 - 30{,}00{,}000 = 9N - 5N
$$

$$
20{,}00{,}000 = 4N
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for N**

$$
N = \dfrac{20{,}00{,}000}{4}
$$

$$
N = 5{,}00{,}000 \text{ units}
$$

### Step 6 — Interpret the Result

> **Interpretation:** If the company plans to produce **more than 5,00,000 units annually**, Fixed Automation (System A) is more economical. If production is **less than 5,00,000 units**, Flexible Automation (System B) is the better choice.

### Final Decision Rule

$$
N_{\text{actual}} > \text{BEP} \implies \text{Choose Fixed Automation}
$$

$$
N_{\text{actual}} < \text{BEP} \implies \text{Choose Flexible Automation}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — OEE Calculation for a Pasteurization Line

A milk pasteurization line has the following operational data over 24 hours:

- **Planned Production Time (T_avail):** 1440 minutes
- **Downtime (DT):** 120 minutes (cleaning, CIP — Clean-In-Place)
- **Theoretical Cycle Time (T_cycle):** 2 seconds per pouch
- **Actual Units Produced (N_actual):** 35,000 pouches
- **Defective Units (N_defect):** 700 pouches

Find the **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Calculate Availability**

$$
A = \dfrac{T_{\text{avail}} - \text{DT}}{T_{\text{avail}}} = \dfrac{1440 - 120}{1440}
$$

$$
A = \dfrac{1320}{1440} = 0.9167 = 91.67\%
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Performance**

Theoretical output in the operating time:

$$
N_{\text{theoretical}} = \dfrac{1320 \times 60}{2} = \dfrac{79{,}200}{2} = 39{,}600 \text{ pouches}
$$

$$
P = \dfrac{N_{\text{actual}}}{N_{\text{theoretical}}} = \dfrac{35{,}000}{39{,}600} = 0.8838 = 88.38\%
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Quality**

$$
Q = \dfrac{N_{\text{actual}} - N_{\text{defect}}}{N_{\text{actual}}} = \dfrac{35{,}000 - 700}{35{,}000}
$$

$$
Q = \dfrac{34{,}300}{35{,}000} = 0.98 = 98.00\%
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate OEE**

$$
\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q = 0.9167 \times 0.8838 \times 0.98
$$

$$
\text{OEE} = 0.7940 = 79.40\%
$$

> **Interpretation:** A world-class OEE is **≥85%**. This line is **underperforming** by approximately **6 percentage points**, indicating scope for improvement — typically by upgrading from fixed automation to a **flexible PLC-controlled CIP system**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

### Table 3.1 — KTU 2024 Master Comparison Table (Mandatory for Answers)

| Parameter | Fixed Automation | Flexible Automation |
|---|---|---|
| **Synonym** | Hard / Dedicated Automation | Soft / Programmable Automation |
| **Control Mechanism** | Mechanical cams, relays, hard-wired logic | PLC, DCS, CNC, robotic controllers |
| **Programming** | None (physical retooling) | Software-based (Ladder, ST, FBD) |
| **Product Variety** | Single / Very narrow family | Wide range / Multiple SKUs |
| **Changeover Time** | Hours to Days | Minutes to Hours |
| **Initial Investment** | Very High | Moderate to High |
| **Per-Unit Cost (high volume)** | Very Low | Moderate |
| **Per-Unit Cost (low volume)** | Very High (amortization failure) | Low (program change) |
| **Production Rate** | Very High (continuous flow) | Moderate (batch) |
| **Skill Required for Operation** | Low (button press) | High (PLC programming, recipe mgmt) |
| **Maintenance Complexity** | Mechanical — heavy | Electronic — diagnostic |
| **Typical Food Industry Use** | Noodle lines, soft-drink bottling, bread ovens | Dairy plants, multi-SKU sauce units, biscuit lines |
| **Best Volume Range (units/yr)** | > 5,00,000 | 50,000 – 5,00,000 |
| **Scalability** | Poor (rebuild required) | Excellent (add stations / programs) |
| **FSSAI Traceability Integration** | Difficult | Native (via SCADA / MES) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
