# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PEFTT864]**
**Define SCADA. List any four functional components of a SCADA system.**

**Model Answer:**

> **SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition)** is a computer-based industrial automation system that centrally monitors, acquires, and processes real-time data from distributed field devices (PLCs/RTUs) to enable supervisory-level control, alarm management, and historical data visualization of an industrial process.

**Four functional components:**
1. **MTU/SCADA Server** — Central computer that polls field devices, stores data, and hosts the SCADA software.
2. **HMI (Human-Machine Interface)** — Graphical mimic screens for operator interaction and visualization.
3. **RTU/PLC (Field Devices)** — Microprocessor-based local controllers that interface with sensors and actuators.
4. **Communication Network** — Data highway (Modbus/Profinet/Ethernet) connecting all levels.
5. *(Optional fifth)* — **Historian Database** for long-term time-series data storage.

*(Any four clearly explained = 3 marks; definition alone = 1 mark.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, PEFTT864]**
**Differentiate between SCADA and DCS in any six aspects.**

**Model Answer (compact table form acceptable):**

| Aspect | **SCADA** | **DCS** |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Plant-wide, multi-site supervision | Single integrated process unit |
| **Architecture** | Open, vendor-neutral | Proprietary, tightly integrated |
| **Process type** | Discrete / batch / packaged | Continuous (evaporation, fermentation) |
| **Communication** | Ethernet/Modbus/OPC UA standard | Proprietary fieldbus (FF, Profibus-PA) |
| **Cost** | Lower (commodity hardware) | Higher (licensed controllers) |
| **Redundancy** | Optional, usually at server level | Built-in at controller & I/O level |

*(Any six clear distinguishing points = 3 marks; five points = 2 marks.)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice, 14 Marks)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, PEFTT864, Module 2, Set A]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a SCADA system** used in a food processing plant. Label all levels clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **PT-100 temperature transmitter** in a milk HTST pasteurizer is calibrated for **−10 °C = 4 mA** and **+200 °C = 20 mA**. The SCADA HMI displays a current of **14.4 mA**. Calculate the corresponding pasteurization temperature. Comment on whether the milk is safely pasteurized (target ≥ 72 °C for 15 s). **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) SCADA Architecture (7 marks)**

The SCADA system follows a **five-level hierarchical model** based on ISA-95 / IEC 62264.

**Level 0 — Physical Process:** Milk receiving, pasteurizer plate heat exchanger, separator, homogenizer, packaging line.

**Level 1 — Field Devices (Sensors & Actuators):**
- *Sensors:* PT-100 RTDs, conductivity probes, flow meters, pH meters, level switches, turbidity sensors.
- *Actuators:* Pneumatic diversion valves, steam control valves with positioners, VFDs for pumps and conveyors, CIP chemical dosing pumps.

**Level 2 — Local Control (PLCs / DCS Controllers):**
- PLCs (e.g., Siemens S7-1500, Allen-Bradley ControlLogix) executing **PID control loops** — for example, maintaining pasteurizer outlet temperature at 72 ± 0.5 °C, controlling separator speed, regulating CIP return conductivity.
- Scan time: 5–50 ms.

**Level 3 — Supervisory Level (SCADA itself):**
- **MTU/SCADA Server** with software (e.g., WinCC, Ignition, iFIX) running:
  - HMI mimic screens
  - Alarm subsystem
  - Trend/historian
  - Recipe manager
  - Report generator
  - User authentication & audit trail
- Connected to PLCs via **Profinet / EtherNet/IP / Modbus TCP**.

**Level 4 — Enterprise / MES / ERP Integration:**
- Bidirectional data flow to MES (production orders, OEE) and ERP (raw material consumption, finished goods) via **OPC UA** or **REST APIs**.

*(Full block diagram as in §4.1 carries 3 marks; detailed level-wise description carries 4 marks.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Write the standard 4–20 mA conversion equation.**

$$
E = \frac{(I - 4)}{(20 - 4)} \times (E_{max} - E_{min}) + E_{min}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the given values.**

$I = 14.4$ mA, $E_{min} = -10$ °C, $E_{max} = +200$ °C.

**Step 3 — Substitute into the equation.**

$$
E = \frac{(14.4 - 4)}{(20 - 4)} \times (200 - (-10)) + (-10)
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the numerator and denominator.**

$$
E = \frac{10.4}{16} \times 210 - 10
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the ratio.**

$$
\frac{10.4}{16} = 0.650
$$

**Step 6 — Multiply by the range.**

$$
0.650 \times 210 = 136.5
$$

**Step 7 — Add the lower-range value.**

$$
E = 136.5 - 10 = 126.5 \text{ °C}
$$

$$
\boxed{E = 126.5 \text{ °C}}
$$

**Step 8 — Food-Safety Interpretation.**

Since the required HTST pasteurization temperature is **≥ 72 °C for ≥ 15 seconds**, the measured value of **126.5 °C** is well above the legal limit. However, in a real dairy plant, this temperature is **far above HTST range** (HTST is 72 °C, UHT is 135–150 °C for 2–5 s). Therefore:

- If this is an **HTST line** → SCADA must **trigger a HIGH-HIGH alarm** and **divert the flow to the re-processing tank** to prevent scorching, Maillard browning, and whey-protein denaturation.
- If this is a **UHT line** → the value is within acceptable operating range and the milk is **safely sterilized**.

*(Correct formula: 2 marks; correct substitution: 1 mark; correct arithmetic: 1 mark; correct units: 1 mark; meaningful food-safety interpretation: 2 marks.)*

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, PEFTT864, Module 2, Set B]**

**(a)** Explain the **role of PLCs, RTUs, and HMIs** in a SCADA system. Compare any **three industrial communication protocols** commonly used in food-industry SCADA. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A juice concentrate plant's SCADA supervises **1 500 tags** sampled at **2 Hz**, with each sample consuming **8 bytes**. The plant operates **350 days/year, 24 hours/day**. Calculate the **annual raw historian storage requirement** in GB. If the SCADA uses a compression ratio of **70 %**, what is the actual disk size to be provisioned? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Roles and Protocols (7 marks)**

**Roles in a SCADA system:**

- **PLC (Programmable Logic Controller):** The workhorse of Level 2. A ruggedized industrial computer that executes ladder/structured-text logic in real-time to control valves, motors, and heaters. In a food plant, a PLC controls the bottle-filler sequence, CIP cycle, and pasteurizer PID. Scan time 5–50 ms.

- **RTU (Remote Terminal Unit):** Similar to a PLC but optimized for **geographically dispersed, telemetry-based** applications — typically over radio, GSM, satellite, or DNP3. In a food plant, an RTU monitors a **distant cold-storage warehouse** reporting back to the central SCADA via GSM every few minutes. Lower I/O count, lower cost, battery-tolerant.

- **HMI (Human-Machine Interface):** The **graphical window** of the SCADA. Provides mimic diagrams, trends, alarms, recipes, and operator controls. The HMI does not store data or control directly — it is a *client* of the SCADA server. Modern HMIs are web-based, accessible from any browser, even on a tablet.

**Three Industrial Protocols Compared:**

| Attribute | **Modbus TCP** | **Profinet** | **OPC UA** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Developed by | Modicon (1979) | Siemens / PI | OPC Foundation |
| Physical Layer | Standard Ethernet | Real-time Ethernet | Standard Ethernet / TLS |
| Speed | ~100 Mbps | 100 Mbps–1 Gbps | 100 Mbps–10 Gbps |
| Determinism | Best-effort | Hard real-time (< 1 ms) | Configurable |
| Security | None native | PROFINET Security | Built-in encryption + auth |
| Best Use in Food Plant | Cheap device integration | Siemens PLC-to-PLC | SCADA–MES–ERP integration |

*(Three protocols with five attributes each: 3 marks; roles of PLC/RTU/HMI: 4 marks.)*

---

**(b) Historian Sizing Numerical (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — List the parameters.**

$N_{tags} = 1\,500$, $f = 2$ Hz, $B = 8$ bytes/sample, $t = 350 \times 24 \times 3\,600$ s.

**Step 2 — Total operating seconds per year.**

$$
t = 350 \times 86\,400 = 30\,240\,000 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the storage formula.**

$$
S = N_{tags} \times f \times t \times B
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute.**

$$
S = 1\,500 \times 2 \times 30\,240\,000 \times 8
$$

**Step 5 — Compute stepwise.**

$$
1\,500 \times 2 = 3\,000 \text{ samples/s}
$$

$$
3\,000 \times 8 = 24\,000 \text{ bytes/s}
$$

$$
24\,000 \times 30\,240\,000 = 725\,760\,000\,000 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to GB (using $10^9$).**

$$
S_{raw} = \frac{725.76 \times 10^9}{10^9} = 725.76 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 7 — Apply 70 % compression (i.e., data reduced to 30 % of original).**

$$
S_{actual} = 725.76 \times 0.30 = 217.73 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 8 — Practical provisioning (add 30 % margin for OS, indexes, snapshots).**

$$
S_{provision} = 217.73 \times 1.30 \approx 283 \text{ GB}
$$

$$
\boxed{S_{raw} \approx 726 \text{ GB/year} \quad ; \quad S_{provision} \approx 300 \text{ GB/year}}
$$

*(Formula: 2 marks; substitution: 1 mark; arithmetic: 2 marks; compression: 1 mark; engineering provisioning comment: 1 mark.)*

---

> [!WARNING] KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers
> 1. **Confusing SCADA with PLC** — SCADA is *supervisory*, not the direct controller. Marks are deducted if the student describes ladder-logic execution as a SCADA function.
> 2. **Using 20 instead of 16 in 4–20 mA conversions** — the denominator must be (20 − 4) = 16, not 20. This single mistake causes 25 % error.
> 3. **Skipping the food-safety interpretation** in numerical questions — KTU awards marks for *applied* understanding, not just arithmetic.
> 4. **Writing "SCADA and DCS are the same"** — they are not. DCS is a tightly integrated control system; SCADA is a *supervisory overlay* that can sit over multiple vendors.
> 5. **Forgetting the role of the Historian** — many students omit the long-term data archive, but it is critical for traceability and root-cause analysis.
> 6. **Mentioning "SCADA is used in power plants"** only — the question is from *Automation in Food Industry*; always anchor examples in dairy, bakery, brewery, juice, retort, CIP, etc.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2

- **SCADA = Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition** — a *supervisory* system, not a direct controller. It overlays PLCs, RTUs, and DCS islands.
- **Five-level architecture:** Level 0 (Process) → Level 1 (Field) → Level 2 (Control PLCs/RTUs) → Level 3 (SCADA itself) → Level 4 (MES/ERP/Cloud).
- **Core components:** MTU/Server, HMI, Historian, Alarm subsystem, Communication network, Field devices, Recipe manager, Report generator.
- **SCADA vs DCS:** SCADA is open, multi-vendor, discrete/batch oriented; DCS is proprietary, single-vendor, continuous-process oriented. Food plants often use **hybrid** architectures.
- **Standard signal:** **4–20 mA** current loop (live-zero for fault detection). Conversion equation: $E = \frac{(I-4)}{16} \times (E_{max} - E_{min}) + E_{min}$.
- **Top protocols to memorize for KTU:** Modbus (RTU/TCP), Profinet, EtherNet/IP, OPC UA, DNP3. **OPC UA is the modern SCADA–MES/ERP integration protocol.**
- **Storage sizing formula:** $S = N_{tags} \times f \times t \times B$ — multiply tags, sampling rate, time, and bytes; convert to GB.
- **Availability formula:** $A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$; redundant pair: $A_{system} = 1 - (1-A_1)(1-A_2)$.
- **F₀ sterilization** (canned/retort food): $F_0 = \Delta t \cdot 10^{(T - T_{ref})/z}$ with $T_{ref} = 121.1$ °C, $z = 10$ °C, target $F_0 \ge 3$ min for *C. botulinum* safety. SCADA integrates F₀ over the entire batch.
- **Alarm priority levels:** Priority 1 (Critical, red, siren) → Priority 4 (Low, blue, no sound).
- **Redundancy:** Hot-standby SCADA servers with < 5 s failover are industry standard for food plants supplying multinational brands.
- **Traceability & compliance:** SCADA enables HACCP, FSSC 22000, FDA 21 CFR Part 11, EU 178/2002 compliance via electronic batch records.
- **OEE = Availability × Performance × Quality** — the single most important SCADA-derived KPI for any food plant manager.
- **Common food-industry applications:** HTST/UHT pasteurization control, CIP cycle automation, retort F₀ integration, fermentation pH/temperature profile, cold-chain warehouse RTU monitoring, batching & recipe management, packaging line OEE tracking, energy monitoring of chillers/boilers.
- **Common exam mistake to avoid:** Confusing **SCADA** (supervisory software) with **PLC** (field controller) — they are *complementary*, not interchangeable.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robotics in Food Processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT864 - AUTOMATION IN FOOD INDUSTRY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Robotics in Food Processing # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Robotics in Food Processing** is the integrated application of programmable, sensor-guided electromechanical machines (industrial robots) to perform repetitive, hazardous, or precision-critical operations across the food value chain — from raw material handling and sorting to primary processing, packaging, and palletizing — while complying with hygienic design principles, food safety regulations, and Good Manufacturing Practices (GMP). According to the **International Federation of Robotics (IFR)** and aligned with the **ISO 8373:2021** standard, an *industrial robot* is defined as an **automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose manipulator, programmable in three or more axes**, which may be either fixed in place or mobile for use in industrial automation applications. In the food industry context, the **Robotic Industries Association (RIA)** further specifies that food-grade robots must additionally conform to: - **3-A Sanitary Standards** - **EHEDG (European Hygienic Engineering & Design Group) guidelines** - **FDA 21 CFR** (Code of Federal Regulations) for food contact surfaces - **NSF/ANSI 169** for special-purpose food equipment ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The "Invisible Kitchen Helper" Analogy:** > Imagine a master chef in a commercial kitchen. This chef never tires, never gets distracted, never sneezes over the food, can lift a **25 kg** sack of flour one moment and gently place a **5 g** strawberry the next, and repeats the *exact same* cutting angle **10,000 times a day** without a single millimeter of drift. Now replace the chef with a **robotic arm** equipped with specialized grippers, vision cameras, and food-safe stainless-steel joints. That is the essence of food processing robotics — **superhuman precision, tireless repetition, and contamination-free operation.** ## 1.3 Core Classification Metrics | Parameter | Typical Industrial Range | Food-Industry Range | |---|---|---| | **Payload Capacity** | 3 kg – 2,000 kg | **0.5 kg – 500 kg** | | **Number of Axes (DoF)** | 4 – 6 | **3 – 6** | | **Positioning Repeatability** | ±0.02 mm – ±0.1 mm | **±0.05 mm – ±2 mm** | | **Cycle Time** | 0.3 s – 30 s | **0.5 s – 60 s** | | **Reach Envelope** | 0.5 m – 3.5 m | **0.3 m – 2.5 m** | | **Operating Temperature** | 0 °C – 45 °C | **–30 °C to +200 °C** (with variants) | ## 1.4 Hygiene & Safety Constants (Industry Standards) > [!IMPORTANT] > The hygiene design of food robots is governed by two cardinal constants: > 1. **Surface Roughness (Ra)** ≤ **0.8 µm** for food-contact zones (per EHEDG Doc. 2) > 2. **Ingress Protection Rating** ≥ **IP65** for washdown zones; **IP67/IP69K** for high-pressure cleaning zones. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomical Architecture of a Food-Processing Robot A food-processing robot is a **cyber-physical system (CPS)** that bridges the digital control layer with the physical manipulation layer. Its architecture can be decomposed into **four primary subsystems**: ### 2.1.1 Mechanical Subsystem (The Musculoskeletal Frame) - **Base / Pedestal** — Anchors the robot; often stainless-steel AISI **304** or **316L** for corrosion resistance against cleaning chemicals (e.g., NaOCl, H₂O₂, peracetic acid). - **Links & Joints** — Provide the kinematic chain. Joints are typically **revolute (R)** or **prismatic (P)**, classified by the **Grübler-Kutzbach criterion** for mobility. - **End-Effector (Gripper / Tool)** — The "hand" of the robot. In food applications, these are highly specialized: - *Vacuum grippers* for eggs, chocolates - *Soft pneumatic grippers* (silicone fingers) for fruits, bread - *Mechanical parallel grippers* for meat trays - *Cups / suction arrays* for biscuits - *Cutting blades / water-jet nozzles* for portioning ### 2.1.2 Actuation Subsystem (The Muscles) Converts electrical/pneumatic/hydraulic energy into mechanical motion. In food plants, **electric servo drives** dominate due to clean-room compatibility, unlike hydraulics that risk **HACCP**-critical oil leaks. ### 2.1.3 Sensory Subsystem (The Nervous System) - **Proprioceptive sensors** — Encoders (position), resolvers, torque sensors, inertial measurement units (IMU). - **Exteroceptive sensors** — 2D/3D machine vision (CMOS/CCD cameras), hyperspectral imaging, near-infrared (NIR) spectrometers, laser triangulation, ultrasonic, and X-ray for contaminant detection. ### 2.1.4 Control Subsystem (The Brain) A hierarchical architecture following the **IEC 61131-3 / IEC 61499** standard, executing the **sense → plan → act** loop, typically at frequencies of **125 Hz – 1 kHz** for industrial controllers. ## 2.2 Kinematic Foundation — Degrees of Freedom (DoF) The **Degrees of Freedom (DoF)** of a robotic manipulator is the number of independent parameters that uniquely define its configuration in space. $$DOF = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$ Where: - $n$ = number of links (excluding base) - $j_1$ = number of lower-pair joints (1 DoF, e.g., revolute) - $j_2$ = number of upper-pair joints (2 DoF, e.g., planar) For the most common food-industry robot — the **6-DoF articulated (anthropomorphic) arm** — this enables arbitrary position AND orientation in 3D space, which is essential for tasks like orienting a chicken breast before cutting or placing a bottle in a crate. ## 2.3 Reach & Payload — The Key Selection Trade-off The **payload-reach curve** is a fundamental engineering graph used by every OEM (Fanuc, ABB, KUKA, Stäubli, Yaskawa, Kawasaki). It follows a roughly **inverse-cubic decay**: $$P_{max}(r) \approx P_0 \cdot \left(\frac{r_0}{r}\right)^k$$ Where: - $P_{max}(r)$ = maximum allowable payload at reach $r$ - $P_0$ = rated payload at rated reach $r_0$ - $k$ = decay exponent (empirically **1.5 – 2.5** for articulated arms) > [!NOTE] > **Real-world utility:** A plant engineer selecting a robot for *palletizing 15 kg cartons at 1.8 m reach* versus *picking 0.1 kg chocolates at 0.5 m reach* must consult this curve; the same robot model cannot legally perform both tasks at full speed without structural overstress. ## 2.4 Repeatability vs. Accuracy — A Critical Distinction > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU examiners frequently test this distinction. Do NOT confuse them. - **Accuracy** = How close the robot gets to an *absolute commanded* position (depends on calibration, typically **±0.5 mm to ±5 mm**). - **Repeatability** = How close it returns to an *already-taught* position over many cycles (typically **±0.02 mm to ±0.1 mm**). $$R = \pm k \cdot \sigma_{pos}$$ Where $\sigma_{pos}$ is the standard deviation of position error, and $k$ is a confidence multiplier (often $k=3$ for **99.7% confidence**, per the 3-sigma rule). Food applications like **chocolate decoration** or **surgical-style fish filleting** demand high *repeatability*; *vision-guided bin-picking* demands high *accuracy*. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Grübler-Kutzbach DoF | $DOF = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | Dimensionless; for planar: $M=3(n-1)-2j_1-j_2$ | | 2 | Payload-Reach Decay | $P_{max}(r) \approx P_0 \left(\frac{r_0}{r}\right)^k$ | $k \approx 1.5$ to $2.5$ | | 3 | Repeatability (3σ) | $R = 3\sigma_{pos}$ | mm; 99.7% confidence | | 4 | Cycle Time Throughput | $T_c = t_{pick} + t_{place} + 2t_{move}$ | seconds; total cycle | | 5 | Vision System Resolution | $R_{px} = \frac{FOV}{N_{pixels}}$ | mm/pixel | | 6 | Conveyor Tracking Velocity | $v_{robot} = v_{belt} \cdot \cos\theta$ | m/s; $\theta$ = approach angle | | 7 | Food Safety Surface Ra | $R_a \leq 0.8\,\mu m$ | µm (EHEDG) | | 8 | Washdown IP Rating | $IP \geq 65$ (general) / $IP69K$ (high-pressure) | IEC 60529 | | 9 | Robot Working Envelope | $V = \int \int \int dV$ over reachable workspace | m³ | | 10 | Energy of Pneumatic Pick | $E = P \cdot A \cdot d$ | Joules; suction cup work | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** The food industry uses these formulas during **CAPEX justification** (capital expenditure analysis), **line balancing** (matching robot cycle time to conveyor belt speed), and **HACCP risk assessment** (validating hygienic design). ## 2.6 The Five Key Selection Criteria for Food Robots (KTU High-Yield) 1. **Hygienic Design Certification** (3-A, EHEDG, NSF) 2. **IP Protection Class** matching the washdown zone 3. **Payload + Reach + Repeatability** envelope 4. **Throughput / Cycle Time** matching line takt time 5. **Vision & Sensor Integration** capability (open architecture vs. proprietary) The takt time equation ties throughput to customer demand: $$T_{takt} = \frac{T_{available}}{D_{demand}}$$ Where $T_{available}$ is total available production time per day, and $D_{demand}$ is the daily demand in units. The robot cycle time $T_c$ must satisfy: $$T_c \leq T_{takt}$$ # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Degrees of Freedom for a 6-DoF Articulated Robot **Problem Setup:** A standard food-processing articulated robot has the following kinematic chain: - 1 base link (fixed) - 6 moving links ($n = 6$) - 6 revolute joints ($j_1 = 6$) - 0 prismatic or higher-pair joints ($j_2 = 0$) **Step-by-step Solution:** $$\text{Step 1: Identify link count.}$$ $$n = 6 \quad \text{(revolute joints J1, J2, J3, J4, J5, J6 connected by 6 links)}$$ $$\text{Step 2: Identify lower-pair joints.}$$ $$j_1 = 6 \quad \text{(each revolute joint contributes 1 DoF, restricting 5 DoF)}$$ $$\text{Step 3: Identify higher-pair joints.}$$ $$j_2 = 0 \quad \text{(no planar or spherical contact joints)}$$ $$\text{Step 4: Apply the Grübler-Kutzbach mobility formula for spatial mechanisms.}$$ $$DOF = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$ $$\text{Step 5: Substitute the numerical values.}$$ $$DOF = 3(6 - 1) - 2(6) - 0$$ $$\text{Step 6: Compute the arithmetic.}$$ $$DOF = 3 \times 5 - 12 = 15 - 12 = 3$$ **Wait — this gives DOF = 3, which is INCORRECT for a 6-DoF robot.** > [!NOTE] > **Correction Insight:** The Grübler-Kutzbach formula above applies to **closed-loop** mechanisms. For an **open-chain** spatial serial manipulator (which a robot arm is), the correct formula is simply: $$DOF_{open} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i$$ where $f_i$ is the degrees of freedom of joint $i$. Each revolute joint contributes 1 DoF, hence: $$DOF_{open} = 6 \times 1 = 6 \text{ DoF}$$ > **This 6-DoF enables the end-effector to reach any point $(x, y, z)$ AND assume any orientation $(\phi, \theta, \psi)$ in 3D space — the so-called "dexterous workspace" required for tasks like carving a whole turkey.** ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cycle Time & Throughput Calculation for a Robotic Pick-and-Place Line **Problem Setup:** A biscuit-packaging line uses a delta robot to pick biscuits from a conveyor and place them into blister trays. Given: - Pick time $t_{pick}$ = 0.30 s - Place time $t_{place}$ = 0.35 s - Horizontal move time $t_{move}$ = 0.20 s - Vertical return time (same) = 0.20 s - Number of biscuits per tray $N$ = 12 **Step-by-step Solution:** $$\text{Step 1: Total time per single pick-place cycle.}$$ $$T_c = t_{pick} + t_{place} + t_{move}^{out} + t_{move}^{return}$$ $$\text{Step 2: Substitute numerical values.}$$ $$T_c = 0.30 + 0.35 + 0.20 + 0.20$$ $$\text{Step 3: Compute.}$$ $$T_c = 1.05 \text{ s per biscuit}$$ $$\text{Step 4: Time to fill one complete tray.}$$ $$T_{tray} = N \cdot T_c = 12 \times 1.05$$ $$\text{Step 5: Compute.}$$ $$T_{tray} = 12.60 \text{ s per tray}$$ $$\text{Step 6: Throughput in trays per hour.}$$ $$\eta = \frac{3600 \text{ s/h}}{T_{tray}}$$ $$\eta = \frac{3600}{12.60} \approx 285.7 \text{ trays/hour}$$ $$\text{Step 7: Daily output (assuming 16-hour production shift).}$$ $$N_{day} = \eta \times 16 = 285.7 \times 16 \approx 4571 \text{ trays/day}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Vision System Resolution for Strawberry Sorting **Problem Setup:** A vision-guided robot must classify strawberries into Grade A (> 25 mm) and Grade B (≤ 25 mm). The camera has: - Field of View (FOV) = 200 mm - Sensor pixel count = 2048 pixels (horizontal) **Step-by-step Solution:** $$\text{Step 1: Apply the resolution formula.}$$ $$R_{px} = \frac{FOV}{N_{pixels}}$$ $$\text{Step 2: Substitute.}$$ $$R_{px} = \frac{200 \text{ mm}}{2048 \text{ pixels}}$$ $$\text{Step 3: Compute.}$$ $$R_{px} \approx 0.0977 \text{ mm/pixel}$$ $$\text{Step 4: Required pixel span for Grade-A threshold (25 mm strawberry).}$$ $$N_{threshold} = \frac{25 \text{ mm}}{0.0977 \text{ mm/pixel}} \approx 256 \text{ pixels}$$ $$\text{Step 5: Verdict.}$$ $$256 \text{ pixels} \gg 2 \text{ pixels} \implies \text{Resolution is MORE than sufficient for $\pm$0.5 mm sorting accuracy.}$$ ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Repeatability Confidence Interval (3-Sigma Rule) **Problem Setup:** A SCARA robot's position error during 1000 biscuit placements is measured with a standard deviation $\sigma_{pos}$ = 0.04 mm. Compute the **3σ repeatability** statement. **Step-by-step Solution:** $$\text{Step 1: Recall the 3-sigma rule.}$$ $$R_{3\sigma} = 3 \cdot \sigma_{pos}$$ $$\text{Step 2: Substitute.}$$ $$R_{3\sigma} = 3 \times 0.04 \text{ mm}$$ $$\text{Step 3: Compute.}$$ $$R_{3\sigma} = 0.12 \text{ mm}$$ $$\text{Step 4: Interpretation — 99.7\% of all placements lie within $\pm$0.12 mm of the taught position.}$$ $$\text{This is suitable for chocolate molding but borderline for precision sushi rice ball placement.}$$ ## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Robot Type vs. Food Application | Food Operation | Recommended Robot Type | DoF | End-Effector | Key Sensor | Hygiene Class | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Palletizing cereal boxes** | 6-DoF Articulated | 6 | Fork / clamp gripper | Proximity | IP65 | | **Sorting apples** | 4-DoF SCARA + Vision | 4 | Soft suction cup | 2D Vision | IP67 | | **Cutting chicken breast** | 6-DoF Articulated + Vision | 6 | Water-jet / blade | 3D Vision + Force | IP69K | | **Picking chocolates** | 3-DoF Delta | 3 | Vacuum array | Vision tracking | IP65 | | **Packing biscuits** | 3/4-DoF Delta | 3–4 | Soft pneumatic | Vision | IP65 | | **Slaughterhouse carcass handling** | 6-DoF Heavy-payload | 6 | Custom hook | Tactile | IP66 | | **Bread loaf slicing** | 6-DoF + force feedback | 6 | Serrated blade | Force/torque | IP69K | | **Wine bottle crate packing** | 6-DoF Articulated | 6 | Foam-lined gripper | Vision | IP65 | | **Pizza topping** | 6-DoF Collaborative | 6 | Sauce dispenser | Vision | IP67 | | **Dairy cup denesting** | 4-DoF SCARA | 4 | Vacuum mandrel | Vision | IP69K | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of a Food-Processing Robotic Cell ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CTRL["CONTROL LAYER"] HMI["HMI Touchscreen
Operator Interface"] PLC["Programmable Logic Controller
IEC 61131-3 Ladder Logic"] RC["Robot Controller
Trajectory Planner"] end subgraph SENS["SENSORY LAYER"] V1["2D Machine Vision Camera
CMOS 5 MP"] V2["3D Laser Triangulation
Point Cloud Generator"] V3["Near Infrared NIR
Brix and Moisture Sensor"] V4["Force Torque Sensor
6 Axis F/T Transducer"] V5["Inductive Proximity
Encoder at each Joint"] end subgraph ACT["ACTUATION LAYER"] S1["Servo Drive 1
Base Joint J1"] S2["Servo Drive 2
Shoulder J2"] S3["Servo Drive 3
Elbow J3"] S4["Servo Drive 4
Wrist J4"] S5["Servo Drive 5
Wrist J5"] S6["Servo Drive 6
Wrist J6"] end subgraph MECH["MECHANICAL LAYER"] ARM["6 DOF Articulated Arm
Stainless Steel AISI 316L"] EE["End Effector
Soft Vacuum Gripper"] PROD["Food Product on Conveyor"] end subgraph SAFE["SAFETY LAYER"] EST["Emergency Stop Circuit
Category 1 PL e"] SCN["Light Curtain Safety Scanner"] COL["Collaborative Force Limiter
ISO TS 15066"] end HMI --> PLC PLC --> RC RC --> S1 RC --> S2 RC --> S3 RC --> S4 RC --> S5 RC --> S6 S1 --> ARM S2 --> ARM S3 --> ARM S4 --> ARM S5 --> ARM S6 --> ARM ARM --> EE V1 --> RC V2 --> RC V3 --> PLC V4 --> RC V5 --> RC EE --> PROD EST --> PLC SCN --> PLC COL --> RC ``` ## 4.2 State Machine: Robotic Pick-and-Place Cycle ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Idle Idle --> VisionScan : Object Detected on Belt VisionScan --> Classification : Image Captured Classification --> PathPlanning : Grade Identified A or B Classification --> Reject : Defective or Out of Spec Reject --> Idle : Reject Actuator Fires PathPlanning --> Approach : Trajectory Computed Approach --> Grasp : End Effector at Object Grasp --> Lift : Vacuum Confirmed Lift --> Transport : Object Raised Transport --> Placement : Above Target Zone Placement --> Release : Vacuum Released Release --> Retract : End Effector Withdrawn Retract --> Idle : Cycle Complete VisionScan --> FaultState : Camera Error Grasp --> FaultState : Vacuum Loss FaultState --> [*] : Operator Reset ``` ## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Hygienic Design Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Food Application
Raw vs High Care vs Ambient"] --> B{"Washdown
Zone Type"} B -->|Dry Zone| C["IP54 Rated Robot
No Daily Washdown"] B -->|Wet Zone| D["IP65 Rated Robot
Daily Low Pressure Wash"] B -->|High Care Zone| E["IP67 Rated Robot
Daily Foam Cleaning"] B -->|Sterile Zone| F["IP69K Rated Robot
High Pressure Hot Wash 80C"] C --> G["Standard Paint
Carbon Steel OK"] D --> H["Stainless Steel Joint Covers
AISI 304"] E --> I["Full Stainless AISI 316L
Sealed Joints"] F --> J["Hygienic Design Certified
3A EHEDG NSF"] G --> K["Standard Lubricant
Food Grade Optional"] H --> L["Food Grade H1 Lubricant
USDA Approved"] I --> M["Food Grade H1 Lubricant
Hermetically Sealed Bearings"] J --> N["No External Lubrication
Self Lubricating Bushes"] ``` ## 4.4 Comparative Topology: Robot Types Used in Food Industry ```mermaid flowchart TB RT["Food Industry Robot Taxonomy"] RT --> SA["Serial Arm Type"] RT --> PA["Parallel Arm Type"] RT --> MO["Mobile Type"] RT --> CO["Collaborative Cobot Type"] SA --> ART["Articulated 6 DOF
Reach 0.5 to 2.5 m
Payload 1 to 500 kg"] SA --> SC["SCARA 4 DOF
Reach 0.3 to 1.0 m
Payload 1 to 20 kg"] SA --> CY["Cylindrical 4 DOF
Reach 0.5 to 1.5 m
Payload 5 to 50 kg"] PA --> DE["Delta 3 to 4 DOF
Reach 0.3 to 1.2 m
Payload 0.5 to 12 kg"] MO --> AGV["AGV Mobile Platform
Payload 50 to 1500 kg"] MO --> AMR["AMR Autonomous Mobile
SLAM Navigation"] CO --> COL["Cobot 6 to 7 DOF
Payload 3 to 35 kg
Force Limited ISO TS 15066"] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper Style]** ### Q1. Define an industrial robot as per ISO 8373:2021. Mention any two hygienic design standards applicable to food-industry robots. **Model Answer (3 marks):** An **industrial robot** (per **ISO 8373:2021**) is defined as: > *"An automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose manipulator, programmable in three or more axes, which may be either fixed in place or mobile for use in industrial automation applications."* Two hygienic design standards applicable to food robots: 1. **3-A Sanitary Standards** — Govern the design of food-processing equipment surfaces, joints, and cleanability. 2. **EHEDG Guidelines** (European Hygienic Engineering & Design Group) — Specify surface finish ($R_a \leq 0.8\,\mu m$), drainage angles ($\geq 3°$), and material selection. *(Acceptable alternatives: NSF/ANSI 169, FDA 21 CFR, USDA standards.)* --- ### Q2. Differentiate between robot **repeatability** and **accuracy**. Why is this distinction important in food-grade vision picking? **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Property | Repeatability | Accuracy | |---|---|---| | Definition | Ability to return to a **previously taught** position | Ability to reach an **absolute commanded** position | | Depends on | Mechanical precision, backlash, encoder resolution | Calibration, kinematic model, environmental errors | | Typical value | ±0.02 to ±0.1 mm | ±0.5 to ±5 mm | | Statistic | 3σ confidence | Absolute error | **Importance in food vision picking:** A vision-guided robot picking randomly oriented strawberries from a conveyor must first *see* and *calculate* the absolute fruit position (high **accuracy** required) and then *precisely place* the strawberry into a fixed tray cell over 10,000 cycles (high **repeatability** required). Confusing the two leads to either **mis-picks** (poor accuracy) or **drift over time** (poor repeatability). --- ## 5.2 PART B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks) > **Note to students:** KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) provides an internal choice within each module. You must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. Both carry 14 marks. --- ### ✅ OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]** **Q3(a).** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a robotic work cell** used in food packaging. Label all major subsystems and describe the role of the **end-effector** in detail. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer (7 marks):** A robotic work cell for food packaging integrates the following subsystems (block diagram — see SECTION 4.1): 1. **Mechanical Subsystem (2 marks):** Stainless-steel **AISI 316L** articulated arm with 6 DoF, providing reach up to 1.5 m and payload of 5–10 kg. The arm must be **IP65/IP67** rated for washdown compatibility. 2. **Sensory Subsystem (1 mark):** Machine vision (CMOS camera, 5 MP) for product location; photoelectric sensors for conveyor synchronization; force/torque sensor for delicate grasping of bakery items. 3. **Control Subsystem (1 mark):** PLC (Siemens S7-1500 / Allen-Bradley CompactLogix) coordinating with the robot controller; HMI panel for operator recipe selection. 4. **Actuation Subsystem (1 mark):** Six servo drives controlling the six joint motors; pneumatic line (6 bar, filtered and dried) for vacuum-gripper actuation. 5. **End-Effector (2 marks):** The most application-critical component. In biscuit packaging, a **multi-cup vacuum gripper** with 4–8 silicone suction cups is used. Selection criteria: - Food-grade silicone (FDA 21 CFR §177.2600 compliant) - Vacuum level: **–60 kPa to –80 kPa** typical - Quick-change coupling for washdown hygiene - Gentle contact force ≤ 0.5 N to avoid biscuit breakage --- **Q3(b).** A **delta robot** is used to pick chocolates from a moving conveyor (speed 0.8 m/s) and place them into a 6×8 thermoform tray. The robot cycle time is 0.45 s per pick. Each tray holds 48 chocolates. Calculate: (i) Time to fill one tray. (ii) Hourly throughput in trays. (iii) Whether the robot can keep pace if line speed increases to 1.2 m/s. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer (7 marks):** **(i) Time to fill one tray (2 marks):** $$T_{tray} = N \times T_c = 48 \times 0.45 = 21.6 \text{ s}$$ **(ii) Hourly throughput in trays (2 marks):** $$\eta = \frac{3600}{T_{tray}} = \frac{3600}{21.6} \approx 166.67 \text{ trays/hour}$$ $$\text{Daily output (16-h shift)} = 166.67 \times 16 \approx 2667 \text{ trays/day}$$ **(iii) Line speed increase analysis (3 marks):** The **conveyor-tracking condition** requires the robot's *pick window* to accommodate the product's traverse across the vision field: $$t_{window} = \frac{L_{FOV}}{v_{belt}}$$ With a typical FOV of $L_{FOV} = 200$ mm = 0.2 m: - At 0.8 m/s: $t_{window} = 0.2/0.8 = 0.25$ s (robot has $T_c = 0.45$ s, so it must track across multiple vision frames — feasible with vision prediction algorithm) - At 1.2 m/s: $t_{window} = 0.2/1.2 \approx 0.167$ s The window shrinks by 50%, but the robot's mechanical cycle time is unchanged (0.45 s). Therefore, **the robot cannot maintain pick reliability** — it will begin to **miss picks or double-pick** adjacent chocolates. **Verdict:** ⚠️ **No, the robot cannot keep pace** without upgrading to a faster model (e.g., a delta with $T_c \leq 0.30$ s) or installing a **dual-pick end-effector**. --- ### ✅ OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Style]** **Q4(a).** Explain the **types of robots** commonly used in food processing with suitable examples. Compare the **articulated robot** and the **delta robot** in terms of speed, payload, and hygiene rating. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer (7 marks):** **Types of robots in food processing (4 marks):** 1. **Articulated Robot (6 DoF)** — e.g., Fanuc M-10iA, ABB IRB 1200. Used for palletizing, case packing, meat cutting. 2. **SCARA Robot (4 DoF)** — e.g., Epson G6. Used for screw-cap tightening, top-loading. 3. **Delta Robot (3–4 DoF)** — e.g., Omron Hornet, ABB FlexPicker. Used for high-speed picking of chocolates, biscuits, and packaged candies. 4. **Collaborative Robot (Cobot, 6–7 DoF)** — e.g., Universal Robots UR10e, FANUC CR-15iA. Used alongside humans for mixed-task lines (sushi, salad assembly). 5. **Cartesian / Gantry Robot (3 DoF)** — e.g., for pick-and-place in snack bars. 6. **Mobile Robot (AGV/AMR)** — e.g., MiR250, for autonomous material transport in warehouses. **Comparison Table (3 marks):** | Parameter | Articulated (6 DoF) | Delta (3–4 DoF) | |---|---|---| | **Speed (pick rate)** | 30–60 picks/min | **120–300 picks/min** | | **Payload** | **5–500 kg** | 0.5–12 kg | | **Reach** | 0.5–3.5 m | 0.3–1.2 m | | **Workspace shape** | Spherical | Hemispherical dome | | **Hygiene rating** | IP65 – IP69K | IP65 – IP67 | | **Footprint** | Larger | Compact ceiling-mount | | **Best for** | Heavy, large, varied items | Light, small, high-speed items | > **Key insight:** The delta's parallel-arm architecture trades reach and payload for **outstanding acceleration** (up to **10 g**), making it the workhorse of **high-throughput confectionery and bakery lines**. --- **Q4(b).** Describe the **hygienic design considerations** for robots used in the **high-care zone** of a dairy plant. Mention at least **four specific design features** and their rationale. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer (7 marks):** The **high-care zone** of a dairy plant is an environment where products are exposed to the atmosphere after pasteurization, requiring **microbiological control at <10 CFU/m³** air quality. Robotic systems deployed here must satisfy: 1. **Material of Construction — Stainless Steel AISI 316L (2 marks):** Superior corrosion resistance against lactic acid, CIP (clean-in-place) chemicals (NaOH 2%, HNO₃ 1.5%), and chloride-rich washdown. Carbon steel would corrode within weeks. 2. **Surface Finish — $R_a \leq 0.8\,\mu m$ (1.5 marks):** Per EHEDG Doc. 2, ultra-smooth surfaces prevent bacterial adhesion and biofilm formation. Electropolishing is preferred over mechanical polishing. 3. **Sealed Design — IP69K Rating (1.5 marks):** High-pressure (80–100 bar), high-temperature (80 °C) washdown capability prevents ingress of water and cleaning agents into bearings, encoders, and cable conduits. All cable entry points use **sealed cable glands**. 4. **No Horizontal Surfaces — Self-Draining Geometry (1 mark):** Per EHEDG Doc. 13, all robot surfaces must slope at **≥ 3°** to prevent pooling of liquids, which would otherwise become microbial breeding zones. 5. **Food-Grade Lubricants — USDA H1 Category (0.5 mark):** All bearings lubricated with H1 food-grade grease (e.g., Klüber, Molykote) to prevent lubricant contamination in case of accidental contact. 6. **Tool-Free Cleaning / Quick-Release End-Effector (0.5 mark):** The gripper must be removable without tools, so it can be autoclaved or sent through a parts washer independently of the arm. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - ❌ Students often write *"robots should be waterproof"* — too vague. You MUST specify the **IP rating** (IP65, IP67, or IP69K) and tie it to the **zone classification** (low-care vs. high-care vs. sterile). > - ❌ Students confuse **3-A standards** with **ISO 8373** — the former governs **food equipment hygienic design**, the latter governs **robotics terminology**. > - ❌ Students forget the **$R_a$ value** (surface roughness) — this is a **favourite 2-mark question** for KTU. > - ❌ Do NOT omit the **USDA H1 lubricant** requirement — marks are routinely lost here. > - ❌ In numerical questions, always carry **units** (mm, s, kg, mm/s) throughout the calculation; forgetting units loses 0.5–1 mark. > - ❌ In 14-mark answers, a **labelled block diagram** is mandatory; text-only answers are capped at ~10/14 marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > 📋 **Rapid Revision Checklist (High-Density Bullets)** - [x] **Industrial robot (ISO 8373:2021)** = automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose manipulator, **3+ axes**. - [x] **Food robot** = industrial robot + hygienic design + food-grade materials + HACCP compliance. - [x] **Open-chain DoF** = sum of joint freedoms (NOT Grübler-Kutzbach, which is for closed chains). - [x] **Articulated 6-DoF arm** is the most versatile; **delta 3-DoF** is the fastest. - [x] **Repeatability** (3σ) ≠ **Accuracy**; do not interchange in exam. - [x] **Payload–reach trade-off** follows inverse-power decay ($k \approx 1.5$–$2.5$). - [x] **Hygiene surface finish** $R_a \leq 0.8\,\mu m$ (EHEDG). - [x] **Washdown IP ratings**: IP54 (dry) → IP65 (wet) → IP67 (foam) → **IP69K (sterile hot high-pressure)**. - [x] **AISI 316L stainless steel** is the gold standard for high-care food robots. - [x] **USDA H1 food-grade lubricant** mandatory in any food-contact or splash zone. - [x] **End-effector types**: vacuum, soft pneumatic, mechanical, water-jet, blade — selected by product fragility. - [x] **Vision-guided picking** requires: 2D/3D camera + image processing + conveyor encoder synchronization. - [x] **Cycle time** $T_c = t_{pick} + t_{place} + 2t_{move}$; must satisfy $T_c \leq T_{takt}$. - [x] **Cobots** (ISO/TS 15066) enable human-robot collaboration with force-limited joints. - [x] **HACCP** and **GMP** govern every robotic deployment in food; safety scanners, light curtains, and emergency stops are non-negotiable. - [x] **KTU-favourite topics** in this module: hygienic design, robot classification, DoF derivation, cycle-time calculations, vision-system resolution.

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

According to the **ISO 8373:2021** standard adopted by the KTU 2024 Scheme curriculum, an **Industrial Robot** is defined as:

> *"An automatically controlled, re-programmable, multipurpose manipulator, programmable in three or more axes, which can be either fixed in place or mobile for use in industrial automation applications."*

In the context of **Automation in the Food Industry**, food-grade robots are specialized industrial robots designed to operate within the hygienic, wet, cold, hot, and corrosive operating envelopes of food processing lines, while complying with **3-A Sanitary Standards**, **EHEDG (European Hygienic Engineering & Design Group)** guidelines, and **FDA Title 21 CFR** regulations for indirect food contact.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (must reproduce in exam):** A food-industry robot is a reprogrammable, servo-driven mechanical manipulator with **three or more degrees of freedom (DOF ≥ 3)**, equipped with food-safe end-effectors, capable of executing tasks such as picking, packing, palletizing, sorting, slicing, and decasing in a hygienic environment.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a food-industry robot as a **"versatile robotic chef's assistant"** that never gets tired, never gets its hands sticky, and never calls in sick. Imagine a human hand performing the same repetitive motion — placing a pastry on a tray, sorting apples by color, or boxing 200 chocolates per minute — for an entire 8-hour shift. The robot is the engineered, stainless-steel, IP67/IP69K-rated, washdown-tolerant version of that hand, working inside a cleanroom-like food zone.

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Intuition:** A robot in a food plant is like a **muscle-memory worker** — you "teach" it once by guiding its arm (teach-pendant programming) or by simulation software, and it repeats the exact motion **10,000+ times per day** with sub-millimeter precision.

## 1.3 Classification Criteria

Food-industry robots are classified along **four orthogonal axes**:

1. **Mechanical Configuration (Kinematic Structure)** → Articulated, SCARA, Delta, Cartesian, Cylindrical, Polar, Parallel.
2. **Degree of Cooperation with Humans** → Industrial (caged) vs. **Collaborative Robots (Cobots)**.
3. **Mobility** → Fixed-base vs. **Mobile Robots (AGVs / AMRs)**.
4. **Primary Application** → Pick-and-place, Palletizing, Packaging, Sorting, Cutting/Slicing, Decasing, Inspection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Performance Metrics** highlighted in **bold**:
> - **Payload (kg)** — mass the robot can lift at wrist
> - **Reach (mm)** — maximum radial distance from base
> - **Repeatability (± mm)** — positional deviation upon return
> - **Cycle Time (sec)** — time for one standard pick-and-place
> - **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** — number of independent joint motions
> - **Ingress Protection Rating (IP65, IP67, IP69K)** — washdown suitability

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Robots by Mechanical Configuration

### A. Articulated Robots (6-Axis / Jointed-Arm)
- **Structure:** Serial chain of **6 revolute joints** (base rotation, shoulder, elbow, wrist pitch, wrist roll, wrist yaw).
- **Reach:** 0.5 m to 3.5 m.
- **Payload:** 3 kg to 800 kg.
- **Food-Industry Applications:** Deboning poultry, palletizing beverage crates, decasing frozen blocks.
- **Advantages:** Largest dexterous workspace; mimics the human arm.
- **Limitations:** Larger footprint; complex kinematics; higher cost.

### B. SCARA Robots (Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm)
- **Structure:** Two parallel revolute joints for planar motion + one prismatic joint for vertical descent + one rotational joint.
- **DOF:** 4 (R-R-P-R configuration).
- **Cycle Time:** As low as **0.30 sec** for pick-and-place.
- **Food Applications:** High-speed secondary packaging, top-loading of containers, screw-driving on caps.
- **Advantages:** Excellent rigidity in Z-axis, very fast cycle times, high repeatability.
- **Limitations:** Limited vertical reach; cannot reach under obstacles.

### C. Delta Robots (Parallel Kinematic)
- **Structure:** Three (or four) parallel arms connected to a common moving platform via universal/ball joints.
- **DOF:** 3 translational (X, Y, Z) — sometimes augmented with a 4th rotational DOF on the tool.
- **Cycle Time:** **100–150 picks/min** for lightweight products.
- **Food Applications:** Primary packaging of biscuits, chocolates, bread loaves, ice creams; picking from conveyors.
- **Advantages:** Extreme speed, hygienic stainless design, low inertia.
- **Limitations:** Limited payload (typically < 8 kg for food); small workspace.

### D. Cartesian / Gantry / Linear Robots
- **Structure:** Three orthogonal prismatic joints (X, Y, Z) — forms an XYZ gantry.
- **Workspace:** Cuboid (rectangular parallelepiped).
- **Food Applications:** Pick-and-place of large bakery trays, water-jet cutting of fish, dispensing sauces, large-scale palletizing.
- **Advantages:** Simple control (linear axes), large workspace, high accuracy.
- **Limitations:** Bulky; requires strong ceiling/floor mounting.

### E. Cylindrical Robots
- **Structure:** One revolute base + one prismatic vertical + one prismatic radial joint.
- **Workspace:** Cylindrical shell.
- **Food Applications:** Older-generation palletizing; rotating conveyor handoffs.
- **Status:** Largely replaced by 6-axis articulated and gantry robots in modern food plants.

### F. Polar (Spherical) Robots
- **Structure:** Revolute base + prismatic radial + revolute polar joint.
- **Workspace:** Spherical sector.
- **Food Applications:** Rare; mostly legacy injection molding handling.

### G. Collaborative Robots (Cobots)
- **Structure:** 6 or 7 articulated joints with **force/torque sensing**, **rounded contours**, and **power/force limiting (PFL)**.
- **Payload:** 3 kg, 5 kg, 10 kg, 16 kg, 20 kg, 30 kg.
- **Standards:** **ISO/TS 15066:2016** — defines biomechanical limits for collaborative operation.
- **Food Applications:** Light assembly, machine tending, decorating cakes, sample handling in QC labs.
- **Modes:** Safety-rated monitored stop, hand-guiding, speed-and-separation monitoring, power-and-force limiting.

### H. Mobile Robots — AGVs and AMRs
- **AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle):** Follows fixed magnetic strips / QR codes / wires on the floor.
- **AMR (Autonomous Mobile Robot):** Uses **LiDAR + SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping)** for dynamic path planning.
- **Food Applications:** Internal logistics of raw materials, finished goods in warehouses, dairy cold-chain movement.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are directly examinable under Module 2 (Robotics in Food Industry).** Memorize the units and the constant **g = 9.81 m/s²**.

### Table 2.1 — Robot Selection & Performance Equations

| # | Formula | Parameter Description | Units |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------|
| 1 | $\theta_i = q_i$ | Joint variable for joint *i* (revolute angle or prismatic displacement) | rad or m |
| 2 | $T_{ij} = \begin{bmatrix} R_{ij} & p_{ij} \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Homogeneous transformation matrix (Denavit–Hartenberg convention) | $4 \times 4$ |
| 3 | $v_{tcp} = J(\theta)\,\dot{\theta}$ | End-effector (TCP) velocity via Jacobian matrix $J$ | m/s |
| 4 | $\tau = J^T(\theta)\,F$ | Static joint torque from end-effector wrench $F$ | N·m |
| 5 | $W_{payload} = m \cdot g$ | Gravitational weight that the wrist must support | N |
| 6 | $C_t = \dfrac{3600}{\text{cycle time}}$ | **Picks per hour (PPH)** for a pick-and-place cell | picks/h |
| 7 | $T_{throughput} = \dfrac{N_{units}}{C_t}$ | Hours required to process *N* units | h |
| 8 | $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | Overall Equipment Effectiveness (Availability × Performance × Quality) | 0 to 1 |
| 9 | $ROI = \dfrac{(B - C)}{C} \times 100\%$ | Return on Investment (% over project life) | % |
| 10 | $P_{actuator} = \dfrac{\tau_{peak} \cdot \omega_{max}}{\eta_{mech}}$ | Peak motor power at the joint | W |
| 11 | $\Delta l = \alpha\,L\,\Delta T$ | Thermal expansion of arm with temperature swing $\Delta T$ | m |
| 12 | $f_{max} = \dfrac{v_{tcp}}{2 \pi r_{tool}}$ | Max rotational frequency of a circular cutting tool | Hz |

**Note on Jacobian:** $J(\theta) \in \mathbb{R}^{6 \times n}$ for an *n*-DOF robot; singularity occurs when $\det(J) = 0$.

### Table 2.2 — Robot-Type Selection Matrix (Quick Reference)

| Application | Best Robot | DOF | Typical Reach | Cycle Time |
|-------------|-----------|-----|---------------|------------|
| Primary packing (biscuits, chocolates) | **Delta** | 3 + 1 | $\varnothing$ 1.1 m | 0.10–0.15 s |
| Secondary packing (top-loading) | **SCARA** | 4 | 0.3–1.0 m | 0.30–0.45 s |
| Heavy palletizing (>50 kg) | **Articulated 6-axis** | 6 | 2.5–3.2 m | 4–8 s |
| Light collaborative work | **Cobot** | 6 or 7 | 0.5–1.3 m | 0.5–2.0 s |
| Large-area XY cutting/dispensing | **Cartesian** | 3 | up to 50 m | 1–3 s |
| Warehouse logistics | **AMR / AGV** | 2 (driving) + 1 (lift) | infinite (path) | 1–2 m/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** Allow a **safety margin of 25 %** on payload. If you need to lift 8 kg of meat, select a robot rated for at least **10 kg** continuous wrist payload.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Payload-Induced Joint Torque on an Articulated Arm

Consider a 6-axis articulated robot with the wrist at horizontal reach **L** from the shoulder axis, holding a payload of mass **m** in a gravity-down configuration. We need to compute the torque required at the **shoulder joint**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the moment arm.**
The horizontal distance from the shoulder rotation axis to the center of mass of the payload is **L** (assuming the wrist is fully extended horizontally).

**Step 2 — Compute gravitational force on payload.**

$$F_g = m \cdot g$$

where $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

**Step 3 — Compute the torque at the shoulder joint.**

$$\tau_{shoulder} = F_g \cdot L = m \cdot g \cdot L$$

**Step 4 — Include the self-weight of the arm links.**
Let $m_{arm}$ be the equivalent mass of the rotating arm structure, with its center of mass at a distance **L/2** from the shoulder:

$$\tau_{arm} = m_{arm} \cdot g \cdot \frac{L}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Sum the torques to obtain the static shoulder torque.**

$$\tau_{total} = m \cdot g \cdot L + m_{arm} \cdot g \cdot \frac{L}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Apply the 25 % safety factor recommended by KTU Module 2.**

$$\tau_{design} = 1.25 \cdot \tau_{total}$$

**Step 7 — Solve numerical example.**
Let $m = 5 \text{ kg}$, $m_{arm} = 8 \text{ kg}$, $L = 0.8 \text{ m}$:

$$\tau_{total} = (5)(9.81)(0.8) + (8)(9.81)(0.4) = 39.24 + 31.39 = 70.63 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\tau_{design} = 1.25 \times 70.63 = 88.29 \text{ N·m}$$

> **Conclusion:** The shoulder servo motor must deliver at least **88.29 N·m** of continuous torque. A 6-axis articulated robot with **10 kg payload rating** typically offers 200–400 N·m shoulder torque — comfortably within margin.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Picks Per Hour (PPH) and Throughput Time

A delta robot is used to pack biscuits. The measured cycle time $t_c = 0.12 \text{ s/pick}$.

**Step 1 — Convert cycle time to picks per hour.**

$$PPH = \frac{3600}{t_c} = \frac{3600}{0.12} = 30{,}000 \text{ picks/h}$$

**Step 2 — Compute hours required for one shift (say, 200,000 biscuits).**

$$T = \frac{N}{PPH} = \frac{200{,}000}{30{,}000} = 6.67 \text{ h}$$

**Step 3 — Compute OEE contribution.**
Assume Availability $A = 0.95$, Performance $P = 0.90$, Quality $Q = 0.99$:

$$OEE = 0.95 \times 0.90 \times 0.99 = 0.846 = 84.6\%$$

> **Interpretation:** The plant loses ~15.4 % of theoretical capacity to downtime, slow cycles, and rejects. World-class food plants target **OEE ≥ 85 %**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — ROI Justification for Robotization

A bakery invests ₹50,00,000 in a SCARA robot cell. Annual savings from labor reduction and waste control = ₹15,00,000. Annual maintenance = ₹2,00,000.

**Step 1 — Net annual benefit.**

$$B = 15{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000 = 13{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}$$

**Step 2 — Payback period.**

$$\text{Payback} = \frac{C}{B} = \frac{50{,}00{,}000}{13{,}00{,}000} = 3.85 \text{ years}$$

**Step 3 — ROI over a 7-year life.**

$$ROI = \frac{(13{,}00{,}000)(7) - 50{,}00{,}000}{50{,}00{,}000} \times 100\% = \frac{41{,}00{,}000}{50{,}00{,}000} \times 100\% = 82\%$$

**Step 4 — Decision rule (KTU Module 2).**
If **Payback ≤ 3 years** → robot is justified for FMCG/Food. If payback > 5 years → re-evaluate scope.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Robot Selection Decision Logic

| Decision Question | If YES → Choose | If NO → Choose |
|------------------|------------------|----------------|
| Is the product very light (< 1 kg) and high-speed? | **Delta robot** | SCARA / Articulated |
| Is the workspace rectangular and very large? | **Cartesian / Gantry** | Articulated |
| Are humans working in the same cell? | **Cobot (ISO/TS 15066)** | Caged 6-axis |
| Is the payload > 50 kg? | **Heavy-payload articulated** | Delta / SCARA |
| Is the floor space restrictive? | **Ceiling-mounted delta** | Floor-mounted SCARA |
| Does the product require twisting in mid-air? | **6-axis articulated** | SCARA |

**Model Answer (3 Marks distribution):**

  1. **Articulated 6-axis** → Palletizing beverage crates

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Most students write only the *names* of robot types and lose **3–4 marks** out of 14. KTU expects you to **compare them on at least 4 parameters** (DOF, payload, reach, cycle time) and **link each type to a specific food-industry application**. A mere list without comparison = **0 marks** for the comparison sub-part.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU ESE Module 2 – Internal Choice A]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **classification of robots used in the food industry** based on mechanical configuration. Discuss any **five types** with their kinematic structure, DOF, advantages, and at least one specific food-industry application. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A delta robot is deployed on a chocolate primary-packaging line. The measured **cycle time is 0.15 s/pick**. Calculate:
- (i) Picks per hour (PPH)
- (ii) Time required to pack **250,000 chocolates**
- (iii) If the line experiences **8 % downtime** and a **2 % reject rate**, compute the **effective OEE** and the **effective good units per 8-hour shift**. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Answer to Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**(1 Mark – Introduction)** Robots in the food industry are classified by mechanical configuration into six main categories.

**(1 Mark – Articulated 6-Axis):** Serial chain of 6 revolute joints forming a kinematic structure identical to the human arm (shoulder, elbow, wrist with 3 axes). DOF = 6. Reach up to 3.5 m. Payload 3–800 kg. *Advantage:* largest dexterous workspace. *Food application:* palletizing 25 kg sacks of flour at 8–10 cycles/min.

**(1 Mark – SCARA):** Two horizontal revolute joints + one vertical prismatic joint + one rotational tool joint. DOF = 4. Rigid in vertical direction. *Advantage:* cycle times as low as 0.30 s. *Food application:* top-loading bottles into cartons at 60 picks/min.

**(1 Mark – Delta Parallel):** Three (or four) parallel arms connected to a common moving platform via ball joints. DOF = 3 translational + optional 4th rotational. *Advantage:* speeds up to 200 picks/min. *Food application:* primary packaging of biscuits, breads, ice creams.

**(1 Mark – Cartesian):** Three orthogonal prismatic joints (X, Y, Z). *Advantage:* rectangular workspace, easy linear control. *Food application:* water-jet cutting fish fillets on a 4 m × 2 m bed.

**(1 Mark – Cylindrical / Polar):** Combined revolute + prismatic joints; cylindrical workspace. *Food application:* legacy palletizing; mostly obsolete in modern plants.

**(1 Mark – Cobots):** 6 or 7 DOF with rounded contours, force sensing, ISO/TS 15066 compliant. *Advantage:* no safety cage required. *Food application:* cake decorating in artisan bakeries.

**(1 Mark – Synthesis Table):** Summarize DOF, payload, speed, food application for the five chosen types.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**(i) PPH Calculation — 2 Marks**

$$PPH = \frac{3600}{t_c} = \frac{3600}{0.15} = 24{,}000 \text{ picks/h}$$

**(ii) Total packing time — 2 Marks**

$$T = \frac{N}{PPH} = \frac{250{,}000}{24{,}000} = 10.42 \text{ hours}$$

**(iii) OEE & Effective Good Units — 3 Marks**

Given: Availability $A = 1 - 0.08 = 0.92$ (since 8 % downtime), Performance $P = 1.0$ (assume nominal), Quality $Q = 1 - 0.02 = 0.98$.

$$OEE = 0.92 \times 1.0 \times 0.98 = 0.9016 = 90.16\%$$

**Effective output in 8-hour shift:**

$$N_{good} = PPH \times T_{shift} \times OEE = 24{,}000 \times 8 \times 0.9016$$

$$N_{good} = 1{,}73{,}108 \text{ chocolates per shift}$$

> **Conclusion (1 Mark implied):** The delta robot delivers 173,108 defect-free chocolates per shift; the plant needs ~1.45 shifts to pack the order of 250,000 units.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU ESE Module 2 – Internal Choice B]`

**(a)** Compare **SCARA, Delta, and 6-Axis Articulated robots** for food packaging applications, using the parameters: DOF, payload, reach, repeatability, cycle time, hygienic design, and typical cost. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A dairy cooperative wants to automate the movement of **500 kg cheese blocks** from cold storage (4 °C) to the cutting hall (12 °C distance = 80 m). Compare the suitability of **(i) AGV** versus **(ii) AMR** for this task. Recommend one with justification, including safety, hygiene (IP rating), and ROI considerations. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Answer to Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Comparison Table (5 Marks — 1 row per criterion):**

| Parameter | SCARA | Delta | 6-Axis Articulated |
|-----------|-------|-------|--------------------|
| DOF | 4 (R-R-P-R) | 3 + 1 rotational | 6 (all revolute) |
| Payload | 1–20 kg | 0.5–8 kg | 3–800 kg |
| Reach | 0.3–1.0 m | $\varnothing$ up to 1.5 m | 0.5–3.5 m |
| Repeatability | ± 0.01 mm | ± 0.05 mm | ± 0.02 mm |
| Cycle Time | 0.30–0.45 s | 0.10–0.15 s | 1–8 s |
| Hygienic Design | Stainless, IP65 | Stainless, IP67/IP69K | Stainless, IP65 |
| Relative Cost | Medium | High | Medium–High |

**Food Packaging Suitability (2 Marks):**
- **Delta** → primary packaging (lightweight, very high speed).
- **SCARA** → secondary packaging (top-loading, screwing caps).
- **Articulated 6-axis** → tertiary packaging (case packing, palletizing, heavy sacks).

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**(i) AGV Suitability (3 Marks):**
- **Pros:** Deterministic path, predictable timing, suited for fixed cheese-block route, lower unit cost (~₹8–15 lakh).
- **Cons:** Requires magnetic strip embedded in the floor → **hygiene issue** (gaps trap condensate at cold-chain temperatures), cannot dynamically avoid human workers (cheese hall has many operators).
- **IP Rating:** Typically IP54 → **inadequate for cold-chain washdown**.

**(ii) AMR Suitability (3 Marks):**
- **Pros:** **No floor modification** required → hygienic seamless epoxy floor preserved; **LiDAR-based obstacle avoidance** suits mixed-traffic cold halls; SLAM allows flexible re-routing when cold-storage racks are re-organized.
- **Cons:** Higher unit cost (~₹18–30 lakh); needs Wi-Fi coverage throughout cold storage.
- **IP Rating:** IP65–IP67 versions available → **acceptable for cold-chain washdown**.

**(iii) Recommendation + Justification (1 Mark):**

**Recommendation: AMR**, because the **hygiene-preserving navigation** (no floor tape), **dynamic obstacle avoidance** in a worker-dense cheese hall, and **IP67 washdown rating** outweigh the 1.5–2× higher capital cost when amortized over a 10-year life. The **ROI** is achieved in **3.2 years** assuming 1 labour shift saved (₹4 lakh/year) plus 15 % damage reduction on cheese blocks.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Valuation Mistake:** Students often skip the **ROI number** in Part (b). KTU examiners specifically allocate **1 Mark** for the quantitative payback statement. Always end with a payback period in years.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist — must revise before every KTU exam.**

- **Definition:** Industrial robot = re-programmable, ≥ 3 DOF, automatic (ISO 8373:2021).
- **Six Major Kinematic Types:** Articulated, SCARA, Delta, Cartesian, Cylindrical, Polar.
- **Cobots** = ISO/TS 15066 compliant collaborative robots with **power-and-force limiting**.
- **Delta robots** = fastest, used for **lightweight primary packaging** (chocolates, biscuits).
- **SCARA** = best for **top-loading and screw-driving** on caps.
- **6-Axis Articulated** = best for **heavy palletizing and decasing**.
- **Cartesian Gantry** = best for **large rectangular workspaces** (fish cutting, dispensing).
- **AGV vs AMR:** AGV uses **fixed magnetic/QR paths**; AMR uses **LiDAR + SLAM**.
- **Hygiene Ratings:** **IP65** (splash-proof), **IP67** (immersion), **IP69K** (high-pressure hot washdown — dairy/meat).
- **Standards:** ISO 8373 (definition), ISO/TS 15066 (cobot safety), ISO 13849 (cell safety), 3-A / EHEDG (hygienic design), FDA 21 CFR (food-contact materials).
- **Key Equations to Memorize:**
  - $PPH = \dfrac{3600}{t_c}$
  - $\tau = m \cdot g \cdot L$ (shoulder torque)
  - $OEE = A \times P \times Q$
  - $ROI = \dfrac{(B-C)}{C} \times 100\%$
  - $\text{Payback} = \dfrac{C}{B}$ (years)
- **Selection Rule:** Add **25 % safety margin** to payload; match **DOF to task complexity** (simple pick = 3–4 DOF; complex palletizing = 6 DOF).
- **Cycle Time Targets:** Delta ≈ 0.1 s · SCARA ≈ 0.3 s · Articulated ≈ 1–8 s · Cobot ≈ 0.5–2 s.
- **Wrist Sensor Stack:** Force/torque sensor + vacuum sensor + vision camera = "smart food gripper."
- **Payback Benchmark for Food Robots:** **≤ 3 years** is excellent; **3–5 years** is acceptable; **> 5 years** requires re-justification.
- **Mobile Robots in Cold Chain:** Always specify **lubricant grade for -25 °C** and **battery warm-up cycle** before commissioning.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications of Robotics in Food Packaging and Handling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT864 - AUTOMATION IN FOOD INDUSTRY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Applications of Robotics in Food Packaging and Handling # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Robotics in Food Packaging and Handling** refers to the deployment of programmable, multi-axis electromechanical manipulators—integrated with sensors, vision systems, and specialized end-effectors—to execute repetitive, precision-critical operations such as picking, orienting, placing, wrapping, sealing, case-packing, palletizing, and inspection of food products across the **primary, secondary, and tertiary packaging** stages. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEFTT864 – Module 2):** > Robotics in food packaging encompasses the use of automated, programmable machines to perform **material handling, pick-and-place, palletizing/depalletizing, and packaging line operations** within hygienic (IP65/IP67/IP69K rated) environments, ensuring compliance with **FDA, USDA, and EHEDG** food-safety standards. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tireless Sanitary Assistant" Imagine a high-end Japanese sushi bar where a single highly-trained chef must: - Pick delicate nigiri (rice balls) without crushing them - Place them at exact positions on a plate - Do this **15,000 times a day** at the **exact same speed** and **zero fatigue** - Wash hands/sanitize between every single operation - Operate inside a refrigerated stainless-steel chamber That is *exactly* what a **Food Packaging Robot** does — but in a fraction of the time, with micrometer precision, and without ever needing a coffee break. ## 1.3 Why Robotics? The Industrial Justification | Parameter | Human Operator | Robotic System | |---|---|---| | **Cycle Time Consistency** | Variable (±15–20%) | Constant (±0.1%) | | **Operating Speed** | 30–60 picks/min | 100–400 picks/min | | **Hygiene Risk** | High (biological contamination) | Low (sealed IP69K) | | **Repetitive Strain Injury** | Common | None | | **Operating Cost (long-term)** | Recurring wages | One-time + maintenance | | **Operating Temperature Tolerance** | Limited (e.g., –25 °C cold rooms) | Wide (–40 °C to +80 °C capable) | > [!NOTE] > **Key Industry Statistic:** According to the *International Federation of Robotics (IFR) 2023 Report*, the food industry ranks as the **3rd largest adopter** of industrial robots globally, with packaging applications accounting for nearly **42%** of all robotic deployments in food processing plants. ## 1.4 Core Terminology Snapshot - **End-Effector (Gripper):** The "hand" of the robot that physically contacts the food product. - **Degrees of Freedom (DoF):** The number of independent axes of motion (food robots typically use **4–6 DoF**). - **Cycle Time (T_c):** Time to complete one full pick-place-return sequence, usually measured in **seconds (s)**. - **Payload (W_p):** Maximum mass the robot can manipulate at full reach, measured in **kilograms (kg)**. - **Reach (R):** Maximum radial distance from robot base to end-effector, in **millimeters (mm)**. - **Repeatability:** Position accuracy returning to a taught point, typically **±0.02 mm to ±0.1 mm** for food robots. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Taxonomy of Robots Used in Food Packaging Robots are classified by their **kinematic structure** (joint arrangement). Each topology offers distinct advantages for specific food-handling tasks. ### 2.1.1 Articulated Arm Robot (6-Axis) - **Structure:** Series of **6 rotary joints** (anthropomorphic, like a human arm). - **Workspace:** Spherical / near-hemispherical. - **Best For:** Case packing, palletizing, complex 3D trajectories. - **Typical Payload:** 5 kg – 500 kg. - **Industry Examples:** ABB IRB series, KUKA KR, FANUC M-20iA. ### 2.1.2 SCARA Robot (Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm) - **Structure:** 2 parallel rotary joints + 1 linear prismatic joint (Z-axis). - **Workspace:** Cylindrical / planar dome. - **Best For:** High-speed top-loading of flat products (biscuits, chocolates, blister packs). - **Typical Speed:** 100–200 picks/min. - **Compliance:** Rigid in Z, compliant in XY (ideal for vertical insertion). ### 2.1.3 Delta Robot (Parallel Kinematic) - **Structure:** 3 arms connected to a common base via parallelogram linkages, driving a single end-effector platform. - **Workspace:** Hemispherical dome above the base. - **Best For:** Ultra-high-speed pick-and-place of lightweight items (< 1 kg): bread, candies, frozen foods, pouches. - **Typical Speed:** Up to **300–600 picks/min**. - **Acceleration:** Up to **10 g** (extremely fast). ### 2.1.4 Cartesian / Gantry Robot - **Structure:** 3 linear axes (X, Y, Z) on a gantry frame. - **Workspace:** Rectangular box. - **Best For:** Heavy payload, large reach, simple pick-place (e.g., crate stacking). - **Typical Payload:** Up to **1000 kg**. ## 2.2 End-Effectors (Grippers) for Food Handling | Gripper Type | Working Principle | Food Application Example | |---|---|---| | **Mechanical (Finger) Gripper** | Two/three pneumatically actuated fingers | Bottles, jars, tetra-paks | | **Vacuum (Suction) Gripper** | Negative pressure via vacuum pump/cups | Wrapped bread, cheese blocks, pouches | | **Magnetic Gripper** | Electromagnetic field (for ferrous items) | Canned goods (steel cans) | | **Soft / Compliant Gripper** | Silicone/elastomer fingers conforming to shape | Delicate fruits, baked goods, eggs | | **Needle / Claw Gripper** | Penetrating spikes | Fish, meat carcasses | | **Adhesive Gripper** | Sticky polymer surface (gecko-inspired) | Flat chocolates, biscuits, wafer sheets | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** Food-contact grippers must comply with **FDA 21 CFR (U.S.)** and **EU 1935/2004** regulations. Stainless steel **AISI 304** is the standard hygienic material; **AISI 316L** is used for highly corrosive or dairy environments. ## 2.3 Stages of Food Packaging Where Robots Are Deployed ### A. Primary Packaging - Direct contact with the food product. - Examples: Filling bottles, sealing pouches, capping jars, wrapping individual chocolates. - **Key Requirement:** Hygienic design, gentle handling, no product damage. ### B. Secondary Packaging - Grouping of primary packages into consumer units. - Examples: Placing bottles into 6-pack cartons, shrink-wrapping trays, case-packing. - **Key Requirement:** Speed, orientation control, pattern formation. ### C. Tertiary Packaging - Bulk handling for distribution. - Examples: Palletizing cases, stretch-wrapping pallets, depalletizing for retail. - **Key Requirement:** High payload, large reach, stability. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter | Formula / Symbol | Units | Engineering Meaning | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Throughput (P)** | $P = \dfrac{3600}{T_c}$ | units/hr | Pick rate per hour | | 2 | **Cycle Time (T_c)** | $T_c = t_{pick} + t_{move} + t_{place} + t_{return}$ | s (seconds) | Total time for one operation | | 3 | **Theoretical Max Speed** | $v_{max} = \sqrt{a \cdot L}$ | m/s | Velocity for trapezoidal profile | | 4 | **Picks per Minute (PPM)** | $PPM = \dfrac{60}{T_c}$ | picks/min | Standard industry metric | | 5 | **Robot Utilization (η)** | $\eta = \dfrac{T_{active}}{T_{active} + T_{idle}} \times 100$ | % | % time robot is productive | | 6 | **Line Efficiency (η_L)** | $\eta_L = \dfrac{P_{actual}}{P_{theoretical}} \times 100$ | % | Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE) factor | | 7 | **Payload Constraint** | $W_p \leq W_{rated} \cdot F_S$ | kg | Safety factor (F_S = 0.5–0.7) | | 8 | **Dynamic Inertia (I)** | $I = m r^2$ | kg·m² | Rotational inertia about joint axis | | 9 | **Joint Torque (τ)** | $\tau = I \alpha + f_v \omega$ | N·m | Required motor torque | | 10 | **Mean Time Between Failures** | $MTBF = \dfrac{T_{total}}{N_{failures}}$ | hours | Reliability metric | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE (Per KTU Exam Pattern):** > Always carry **units** throughout the calculation. Examiners explicitly allocate marks for unit consistency. State the final answer with units enclosed in parentheses, e.g., `120 units/hr (3 marks)`. ## 2.5 Engineering Utility of Robotic Packaging Systems - **Throughput Boost:** Lines achieve **3× to 10× higher output** versus manual handling. - **Hygiene Elevation:** Closed-cell stainless-steel design reduces microbial contamination by **>90%** (validated by HACCP studies). - **Labor Reallocation:** Workers shift to supervisory, quality-control, and maintenance roles. - **Wastage Reduction:** Precise handling cuts product damage from **5–8% (manual)** down to **<0.5% (robotic)**. - **Cold-Chain Compatibility:** Robots operate reliably at **–40 °C** in frozen-food warehouses where humans cannot work efficiently. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Throughput from Cycle Time The **theoretical throughput** of a robotic pick-and-place station is derived by inverting the cycle time and scaling to standard time units. ### Given: - A delta robot performs a single pick-and-place cycle in **$T_c = 1.2$ seconds**. - The line operates continuously for **8 hours/day**. ### Step-by-Step Evaluation: **Step 1:** State the basic throughput relation. $$ P = \dfrac{3600}{T_c} $$ *Logic:* The number 3600 converts seconds into one hour (60 s/min × 60 min/hr). **Step 2:** Substitute the given value of cycle time. $$ P = \dfrac{3600 \ \text{s/hr}}{1.2 \ \text{s}} $$ *Logic:* Unit cancellation — the seconds in numerator and denominator cancel, leaving units/hr. **Step 3:** Perform the numerical division. $$ P = 3000 \ \text{units/hr} $$ **Step 4:** Scale to the daily shift duration. $$ P_{daily} = P \times H = 3000 \ \text{units/hr} \times 8 \ \text{hr/day} $$ $$ P_{daily} = 24{,}000 \ \text{units/day} $$ **Step 5:** Apply a realistic line efficiency factor (typical for food plants). $$ P_{actual} = P_{daily} \times \eta_L = 24{,}000 \times 0.85 $$ $$ P_{actual} = 20{,}400 \ \text{units/day} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** Although the robot is *capable* of 24,000 units/day, the realistic OEE-adjusted output is ~20,400 units/day. KTU examiners expect you to *always* state this distinction. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Robot Selection — Payload with Safety Factor A SCARA robot is rated for **$W_{rated} = 10$ kg** payload. Determine the **maximum allowable product mass** including gripper weight, given a safety factor of $F_S = 0.6$. ### Step-by-Step Evaluation: **Step 1:** Write the payload safety constraint. $$ W_p \leq W_{rated} \times F_S $$ *Logic:* Safety factor (F_S) < 1 because we derate the rated capacity to account for dynamic loads, acceleration spikes, and gripper weight. **Step 2:** Substitute the values. $$ W_p \leq 10 \ \text{kg} \times 0.6 $$ **Step 3:** Compute the result. $$ W_p \leq 6 \ \text{kg} $$ **Step 4:** Subtract the gripper mass (assume $W_g = 0.5$ kg) to find the maximum product mass. $$ W_{product,max} = W_p - W_g = 6 - 0.5 = 5.5 \ \text{kg} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Answer:** $W_{product,max} = 5.5$ kg. > **KTU 2024 Tip:** If the product mass exceeds 5.5 kg, the SCARA is *undersized* and a 20 kg-rated articulated arm must be selected. This is a classic 7-mark reasoning question. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Trapezoidal Velocity Profile for Delta Robot Acceleration A delta robot accelerates a **1 kg payload** over a horizontal pick distance of **L = 0.2 m**, achieving **a = 5 m/s²** constant acceleration. Calculate the maximum velocity. ### Step-by-Step Evaluation: **Step 1:** Use the kinematic equation for constant acceleration from rest. $$ v^2 = u^2 + 2 a s $$ **Step 2:** Apply initial velocity u = 0 (starting from rest at pick point). $$ v^2 = 0 + 2 \times 5 \times 0.2 $$ **Step 3:** Compute the squared velocity. $$ v^2 = 2.0 \ \text{(m/s)}^2 $$ **Step 4:** Take the square root to obtain the velocity. $$ v = \sqrt{2.0} = 1.414 \ \text{m/s} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Realistic Note:** In real delta robots, peak velocity reaches **8–10 m/s** with accelerations of **10 g (≈98 m/s²)**. The above represents a conservative classroom-scale calculation for clarity. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Robot Selection Decision Table | Application Task | Recommended Robot | Gripper Type | Cycle Time (s) | Payload (kg) | Hygiene Rating | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Bottle case packing | Articulated (6-axis) | Mechanical finger | 1.5 – 2.5 | 5 – 15 | IP65 | | Bread pick-and-place | Delta | Vacuum suction | 0.10 – 0.20 | 0.2 – 1.0 | IP67 | | Frozen food palletizing | Articulated (heavy) | Bag gripper | 4.0 – 6.0 | 50 – 200 | IP65 | | Chocolate tray loading | SCARA / Delta | Soft / Adhesive | 0.15 – 0.30 | 0.05 – 0.5 | IP67 | | Carton erecting | Cartesian | Vacuum | 2.0 – 3.0 | 1 – 3 | IP65 | | Meat portioning (raw) | Articulated (washdown) | Needle / Claw | 1.0 – 2.0 | 2 – 5 | **IP69K** | | Pouch handling | Delta | Vacuum cup | 0.12 – 0.25 | 0.1 – 0.8 | IP67 | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Robotic Food Packaging Cell ```mermaid flowchart TB IN[INFEED CONVEYOR
Raw Food Product Arrival] --> V1[VISION SYSTEM 1
Position and Orientation Detection] V1 --> PLC[PROGRAMMABLE LOGIC CONTROLLER
Master Coordination Unit] PLC --> R1[ROBOT ARM
6-Axis Articulated or Delta] R1 --> G1[END EFFECTOR
Gripper / Suction / Soft] G1 --> V2[VISION SYSTEM 2
Placement Verification] V2 --> PK[PACKAGING STATION
Tray Carton Pouch] PK --> PL[PALLETIZER ROBOT
Tertiary Packaging] PL --> OUT[OUTFEED
Dispatch to Cold Chain] PLC --> HMI[HMI TOUCHSCREEN
Operator Interface] PLC --> SAF[SAFETY PLC
Light Curtains E Stops] HMI --> DB[DATABASE SERVER
