OEE Traceability] style IN fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#333 style PLC fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style R1 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724 style G1 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24 style PL fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724 style SAF fill:#f5c6cb,stroke:#721c24 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** > - **PLC** is the brain — it synchronizes the conveyor, robot, vision, and safety systems. > - **Vision System 1** identifies product location, orientation, and quality (defects, color, size). > - **End Effector** is the only part that touches the food — it must be food-grade stainless steel or FDA-approved polymer. > - **Palletizer Robot** is a separate downstream cell for tertiary packaging. --- ## 4.2 Decision Flowchart — Choosing the Right Robot for a Food Application ```mermaid flowchart TD A[START: Define Product and Task] --> B{Product Weight W_p} B -->|W_p less than 1 kg| C{Required Speed} B -->|W_p 1 to 10 kg| D{Workspace Geometry} B -->|W_p greater than 10 kg| E[Use ARTICULATED 6-AXIS ROBOT] C -->|Greater than 200 PPM| F[Use DELTA PARALLEL ROBOT] C -->|50 to 200 PPM| G{Insertion Vertical Needed} G -->|Yes| H[Use SCARA ROBOT] G -->|No| F D -->|Rectangular Large Reach| I[Use CARTESIAN GANTRY ROBOT] D -->|Spherical Compact| E F --> J[Add VACUUM or SOFT GRIPPER] E --> K[Add MECHANICAL or NEEDLE GRIPPER] H --> L[Add FINGER or SUCTION GRIPPER] I --> M[Add VACUUM or CLAMP GRIPPER] J --> N[CHECK: IP Rating and Hygiene Compliance] K --> N L --> N M --> N N --> O{Hygiene Zone} O -->|Dry Ambient| P[IP65 Sufficient] O -->|Washdown Wet| Q[IP67 Required] O -->|High Pressure Steam| R[IP69K Mandatory] P --> S[FINAL SELECTION] Q --> S R --> S style A fill:#e7f3ff,stroke:#003366 style S fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,stroke-width:3px style R fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Takeaway:** The decision tree follows a **hierarchical elimination logic** — first by **payload**, then by **speed/workspace**, and finally by **hygiene rating**. Examiners love flowcharts because they test your ability to *systematically reason* through engineering trade-offs. --- ## 4.3 Anatomy of a Food-Grade Robot Cell (Schematic Block View) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PERIPHERALS P1[Infeed Conveyor] P2[Vision Camera 2D or 3D] P3[Light Curtain Safety] P4[Reject Diverter] end subgraph CONTROL C1[Robot Controller] C2[PLC Master] C3[Servo Drives] end subgraph ROBOTIC ARM RA1[Joint 1 Base Rotation] RA2[Joint 2 Shoulder] RA3[Joint 3 Elbow] RA4[Joint 4 Wrist 1] RA5[Joint 5 Wrist 2] RA6[Joint 6 Tool Flange] end subgraph END EFFECTOR EE1[Quick Changer] EE2[Gripper Fingers] EE3[Vacuum Generator] end P1 --> C2 P2 --> C2 C2 --> C1 C1 --> C3 C3 --> RA1 RA1 --> RA2 --> RA3 --> RA4 --> RA5 --> RA6 RA6 --> EE1 EE1 --> EE2 EE1 --> EE3 RA6 --> P4 P3 --> C2 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Hygienic Design Note:** The robot must have **rounded, sloped surfaces** (no flat horizontal ledges where water can pool), **captive stainless-steel fasteners**, and **sealed cable routings** to comply with **EHEDG (European Hygienic Engineering and Design Group)** guidelines. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** **Define the term "End-Effector" as used in robotic food packaging. List any four types of end-effectors commonly used in food handling applications.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** An **end-effector** is the device attached to the **tool flange** of a robot's wrist that physically interacts with the food product to perform picking, placing, holding, or manipulating tasks. It is the *functional equivalent of a human hand* and is the only part of the robotic system in direct contact with the food. **(1 mark for definition)** Four types of end-effectors used in food handling: **(2 marks — 0.5 each)** 1. **Mechanical Finger Gripper** — two pneumatically-actuated fingers for rigid items (bottles, jars). 2. **Vacuum Suction Gripper** — uses negative pressure and suction cups for smooth surfaces (bread, cheese, pouches). 3. **Soft Compliant Gripper** — silicone/elastomer fingers for delicate products (fruits, baked goods, eggs). 4. **Needle / Claw Gripper** — penetrating spikes used for raw meat, fish, or poultry carcasses. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **What is the significance of IP69K rating in food-grade robotic systems? Differentiate between IP65, IP67, and IP69K ratings.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **IP69K rating** signifies the robot's ability to withstand **high-pressure (80–100 bar), high-temperature (80 °C) steam-jet cleaning** from close range, making it suitable for **direct food-contact zones** and **washdown environments** in meat, dairy, and beverage industries. **(1 mark)** | Rating | Protection Level | Food Industry Use | |---|---|---| | **IP65** | Dust-tight + low-pressure water jets | Dry packaging zones (cartoning, palletizing) | | **IP67** | Dust-tight + immersion up to 1 m for 30 min | Wet zones (bottle filling, rinsing) | | **IP69K** | Dust-tight + high-pressure, high-temperature steam jets | Direct food-contact, meat/dairy washdown | **(2 marks for the comparison table)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 | Module 2 Internal Choice]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of an automated robotic food packaging cell**. Clearly label the infeed, vision system, robot controller, end-effector, packaging station, and palletizer. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A delta robot is used to pick wrapped bread loaves from a moving conveyor. The cycle time is measured as **0.20 seconds per pick**. The plant operates in **two shifts of 8 hours each**. The overall line efficiency is **82%**. Calculate: - (i) The theoretical picks per minute (PPM) - (ii) The daily actual throughput in loaves/day - (iii) The monthly throughput assuming **26 working days/month** **(7 marks)** --- ### **Solution to Question A:** #### Part (a) — Functional Architecture (7 marks) An automated robotic food packaging cell consists of the following interconnected subsystems: **(7 marks: 1 mark per major block + 2 marks for explanation of integration)** 1. **Infeed Conveyor System:** Transports the raw food product (e.g., bread loaves) into the work envelope of the robot at a controlled, regulated speed. 2. **Vision Inspection System (2D / 3D Camera):** Detects the **X, Y, Z position, orientation, and quality** of each incoming product on the conveyor. The vision data is sent to the PLC. 3. **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC):** Acts as the **master coordinator**, synchronizing the conveyor, vision system, robot controller, and downstream safety systems. It also handles reject logic. 4. **Robot Controller + Servo Drives:** Receives the target coordinates from the PLC and generates **trajectory commands** for each of the 6 joints of the articulated arm (or 3 parallel arms for a delta). 5. **End-Effector (Gripper):** The actual contact device — for wrapped bread, a **vacuum suction cup gripper** is most common. The gripper picks the loaf and transfers it to the packaging station. 6. **Packaging Station:** Performs **primary packaging** (e.g., placing the loaf into a printed plastic pouch and heat-sealing it). May include a check-weigher and metal detector. 7. **Palletizer Robot (Downstream):** Once a configured number of loaves are grouped into a **case or tray**, a separate (often heavier-payload) articulated robot stacks the cases onto a pallet for tertiary packaging and dispatch. **Integration Logic:** The PLC receives "ready" signals from the infeed, triggers the vision system, waits for valid coordinates, commands the robot to pick, and only releases the next product when the current cycle is complete — ensuring **deterministic, jam-free operation**. --- #### Part (b) — Throughput Calculation (7 marks) **Given:** - Cycle time $T_c = 0.20$ s - Working hours $H = 2 \times 8 = 16$ hr/day - Line efficiency $\eta_L = 82\% = 0.82$ - Working days $D = 26$ days/month **(i) Theoretical Picks Per Minute (PPM):** **(2 marks)** $$ PPM = \dfrac{60}{T_c} = \dfrac{60}{0.20} $$ $$ PPM = 300 \ \text{picks/min} $$ **(ii) Daily Actual Throughput:** **(3 marks)** **Step 1 — Hourly throughput:** $$ P_{hr} = PPM \times 60 = 300 \times 60 = 18{,}000 \ \text{loaves/hr} $$ **Step 2 — Theoretical daily throughput:** $$ P_{daily,theoretical} = P_{hr} \times H = 18{,}000 \times 16 $$ $$ P_{daily,theoretical} = 288{,}000 \ \text{loaves/day} $$ **Step 3 — Apply line efficiency:** $$ P_{actual} = 288{,}000 \times 0.82 $$ $$ \boxed{P_{actual} = 236{,}160 \ \text{loaves/day}} $$ **(iii) Monthly Throughput:** **(2 marks)** $$ P_{monthly} = P_{actual} \times D = 236{,}160 \times 26 $$ $$ \boxed{P_{monthly} = 6{,}140{,}160 \ \text{loaves/month}} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** > - Students often forget to **convert seconds → minutes → hours** correctly. Always show the unit-cancellation logic explicitly. > - Do NOT multiply the line efficiency by the cycle time; it is applied to the **final daily output**, not to individual cycle time. > - Marks are deducted if you forget to state the **formula in symbolic form** *before* substituting values. The 2024 scheme mandates **stepwise, formula-first** answers. --- ### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 | Module 2 Internal Choice]** **(a)** Compare the following robot types with respect to their **kinematic structure, workspace, payload capacity, typical cycle time, and one specific food-packaging application**: - (i) Articulated Robot - (ii) Delta Robot - (iii) SCARA Robot **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss in detail the **role of vision systems and sensors in robotic food packaging**. Explain how 2D vision, 3D vision, and force/torque sensing contribute to quality control and adaptive handling. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Solution to Question B:** #### Part (a) — Comparison Table (7 marks — full table for 7 marks) | Parameter | **Articulated Robot (6-Axis)** | **Delta Robot (3-Axis)** | **SCARA Robot (4-Axis)** | |---|---|---|---| | **Kinematic Structure** | 6 serial rotary joints (anthropomorphic) | 3 parallel arms + central platform | 2 rotary + 1 prismatic + 1 rotary | | **Workspace Geometry** | Spherical, near-hemispherical | Hemispherical dome above base | Cylindrical, planar-friendly | | **Payload Capacity** | 5 kg – 500 kg | 0.1 kg – 8 kg | 1 kg – 20 kg | | **Typical Cycle Time** | 1.0 s – 4.0 s | 0.10 s – 0.30 s | 0.20 s – 0.80 s | | **Pick Rate (PPM)** | 30 – 90 | 200 – 600 | 75 – 250 | | **Best Food Application** | Case packing, palletizing of cartons/boxes | Bread, chocolate, frozen food pick-and-place | Vertical insertion: cap fitting, top-loading biscuits into trays | | **Repeatability** | ±0.02 – ±0.05 mm | ±0.05 – ±0.10 mm | ±0.01 – ±0.02 mm | | **Hygiene Adaptation** | IP65 – IP69K possible | IP65 – IP67 | IP65 – IP67 | **(7 marks: 1 mark per row completed for all three robots; partial credit for incomplete rows.)** --- #### Part (b) — Role of Vision and Sensors (7 marks) **1. 2D Vision Systems:** **(2 marks)** - Use a single **CCD/CMOS camera** mounted above the conveyor to capture a **2D top-view image** of the product. - Algorithms detect: **X-Y position, orientation angle, area, perimeter, and color**. - Used for: **basic pick-and-place**, label verification, color sorting (e.g., rejecting discolored fruits), and barcode reading. - Limitation: Cannot determine the **height (Z)** of stacked or overlapping products. **2. 3D Vision Systems:** **(2 marks)** - Use either **laser triangulation**, **structured light (laser stripe)**, or **time-of-flight cameras**. - Generate a **point cloud** of the scene, providing full **X, Y, Z coordinates** plus surface geometry. - Used for: **bin-picking** (randomly piled products), **layer-cake inspection**, **volume measurement**, and **deformed/irregular items** (chicken breasts, fish fillets). - Enables robots to pick from **unordered bulk containers** — a major trend in Industry 4.0 food factories. **3. Force / Torque Sensors:** **(1.5 marks)** - Mounted at the **robot wrist**, between the arm and the end-effector. - Detect contact forces in **X, Y, Z** and torques about each axis. - Used for: - **Compliant grasping** — adjusting grip force to avoid crushing soft products (strawberries, bread). - **Collision detection** — stopping the robot if it contacts an obstacle (safety). - **Insertion tasks** — detecting proper seating of a bottle cap or lid. **4. Integrated Quality Control Function:** **(1.5 marks)** - Vision + force data is fused to enable **closed-loop, adaptive control**. - Example workflow: A camera detects a slightly off-center loaf → the controller adjusts the robot's pick trajectory → a force sensor confirms gentle contact → the loaf is placed without deformation. - This **sensor fusion** is the foundation of **CPG (Cyber-Physical Systems)** in smart food factories. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 2: Robotics in Food Packaging & Handling** ### Core Concepts - ☐ Robotics in food packaging = programmable multi-axis manipulators for pick, place, pack, palletize. - ☐ Three kinematic families used: **Articulated (versatile), Delta (high-speed), SCARA (insertion)**. - ☐ Cartesian/gantry robots are reserved for **heavy payload, large reach** tasks. ### Key Metrics (Memorize Formulas) - ☐ **Throughput:** $P = 3600 / T_c$ — converts seconds to units/hour. - ☐ **PPM:** $PPM = 60 / T_c$ — picks per minute. - ☐ **Payload constraint:** Always apply **safety factor $F_S = 0.5–0.7$** to rated capacity. - ☐ **Trapezoidal velocity profile:** $v^2 = 2 a s$ (from rest). ### Hygiene & Standards - ☐ Food-contact zones require **IP67 minimum**; **IP69K** for high-pressure washdown. - ☐ **EHEDG** guidelines: sloped surfaces, captive fasteners, no horizontal ledges. - ☐ Gripper material: **AISI 304/316L stainless steel** or **FDA-grade polymers**. ### End-Effector Selection - ☐ Vacuum → smooth/wrapped products (bread, cheese). - ☐ Mechanical fingers → rigid items (bottles, jars). - ☐ Soft/compliant → delicate items (fruits, baked goods). - ☐ Needle/claw → raw meat, fish, poultry. ### Application Mapping - ☐ **Primary packaging** = direct food contact (filling, sealing). - ☐ **Secondary packaging** = grouping into consumer units (case-packing). - ☐ **Tertiary packaging** = distribution (palletizing, stretch-wrapping). ### Vision Systems - ☐ **2D vision** = position + orientation (X, Y, θ). - ☐ **3D vision** = adds Z (height) for bin-picking and irregular shapes. - ☐ **Force/torque sensors** = compliant gripping and collision safety. ### KTU 2024 Exam Smart Tips - ☐ Always show **formula → substitution → evaluation → unit-cancellation**. - ☐ Always state **assumptions** (e.g., "assuming 100% line efficiency" or "with 85% OEE"). - ☐ Use **block diagrams** liberally — a well-labeled diagram fetches **2–3 easy marks**. - ☐ Memorize the **IP rating ladder** (IP65 → IP67 → IP69K). - ☐ In choice questions, **select the one where you can draw at least one neat diagram**.

topics.title: Computer Vision Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT864 - AUTOMATION IN FOOD INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Computer Vision Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Computer Vision System (CVS)** in food industry automation is an engineered opto-electronic assembly comprising image acquisition hardware (cameras, illumination, optics), dedicated image processing algorithms, and decision-making software that mimics the human visual cortex to perform non-destructive, rapid, and objective inspection, classification, grading, and quality control of food products on high-speed production lines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A computer vision system translates raw pixel intensity data into quantifiable features (color, size, shape, texture, defect geometry) that can be acted upon by Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs) and industrial robots in real time.

Mathematically, an image acquired by a vision system is a discrete two-dimensional function:

$$f(x, y, t)$$

where $x$ and $y$ denote spatial coordinates, $t$ denotes the temporal instance, and the amplitude of $f$ at any coordinate is termed the **intensity** or **gray level** of the image at that point.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Food Quality Inspector's Robot Eye"

Imagine a highly trained human food inspector standing at the end of a conveyor belt in a fruit-sorting factory. The inspector has a checklist: *"Is the apple red enough? Is there a bruise? Is the diameter at least 70 mm? Are there any holes from worms?"* The human can do this well but only for a few seconds before tiring, and at most inspects 1–2 fruits per second.

Now replace the human's eyes with a **high-speed industrial camera**, the brain with a **digital image processor**, and the checklist with **algorithmic thresholds**. The result is a **Computer Vision System** that:
- Inspects **hundreds of items per second** without fatigue.
- Provides **objective, reproducible** decisions (no "mood" bias).
- Operates in environments **hostile to humans** (cold, hot, dusty, or sterile rooms).
- Generates **digital data** that can be stored for traceability (an FSSC 22000 / HACCP requirement).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for Examiners:** *Machine Vision* and *Computer Vision* are often used interchangeably. However, in industrial automation, **Machine Vision** typically refers to the engineered system (hardware + software on a factory floor), whereas **Computer Vision** leans more toward the algorithmic and AI-driven interpretation. In KTU context, treat them as synonymous for food inspection applications.

## 1.3 Core Components of a Food-Industry CVS

| # | Component | Function in Food Industry |
|---|-----------|---------------------------|
| 1 | **Illumination Unit** | Provides consistent, repeatable lighting (LED ring, backlight, dome) — eliminates ambient variability. |
| 2 | **Image Sensor** | CCD or CMOS camera converts reflected/transmitted light into digital pixel data. |
| 3 | **Optics / Lens** | Controls field of view, working distance, and magnification. |
| 4 | **Frame Grabber / Interface** | Captures frames via GigE Vision, USB3 Vision, or Camera Link. |
| 5 | **Processing Unit** | PC / embedded processor running image-processing algorithms. |
| 6 | **I/O & Actuators** | Sends reject signals to PLC, pneumatic diverter, or robotic arm. |
| 7 | **HMI / Software** | Operator interface for recipe management, calibration, and diagnostics. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Standard Metrics to Memorize:**
> - **Resolution** — measured in **megapixels (MP)** or **pixels per mm**.
> - **Frame Rate** — measured in **frames per second (fps)**; a typical food line runs at **5,000 – 50,000 products/hour** requiring **≥ 30 fps** cameras.
> - **Pixel Bit Depth** — typically **8-bit (256 gray levels)** or **24-bit RGB**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Pipeline of a CVS

A Computer Vision System for food inspection follows a structured, sequential pipeline. Each step is critical; an error in image acquisition cannot be corrected later by a clever algorithm.

### Step 1 — Image Acquisition (The "Seeing" Stage)
- The **camera** captures the food product under controlled **illumination**.
- The light source wavelength must be chosen carefully. For instance:
  - **Visible (400–700 nm)** — general color and surface defect inspection.
  - **Near-Infrared (NIR, 700–1100 nm)** — internal quality, sugar content (Brix), water content.
  - **Hyperspectral / Multispectral** — combines multiple wavelengths for chemical mapping.

### Step 2 — Pre-Processing (The "Cleaning" Stage)
- **Noise Removal:** Gaussian filter, median filter.
- **Contrast Enhancement:** Histogram equalization.
- **Image Restoration:** Geometric correction for lens distortion.

### Step 3 — Segmentation (The "Isolating" Stage)
- The food item is separated from the background using techniques like **thresholding**, **edge detection**, or **region growing**.
- Output is a **binary mask** where "1" = object pixels and "0" = background.

### Step 4 — Feature Extraction (The "Measuring" Stage)
The system extracts quantifiable features categorized as:
- **Geometric features:** area, perimeter, centroid, major/minor axis length, circularity.
- **Color features:** mean RGB, HSV histograms, color moments.
- **Texture features:** Gray Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) — contrast, correlation, energy, homogeneity.
- **Spectral features:** Mean intensity per wavelength band (for NIR/hyperspectral).

### Step 5 — Classification / Decision (The "Judging" Stage)
- Features are fed into a **classifier** — Rule-based (if–else thresholds), Statistical (Bayes, k-NN), or modern **Deep Learning (CNN, YOLO, ResNet)**.
- The output class (e.g., *"Grade A," "Grade B," "Reject"*) triggers a PLC output.

### Step 6 — Actuation (The "Acting" Stage)
- A **pneumatic nozzle** blows the reject off the line, or a **robotic delta arm** picks and places into grade-specific bins.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this sequence crucial?** In an exam, if asked to "explain the working of a CVS," you must mention all six stages in this order. Skipping the pre-processing or segmentation stage will cost marks.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Meaning | Unit |
|---|---------|---------|------------------|------|
| 1 | Image as a 2-D matrix | $f(x,y) \in [0, L-1]$ | $L$ = number of gray levels ($L=256$ for 8-bit) | — |
| 2 | Image Resolution (spatial) | $R = N_x \times N_y$ | $N_x, N_y$ = pixels along x, y | pixels |
| 3 | File size (uncompressed) | $S = R \times b$ | $b$ = bit depth | bits |
| 4 | Optical resolution on conveyor | $d_{mm} = \dfrac{FoV}{N_x}$ | FoV = field of view on belt | mm/pixel |
| 5 | Minimum detectable defect size | $d_{min} \geq 2 \cdot d_{mm}$ | Nyquist sampling rule | mm |
| 6 | Throughput requirement | $fps \geq \dfrac{Q}{60} \cdot \dfrac{L_{prod}}{L_{img}}$ | $Q$ = products/min, $L$ = lengths in mm | fps |
| 7 | Circularity of a blob | $C = \dfrac{4\pi A}{P^2}$ | $A$ = area (px²), $P$ = perimeter (px) | dimensionless |
| 8 | Aspect Ratio | $AR = \dfrac{d_{max}}{d_{min}}$ | Major / minor Feret diameter | dimensionless |
| 9 | Mean gray value | $\bar{f} = \dfrac{1}{MN}\sum_{x=0}^{M-1}\sum_{y=0}^{N-1} f(x,y)$ | $M, N$ = image dimensions | gray level |
| 10 | Standard deviation (contrast) | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{MN}\sum (f(x,y) - \bar{f})^2}$ | Spread of intensity | gray level |
| 11 | GLCM Contrast | $C_{GLCM} = \sum_{i}\sum_{j}(i-j)^2 \cdot P(i,j)$ | $P(i,j)$ = normalized co-occurrence matrix | — |
| 12 | SNR of an image | $SNR = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\mu_{signal}}{\sigma_{noise}}\right)$ | Signal mean to noise std. ratio | dB |
| 13 | Conveyer speed to camera trigger | $v = k \cdot \dfrac{L_{img}}{t_{exp}}$ | $k$ = overlap factor, $t_{exp}$ = exposure | m/s |
| 14 | HSV saturation | $S = \dfrac{\max(R,G,B) - \min(R,G,B)}{\max(R,G,B)}$ | (if max ≠ 0) | — |
| 15 | True Positive Rate (classification) | $TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Recall / sensitivity | — |

> [!CAUTION]
> In the table above, all absolute-value-like delimiters have been replaced with $\vert$ or $\mid$ to preserve Markdown table integrity. Do **not** use the raw `\|` inside the table cells in your answer booklets either — write it as $\mid$ for LaTeX or `(abs)` for plain text.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering

| Application | Industry Segment | Measured Feature |
|-------------|------------------|------------------|
| Apple defect sorting | Horticulture | Color, bruises, stem/calyx |
| Pizza topping inspection | Bakery | Topping count, distribution |
| Bread slice volume | Bakery | Height map via laser triangulation |
| Chicken carcass contamination | Meat | Fecal matter fluorescence under UV |
| Glass bottle crack detection | Beverage | Edge / texture analysis |
| Pill sorting in pharma | (Adjacent field) | Color, shape, embossed text |
| Fish species classification | Seafood | Shape + color + texture |
| Almond / peanut grading | Nuts | Size + defect + insect damage |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> All derivations below are presented in long-form, with every algebraic transition explicitly justified. A blank line separates every display equation from surrounding text, per KTU LaTeX formatting norms.

## 3.1 Derivation: Optimum Camera Resolution for a Given Conveyor Setup

**Problem Context:** A bakery produces biscuits of length $L_{prod} = 60$ mm on a conveyor moving at $v = 1.5$ m/s. The smallest defect to be detected is $d_{min} = 1$ mm. The camera has a horizontal field of view $FoV = 300$ mm and an exposure time $t_{exp} = 200\ \mu s$. Find:
(a) the required pixel resolution along the scan direction,
(b) the minimum number of pixels along the conveyor length,
(c) the required frame rate.

### Part (a) — Required Pixel Resolution

According to the **Nyquist Sampling Theorem** (a fundamental KTU digital-imaging concept), to faithfully resolve a feature of size $d_{min}$, the pixel footprint on the product must be at most **half** that size.

$$\text{Pixel footprint } d_{mm} = \dfrac{FoV}{N_x}$$

Nyquist criterion for minimum detectable defect:

$$d_{min} \geq 2 \cdot d_{mm}$$

Substituting the expression for $d_{mm}$:

$$d_{min} \geq 2 \cdot \dfrac{FoV}{N_x}$$

Solving for $N_x$ (the number of pixels along the scan direction):

$$N_x \geq \dfrac{2 \cdot FoV}{d_{min}}$$

Plugging in numerical values $FoV = 300$ mm and $d_{min} = 1$ mm:

$$N_x \geq \dfrac{2 \cdot 300}{1}$$

$$N_x \geq 600\ \text{pixels}$$

**Interpretation:** A 600-pixel camera is the *minimum* required. Practically, engineers double this to provide a safety margin against motion blur and lens imperfections, choosing a **1200-pixel (or higher)** sensor.

### Part (b) — Minimum Pixels Along Product Length

Since the entire field of view (300 mm) is imaged, the same calculation applies, but we now express the constraint in terms of the product length:

$$N_{prod} = \dfrac{L_{prod}}{d_{mm}} = \dfrac{L_{prod} \cdot N_x}{FoV}$$

Using $N_x = 600$ (minimum), $L_{prod} = 60$ mm, $FoV = 300$ mm:

$$N_{prod} = \dfrac{60 \cdot 600}{300}$$

$$N_{prod} = 120\ \text{pixels per biscuit}$$

**Interpretation:** Each 60-mm biscuit will be represented by at least 120 pixels along its length. A 1-mm defect will therefore span 2 pixels — the bare minimum to be detectable.

### Part (c) — Required Frame Rate

The biscuit is on the conveyor for a dwell time:

$$t_{dwell} = \dfrac{L_{prod}}{v} = \dfrac{60 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}}{1.5\ \text{m/s}}$$

$$t_{dwell} = 0.04\ \text{s} = 40\ \text{ms}$$

To guarantee at least one full image per biscuit:

$$fps \geq \dfrac{1}{t_{dwell}}$$

$$fps \geq \dfrac{1}{0.04}$$

$$fps \geq 25\ \text{frames per second}$$

**Check on Motion Blur:** The displacement of the biscuit during one exposure:

$$\Delta x = v \cdot t_{exp} = 1.5 \cdot 200 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\Delta x = 3 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m} = 0.3\ \text{mm}$$

Since $\Delta x = 0.3$ mm $< d_{min} = 1$ mm, the exposure time is acceptable. A **global shutter** camera is mandatory to avoid rolling-shutter distortion of moving products.

## 3.2 Derivation: Circularity Feature for Shape Classification

**Context:** A vision system extracts apple silhouettes. The extracted blob has area $A = 1850$ pixels and perimeter $P = 162$ pixels. Classify the apple's circularity.

The **circularity** (also called *isoperimetric quotient*) of a 2-D shape is defined as:

$$C = \dfrac{4 \pi A}{P^2}$$

This metric equals **1.0** for a perfect circle, and **decreases** for elongated, rough, or star-shaped objects.

Substituting the observed values:

$$C = \dfrac{4 \pi \cdot 1850}{162^2}$$

$$C = \dfrac{4 \cdot 3.1416 \cdot 1850}{26244}$$

$$C = \dfrac{23247.6}{26244}$$

$$C \approx 0.886$$

**Classification rule (typical grading line):**
- $C \geq 0.90$ → "Premium round grade"
- $0.75 \leq C < 0.90$ → "Standard grade"
- $C < 0.75$ → "Reject (irregular shape)"

Since $C = 0.886$, the apple is in the **Standard grade** band.

**Sanity check:** For a perfect circle of radius $r$ (in pixels), $A = \pi r^2$ and $P = 2\pi r$. Substituting:

$$C_{circle} = \dfrac{4 \pi \cdot \pi r^2}{(2\pi r)^2} = \dfrac{4 \pi^2 r^2}{4 \pi^2 r^2} = 1$$

Confirmed: a perfect circle yields $C = 1$. Our apple's $C = 0.886$ indicates a slightly oblong shape, consistent with natural fruit morphology.

## 3.3 Derivation: Threshold-Based Defect Segmentation (Global Thresholding)

**Context:** A grayscale image $f(x,y)$ of a chocolate cookie has a histogram that is bimodal: cookie pixels cluster around gray level $\mu_1 = 180$ (light brown) and defect pixels cluster around $\mu_2 = 60$ (dark scorch marks). Find an optimal threshold $T$ using **Otsu's Method** (maximizing inter-class variance).

The normalized histogram of gray levels $i \in [0, 255]$ is $p_i$. The probabilities of the two classes separated at threshold $T$ are:

$$\omega_0(T) = \sum_{i=0}^{T} p_i$$

$$\omega_1(T) = \sum_{i=T+1}^{255} p_i$$

The class means are:

$$\mu_0(T) = \dfrac{\sum_{i=0}^{T} i \cdot p_i}{\omega_0(T)}$$

$$\mu_1(T) = \dfrac{\sum_{i=T+1}^{255} i \cdot p_i}{\omega_1(T)}$$

The **inter-class variance** (to be maximized) is:

$$\sigma_{B}^{2}(T) = \omega_0 \omega_1 \big( \mu_0 - \mu_1 \big)^2$$

The optimal threshold is the value of $T$ that maximizes $\sigma_{B}^{2}(T)$:

$$T^{*} = \arg\max_{T \in [0, 255]} \sigma_{B}^{2}(T)$$

**Numerical illustration:** Assume the following pixel counts (out of 10,000 total):

- Defect (dark) region: 1,500 pixels centered at gray level 60.
- Cookie (light) region: 8,500 pixels centered at gray level 180.

Normalized probabilities (Gaussian-simplified):

$$p_{defect} = \dfrac{1500}{10000} = 0.15, \quad \mu_0 = 60$$

$$p_{cookie} = \dfrac{8500}{10000} = 0.85, \quad \mu_1 = 180$$

Trial threshold $T = 100$ (between 60 and 180, within the valley):

$$\sigma_{B}^{2}(100) = 0.15 \cdot 0.85 \cdot (60 - 180)^2$$

$$\sigma_{B}^{2}(100) = 0.1275 \cdot 14400$$

$$\sigma_{B}^{2}(100) = 1836$$

Any other $T$ in the valley region (say $T=110, 120, 90$) gives the same value to first order (because the Gaussian peaks are tight). Therefore the algorithm converges robustly to a threshold in the valley between the two peaks, separating the dark scorch marks from the cookie surface.

**Output binary image $g(x,y)$:**

$$g(x,y) = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if } f(x,y) \leq T^{*} \quad (\text{defect}) \\ 0, & \text{if } f(x,y) > T^{*} \quad (\text{healthy surface}) \end{cases}$$

The blob of 1's is then measured for area, perimeter, and circularity to decide *reject* vs. *accept*.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Block Architecture of a Food-Industry CVS

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete signal and data flow of an industrial CVS deployed on a food conveyor.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ILLUM[ILLUMINATION MODULE]
        LED1[LED Ring Light]
        LED2[Backlight Panel]
        LED3[Diffused Dome]
    end

    PRODA[Food Product on Conveyor] -->|Reflected Light| OPTIC1[Lens Optics]
    OPTIC1 -->|Focused Image| SENSORA[CMOS or CCD Sensor]

    LED1 -.->|Provides structured light| PRODA
    LED2 -.->|Silhouette imaging| PRODA
    LED3 -.->|Diffuse uniform light| PRODA

    SENSORA -->|Analog or Digital Pixel Stream| FG[Frame Grabber or Interface]
    FG -->|Raw Image Buffer| PREP[Pre-Processing Module]
    PREP -->|Noise Reduction| FILT[Filtering Module]
    FILT -->|Enhanced Image| SEG[Segmentation Module]
    SEG -->|Binary Mask| FEAT[Feature Extraction Module]
    FEAT -->|Feature Vector| CLAS[Classifier or CNN]
    CLAS -->|Decision Class| PLC1[PLC or Industrial PC]
    PLC1 -->|Reject Signal| ACT1[Pneumatic Diverter]
    PLC1 -->|Pick Command| ACT2[Delta Robot Arm]
    PLC1 -->|Pass Signal| ACT3[Accept Chute]

    CLAS -->|Statistics & Logs| DB[(SQL Database for Traceability)]
    HMI[Operator HMI Panel] -->|Recipe Selection| PLC1
    DB --> HMI
```

## 4.2 Image-Processing Pipeline (Detailed Functional View)

This diagram shows the algorithmic stages applied to a single image frame, expanding the central column of the architecture above.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IMG1[Raw Image f of x y] --> IMG2[Step 1 Pre Processing]
    IMG2 --> IMG3[Noise Filtering]
    IMG3 --> IMG4[Histogram Equalization]
    IMG4 --> IMG5[Geometric Correction]
    IMG5 --> IMG6[Step 2 Segmentation]
    IMG6 --> IMG7[Thresholding or Edge Detection]
    IMG7 --> IMG8[Binary Mask Generation]
    IMG8 --> IMG9[Step 3 Feature Extraction]
    IMG9 --> IMGA[Geometric Features]
    IMG9 --> IMGB[Color Features]
    IMG9 --> IMGC[Texture Features]
    IMGA --> IMGD[Combined Feature Vector]
    IMGB --> IMGD
    IMGC --> IMGD
    IMGD --> IMGE[Step 4 Classification]
    IMGE --> IMGF[Output Grade A or B or Reject]
    IMGF --> IMGG[Step 5 Actuation]
    IMGG --> IMGH[Mechanical Sorting]
```

## 4.3 Hardware Layer Architecture (Sensor-to-Actuator)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PH1[Physical Layer]
        CV[Conveyor Belt with Food Items]
        TRIG[Encoder or Optical Trigger Sensor]
    end

    subgraph PH2[Sensing Layer]
        CAM1[Area Scan Camera]
        CAM2[Line Scan Camera for Continuous Web]
        LENS[Fixed Focal or Telecentric Lens]
    end

    subgraph PH3[Computing Layer]
        IP1[Image Processing Industrial PC]
        NET1[Gigabit Ethernet or USB3 Bus]
        GPU1[Optional GPU for Deep Learning]
    end

    subgraph PH4[Control Layer]
        PLC2[Programmable Logic Controller]
        SCADA[SCADA or MES System]
    end

    subgraph PH5[Actuation Layer]
        DIVERTER[Pneumatic Reject Diverter]
        ROBOT[Pick and Place Robot]
        LABEL[Auto Label Applicator]
    end

    CV --> TRIG
    TRIG -->|Trigger Pulse| CAM1
    TRIG -->|Line Sync| CAM2
    LENS --> CAM1
    LENS --> CAM2
    CAM1 -->|GigE Vision| NET1
    CAM2 -->|Camera Link| NET1
    NET1 --> IP1
    IP1 --> GPU1
    IP1 -->|Discrete Outputs| PLC2
    PLC2 --> SCADA
    PLC2 --> DIVERTER
    PLC2 --> ROBOT
    PLC2 --> LABEL
```

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart for Product Acceptance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Image Acquired] --> S1[Extract Blob]
    S1 --> S2{Blob Area within expected size band}
    S2 -->|No| OUT1[Reject as Off Size]
    S2 -->|Yes| S3[Compute Circularity C]
    S3 --> S4{C greater than 0.75}
    S4 -->|No| OUT2[Reject as Misshapen]
    S4 -->|Yes| S5[Compute Mean Color]
    S5 --> S6{Color within grade band}
    S6 -->|No| OUT3[Reject as Discolored]
    S6 -->|Yes| S7[Defect Pixel Count]
    S7 --> S8{Defect Area less than threshold}
    S8 -->|No| OUT4[Reject as Defective]
    S8 -->|Yes| ACC[Accept Grade A]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Model answers below are concise and exam-ready. Word them in your own style in the answer booklet.

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a Computer Vision System. List its main components used in food quality inspection.*

**Model Answer:**
A Computer Vision System (CVS) is an automated opto-electronic system that captures images of food products, processes them digitally, and makes accept/reject decisions without human intervention. It mimics human visual inspection at industrial speeds.

**Main components:**
1. Illumination unit (LED ring / dome / backlight)
2. Image sensor (CCD/CMOS camera) with lens
3. Frame grabber / digital interface
4. Image processing unit (PC / embedded processor)
5. Decision / control unit (PLC)
6. Actuation hardware (diverter / robotic arm)
7. HMI software

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for listing components + 1 for correct order/justification)*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Differentiate between a line-scan camera and an area-scan camera. Give one food-industry application of each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Line-Scan Camera | Area-Scan Camera |
|-----------|------------------|------------------|
| Sensor geometry | Single row of pixels (1 × N) | 2-D matrix (M × N) |
| Image build-up | One line at a time as product moves | Full 2-D frame in one exposure |
| Trigger | Continuous line sync from encoder | Single trigger pulse per object |
| Typical use | Continuous webs, e.g., **biscuit sheet inspection on a baking belt** | Discrete items, e.g., **whole apple sorting on a cup-conveyor** |

*(3 marks: 1 for table + 1 for food example of each)*

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the working of a computer vision system for automated grading of fruits (e.g., apples) on a conveyor belt. Explain each functional block in detail. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A colour camera with 1024 × 768 pixels views a conveyor of width 400 mm. The smallest defect to be detected is 1.5 mm. Determine:
   (i) The pixel footprint on the conveyor.
   (ii) Whether the chosen resolution satisfies the Nyquist criterion.
   (iii) The minimum circularity of an acceptable apple if its measured area is 2400 pixels and perimeter is 175 pixels. Classify the apple as Premium / Standard / Reject. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Block Diagram & Description (7 marks)**

Working stages of the CVS for fruit grading:

1. **Illumination (1 mark):** A controlled LED dome light is used to provide uniform, shadow-free diffuse lighting on the apple surface. This eliminates ambient variability and ensures repeatable image contrast.
2. **Image Acquisition (1 mark):** A colour area-scan CCD/CMOS camera captures the fruit from above as it is transported by a cup-conveyor under the camera station. A photo-eye trigger synchronises the camera with the fruit's arrival.
3. **Pre-Processing (1 mark):** The captured RGB image is converted to grayscale or HSV. Median filtering removes salt-and-pepper noise; histogram equalisation enhances contrast between fruit and background.
4. **Segmentation (1 mark):** Otsu's thresholding separates the fruit (foreground) from the conveyor background. Morphological operations (opening, closing) clean the binary mask.
5. **Feature Extraction (1 mark):** Geometric features (area, perimeter, major/minor axis, circularity), colour features (mean R, G, B; HSV values), and texture features (GLCM contrast, correlation) are computed.
6. **Classification & Actuation (1 mark):** A rule-based or fuzzy-logic classifier assigns the grade. A "reject" signal is sent to a PLC, which energises a pneumatic diverter to blow substandard fruit off the line.
7. **Data Logging (1 mark):** Grade statistics are written to a database for traceability under FSSC 22000 / HACCP audits.

*(Refer to Section 4.1 block diagram for full schematic.)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**(i) Pixel Footprint (2 marks)**

$$d_{mm} = \dfrac{FoV}{N_x} = \dfrac{400\ \text{mm}}{1024\ \text{pixels}}$$

$$d_{mm} \approx 0.391\ \text{mm/pixel}$$

**(ii) Nyquist Check (2 marks)**

Nyquist requires $d_{min} \geq 2 \cdot d_{mm}$:

$$2 \cdot d_{mm} = 2 \cdot 0.391 = 0.781\ \text{mm}$$

Since $d_{min} = 1.5$ mm $\geq 0.781$ mm, the criterion is **satisfied**. The defect spans roughly:

$$\dfrac{1.5}{0.391} \approx 3.84\ \text{pixels}$$

which is comfortably detectable (≥ 2 pixels).

**(iii) Circularity (3 marks)**

$$C = \dfrac{4 \pi A}{P^2} = \dfrac{4 \pi \cdot 2400}{175^2}$$

$$C = \dfrac{30159.3}{30625}$$

$$C \approx 0.985$$

Since $C = 0.985 \geq 0.90$, the apple qualifies for the **Premium round grade**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the six-stage image processing pipeline of a food-industry CVS. For each stage, state the input, the operation, and the typical output. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss any **five** real-world food-industry applications of computer vision systems. For each, identify the specific feature measured and the defect detected. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Six-Stage Pipeline (7 marks — 1 mark per stage + 1 for coherence)**

| Stage | Input | Operation | Output |
|-------|-------|-----------|--------|
| 1. Image Acquisition | Light from food surface | Camera + lens + illumination | Raw digital image $f(x,y)$ |
| 2. Pre-Processing | Raw image | Filtering, equalisation, geometric correction | Cleaned, normalised image |
| 3. Segmentation | Cleaned image | Thresholding / edge detection | Binary mask of object |
| 4. Feature Extraction | Binary mask + grayscale | Area, perimeter, colour, texture measurements | Numeric feature vector |
| 5. Classification | Feature vector | Rule-based, statistical, or CNN model | Class label (Grade A/B/Reject) |
| 6. Actuation | Class label | PLC output, diverter energised | Physical sorting action |

*(1 mark for table format & completeness, 1 mark for naming real algorithms per stage.)*

**(b) Five Food Applications (7 marks — ~1.4 marks each)**

1. **Apple sorting** — Feature: **colour** (HSV histogram) + **circularity**. Defect: bruises, scabs, off-shape fruit. *(1.4 marks)*
2. **Pizza topping inspection** — Feature: **blob count & centroid distribution**. Defect: missing or misplaced toppings. *(1.4 marks)*
3. **Bread volume measurement** — Feature: **3-D height map** (via laser triangulation). Defect: under-proofed or collapsed loaves. *(1.4 marks)*
4. **Chicken carcass inspection** — Feature: **fluorescence under UV** (fecal matter glows). Defect: fecal contamination — a food-safety hazard. *(1.4 marks)*
5. **Glass-bottle inspection** — Feature: **edge sharpness & texture gradient**. Defect: cracks, chips, stress fractures. *(1.4 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing Machine Vision with Image Processing:** Image processing is *one* module of CVS; do not write it as the whole answer. Mention the **acquisition + actuation** chain too.
> 2. **Skipping Units in Numerical Problems:** A 14-mark numerical without units will lose 1–2 marks. Always write `mm`, `pixels`, `fps`, `m/s`.
> 3. **Writing "Camera" instead of "CCD/CMOS sensor":** Examiners expect the *sensor technology* word, not the colloquial "camera."
> 4. **Forgetting the Illumination Block:** In block diagrams, the lighting is often missing. Always include a separate "Illumination" block — it carries 1 mark.
> 5. **No mention of PLC/Robot in actuation:** Just "the system decides" is incomplete. Specify *what physical action* the decision triggers (pneumatic diverter, robot, alarm).
> 6. **Otsu's method in formula form:** If you write only the rule but not the formula $\sigma_{B}^{2} = \omega_0 \omega_1 (\mu_0 - \mu_1)^2$, you lose 2 marks in derivation questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — Computer Vision Systems**

- ✅ A **Computer Vision System** automates human visual inspection using cameras, lighting, algorithms, and actuators.
- ✅ Six functional stages: **Acquisition → Pre-processing → Segmentation → Feature Extraction → Classification → Actuation**.
- ✅ **Illumination** is the most under-appreciated yet critical block — poor lighting = poor image = poor decisions.
- ✅ Camera types: **Area-scan** for discrete items; **Line-scan** for continuous webs.
- ✅ **Nyquist criterion:** defect size ≥ 2 × pixel footprint.
- ✅ Key geometric feature: **Circularity** $C = 4\pi A / P^2$, with $C = 1$ for a perfect circle.
- ✅ **GLCM** is the standard for texture; **HSV** is preferred over RGB for colour-based sorting.
- ✅ **Otsu's method** finds the threshold that maximises inter-class variance $\sigma_{B}^{2} = \omega_0 \omega_1 (\mu_0 - \mu_1)^2$.
- ✅ Frame rate requirement: $fps \geq Q/60$ (products per second), where $Q$ is throughput.
- ✅ Exposure time must be short enough that **motion blur** $v \cdot t_{exp} < d_{min}$.
- ✅ A **global shutter** camera is essential for moving products; rolling shutter causes skew.
- ✅ Output of the CVS is sent to a **PLC**, which then triggers **pneumatic / robotic actuation** — never connect the camera directly to the diverter.
- ✅ CVS supports **traceability** by logging every grade decision — vital for **FSSC 22000 / HACCP / ISO 22000** audits.
- ✅ Modern food CVS increasingly uses **Deep Learning (CNN, YOLO, ResNet)** for defect classification.
- ✅ Be ready to draw the **block diagram** in any 14-mark question — it alone fetches 3–4 marks.
- ✅ Numerical problems are always based on **Nyquist** and **Circularity** formulas — memorise both cold.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics of Computer Vision
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT864 - AUTOMATION IN FOOD INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Basics of Computer Vision

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Computer Vision (CV)** is an interdisciplinary branch of automation engineering and artificial intelligence that enables machines to **interpret, analyze, and make decisions** from digital images and video streams. In the context of food process automation, computer vision is formally defined as a non-contact, real-time sensory system that mimics human visual perception to extract quantitative information (size, shape, color, texture, and chemical composition) from food matrices for the purposes of **quality grading, defect detection, foreign object identification, sortation, and process control**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, a computer vision system for food applications is treated as a **measurement-and-control subsystem** composed of:
- An **illumination module** (structured lighting)
- An **image acquisition module** (lens + sensor)
- An **image processing module** (hardware + algorithms)
- A **decision/actuation module** (PLC / actuator interface)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 PEFTT864):** Computer vision in food industry automation is *not* treated as a pure software topic — it is a **mechatronic integration** of optics, sensors, illumination engineering, and embedded control. Students must understand both the image formation physics *and* the practical hardware selection criteria.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Think of a computer vision system as giving a **robot a "superhuman eye" with a rule book** in a food processing line.

- A human grader on a conveyor looks at an apple and decides: "Red, no bruises, Grade A." This is slow, tiring, and inconsistent.
- A computer vision system replaces that eye with a **camera**, the brain with a **processor**, and the rule book with an **algorithm**. It does the same grading **120 fruits per second**, with the same decision every single time.

In other words, computer vision is essentially a **digitized, programmable, tireless QC inspector** standing on the production line.

## 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics (Highlighted for Memory)

The following terms are **high-yield** for KTU examinations and must be memorized:

- **Pixel** — Smallest addressable element of a digital image, characterized by intensity values.
- **Spatial Resolution** — Measured in **pixels/mm** or **megapixels**; defines the smallest distinguishable feature.
- **Field of View (FOV)** — The visible width/height of the inspection zone at the working distance, typically in **mm**.
- **Working Distance (WD)** — The physical distance from the lens front element to the object under inspection, in **mm**.
- **Focal Length (f)** — Distance at which a lens focuses parallel light rays, in **mm**.
- **Frame Rate** — Number of image frames captured per second, in **fps (frames per second)**; critical for high-speed conveyors.
- **Depth of Field (DOF)** — Range of object distances that remain in acceptable focus, in **mm**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industrial Benchmark:** A typical food sorting line operates at **1–10 m/s** conveyor speed, requiring vision systems with **≥ 5,000 fps** in line-scan configurations and **≥ 60 fps** in area-scan configurations. Always quote units explicitly in KTU numerical answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Pipeline of a Computer Vision System

A computer vision system in a food plant executes a strict sequential pipeline. The **why** and **how** of each stage is given below:

### Stage 1 — Illumination
- **Why:** Food surfaces are highly reflective, transparent, or irregular. Ambient light creates noise.
- **How:** Controlled lighting (LED ring, backlight, dome, coaxial, or structured laser) is used to **maximize contrast** between the feature of interest (e.g., bruise) and the background (e.g., healthy peel).
- Engineering rule: **Reflectance from a Lambertian surface** follows:
  $$I(x, y) = R(x, y) \cdot L(x, y)$$
  where $I$ is the recorded intensity, $R$ is the reflectance map of the food surface, and $L$ is the illumination pattern.

### Stage 2 — Image Formation (Optics)
- **Why:** A real-world 3D scene must be projected onto a 2D sensor plane.
- **How:** The lens obeys the **Gaussian thin-lens equation**. A point at object distance $u$ is brought to focus at image distance $v$ given focal length $f$:
  $$\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{v} - \frac{1}{u}$$
  (Sign convention: real objects have $u < 0$; real image has $v > 0$.)

### Stage 3 — Image Capture (Sensor)
- **Why:** Photons must be converted to discrete digital values.
- **How:** A CCD or CMOS sensor integrates photons over an exposure time and outputs a 2D array of pixel intensities, typically quantized to **8-bit (0–255)** per channel.

### Stage 4 — Pre-processing
- **Why:** Raw images contain noise, uneven illumination, and motion blur.
- **How:** Filters (Gaussian, median), histogram equalization, and color space conversion are applied.

### Stage 5 — Feature Extraction
- **Why:** Decisions must be based on measurable features, not raw pixels.
- **How:** Edge detection (Sobel, Canny), thresholding, morphological operations, or modern CNN-based feature maps.

### Stage 6 — Decision & Actuation
- **Why:** The CV system must interface with the physical line.
- **How:** The processor (often an industrial PLC, IPC, or embedded GPU) issues a digital trigger to pneumatic jets, diverter arms, or conveyor VFDs.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below contains every formula a student is expected to know for the ESE on this topic. **No vertical bars (`|`) are used in cell contents** to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Concept | Equation | Variables & Units | Application in Food Industry |
|---|---------|----------|--------------------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | Thin-lens (Gaussian) equation | $\dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{v} - \dfrac{1}{u}$ | $f$ in mm, $v$ in mm, $u$ in mm | Lens selection for inspection camera |
| 2 | Linear magnification | $m = \dfrac{v}{u} = \dfrac{h'}{h}$ | $h'$ image height, $h$ object height (mm) | Sizing fruits, biscuits, grains |
| 3 | Field of View (FOV) | $\text{FOV} = 2 f \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right)$ | $\theta$ sensor diagonal FOV, $f$ focal length (mm) | Determining inspection width |
| 4 | Working distance (sized image) | $\text{WD} = \dfrac{\text{Sensor size} \cdot \text{Object size}}{\text{Image size}} + f$ | All in mm | Conveyor camera mounting height |
| 5 | Pixel resolution (mm/pixel) | $R_p = \dfrac{\text{FOV}}{\text{Pixels across FOV}}$ | mm per pixel | Smallest detectable defect size |
| 6 | Frame rate (line-scan) | $\text{fps}_{\text{line}} = \dfrac{v_{\text{conveyor}}}{R_p}$ | $v_{\text{conveyor}}$ in mm/s, $R_p$ in mm | Synchronization with line speed |
| 7 | Luminance (grayscale) | $Y = 0.299 R + 0.587 G + 0.114 B$ | RGB 8-bit channel values | Ripeness & color grading |
| 8 | Lambertian reflectance | $I(x,y) = R(x,y) \cdot L(x,y)$ | Intensity (counts), reflectance (–), luminance (lux) | Illumination design |
| 9 | Depth of Field (DOF) | $\text{DOF} \approx \dfrac{2 N c f^2}{f^2 - N^2 c^2}$ | $N$ f-number, $c$ circle of confusion, $f$ focal length | Tolerance for product height variation |
| 10 | Pinhole projection | $x = f \dfrac{X}{Z}, \quad y = f \dfrac{Y}{Z}$ | $X,Y,Z$ world coords, $x,y$ image coords (mm) | Camera calibration math |

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

- **Quality control:** Detecting bruises on apples, cracks in eggs, broken biscuits.
- **Process control:** Real-time feedback on roast color of coffee beans.
- **Traceability:** OCR/2D-code reading on packaged goods.
- **Safety:** Detecting **foreign objects** (plastic, metal, stone) in bulk grain.
- **Chemical/Compositional analysis:** Hyperspectral vision for fat content, moisture, or sugar (Brix) estimation without contact.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Working Distance from Sensor Size and FOV

A KTU-favourite problem: *"A camera with a 1/3" sensor (6.17 mm diagonal) and 8 mm lens must inspect a 100 mm wide conveyor. Find the working distance."*

### Given
- Sensor diagonal $d_s = 6.17 \text{ mm}$
- Focal length $f = 8 \text{ mm}$
- Required FOV (object width) $W = 100 \text{ mm}$

### Step 1 — Establish the geometric similarity
The lens projects the object plane onto the sensor plane through similar triangles. The image size $w_i$ relates to the object size $W$ by the magnification $m$.

$$m = \frac{w_i}{W} = \frac{v}{u}$$

### Step 2 — Relate image size to sensor size
The image must fit within the sensor. Assuming the FOV occupies the full sensor diagonal:

$$w_i \approx d_s = 6.17 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 3 — Compute magnification
$$m = \frac{6.17 \text{ mm}}{100 \text{ mm}} = 0.0617$$

This is a strong demagnification (object much larger than sensor).

### Step 4 — Use the thin-lens equation
For a real object, $u$ is negative under the Cartesian sign convention. Using magnitudes:

$$\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{v} + \frac{1}{\vert u \vert}$$

From the magnification:
$$\vert u \vert = \frac{v}{m} = \frac{v}{0.0617}$$

### Step 5 — Substitute and solve
Substitute $\vert u \vert = v / 0.0617$ into the lens equation:

$$\frac{1}{8} = \frac{1}{v} + \frac{0.0617}{v} = \frac{1 + 0.0617}{v} = \frac{1.0617}{v}$$

Therefore:
$$v = 8 \times 1.0617 = 8.494 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 6 — Compute the object distance (Working Distance)

$$\vert u \vert = \frac{v}{m} = \frac{8.494}{0.0617} = 137.7 \text{ mm}$$

The working distance is therefore approximately **138 mm** from the lens.

### Step 7 — Compute pixel resolution
If the camera is a 5 MP sensor with 2592 × 1944 pixels across the 100 mm FOV:

$$R_p = \frac{100 \text{ mm}}{2592} = 0.0386 \text{ mm/pixel}$$

A defect of size ≥ **0.08 mm** (≈ 2 pixels) is reliably detectable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this derivation matters in food industry:** On a biscuit line, an under-baked or over-baked disc must be detected before packaging. The 0.08 mm/pixel figure sets the **lower bound on detectable defect size**, which directly drives camera and lens cost.

## 3.2 Derivation: Frame-Rate Requirement for a Moving Conveyor

### Given
- Conveyor speed $v_c = 2 \text{ m/s} = 2000 \text{ mm/s}$
- Pixel resolution $R_p = 0.5 \text{ mm/pixel}$

### Step 1 — Distance travelled per frame
For a line-scan camera, the line rate must match the conveyor speed divided by pixel size:

$$f_{\text{line}} = \frac{v_c}{R_p}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute
$$f_{\text{line}} = \frac{2000 \text{ mm/s}}{0.5 \text{ mm}} = 4000 \text{ lines/s}$$

So the line-scan camera must operate at **4 kHz**. This is non-negotiable for high-speed sorting of grains, pulses, or snack products.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix for Vision Hardware

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following matrix is a high-yield KTU comparison. Memorize it for design-type 14-mark questions.

| Parameter | Area-Scan Camera | Line-Scan Camera | Hyperspectral Camera |
|-----------|------------------|------------------|----------------------|
| Image type | 2D matrix | 1D strip per line | 2D + spectral cube |
| Typical use | Fruits, baked goods, packaged items | Grains, sheets, web inspection | Moisture, fat, Brix, contaminant ID |
| Conveyor speed suitability | Low–medium (≤ 1 m/s) | High (≥ 5 m/s) | Low (lab/inline slow line) |
| Cost | Low–medium | Medium | Very high |
| Lighting | LED ring/dome | LED bar/laser line | Tunable / broadband + dispersion |
| Data throughput | Low | High | Very high (GB/s) |
| Data handling | Simple | Moderate | Requires chemometric models |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: Food-Industry Computer Vision Station

The following **Mermaid block-level flow** depicts the complete signal and material path of a CV-based sorting station on a food processing line, from physical illumination to actuator response.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Conveyor with Food Items] --> B[Illumination Module - LED Dome or Backlight]
    B --> C[Optical Module - Lens and Filters]
    C --> D[Image Sensor - CCD or CMOS]
    D --> E[Frame Grabber or Embedded Interface]
    E --> F[Pre-processing Unit - Noise and Color Correction]
    F --> G[Feature Extraction Unit - Edge and Texture Maps]
    G --> H[Classification Engine - CNN or Rule Based]
    H --> I{Decision Gate}
    I -- Accept --> J[Pass Through Diverter]
    I -- Reject --> K[Pneumatic Reject Jet]
    H --> L[Human Machine Interface - HMI Dashboard]
    H --> M[Data Logger and PLC Interface]
    M --> N[Supervisory Control System]
```

## 4.2 Image Processing Sequence (Internal Algorithm Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Raw Image Input] --> P2[Grayscale Conversion]
    P2 --> P3[Gaussian Filtering]
    P3 --> P4[Histogram Equalization]
    P4 --> P5[Edge Detection Canny]
    P5 --> P6[Contour Extraction]
    P6 --> P7[Feature Vector Size Shape Color]
    P7 --> P8[Classifier Output Grade or Defect]
```

## 4.3 Pinhole Camera Projection Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OBJ[3D Object at Distance Z] --> LENS[Pinhole at Origin]
    LENS --> IMG[Inverted 2D Image at Distance f]
    OBJ -. X Y Z coords .-> LENS
    LENS -. x y image coords .-> IMG
```

> [!NOTE]
> The three diagrams together map: **(1) the physical station**, **(2) the algorithmic pipeline**, and **(3) the geometric projection model**. KTU questions often require reproducing one of these with proper labels.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define computer vision. List any four applications of computer vision in the food industry.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Computer vision is a non-contact sensing technology that uses cameras, illumination, and processing algorithms to automatically extract features (size, shape, color, texture) from food items for quality control and process automation.

**Four applications (2 marks — ½ mark each):**
1. **Defect detection** — bruises on apples, cracks in eggs, broken biscuits.
2. **Size and shape grading** — sorting oranges by diameter, tomatoes by roundness.
3. **Color/ripeness grading** — tomatoes, mangoes, coffee cherries.
4. **Foreign object detection** — plastic, metal, or stone contaminants in grain.

*(Alternate valid answers: OCR on packaged labels, hyperspectral fat/moisture estimation, yield monitoring in meat processing.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between area-scan and line-scan cameras in food inspection.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Area-Scan Camera | Line-Scan Camera |
|---------|------------------|------------------|
| Image captured per cycle | Full 2D frame (1 mark) | Single row of pixels, line by line (1 mark) |
| Conveyor speed | Suitable for low to medium speeds | Suitable for very high conveyor speeds |
| Typical use | Sorting fruits, baked goods | Web inspection, grain sorting, sheet inspection (½ mark) |
| Synchronization | Free-running or triggered | Must be synchronized with conveyor encoder (½ mark) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently **omit units** in lens-equation and FOV derivations → loses 1 mark per occurrence.
> - Drawing diagrams without **arrow direction** or **labels** is treated as incomplete → 1–2 marks deducted.
> - For 14-mark questions, KTU strictly requires **sub-parts (a) and (b)**, each with proper **diagrams + derivations + interpretation**. A purely textual answer is capped at ~8–9 marks.
> - Do **not** mix Cartesian and sign-magnitude conventions in the same derivation. Pick one and state it.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a computer-vision-based fruit grading system. List the key illumination strategies and justify the choice for a glossy, curved fruit such as an apple.

**(b) [7 marks]** A line-scan camera with 2048 pixels per line is to inspect a 200 mm wide conveyor moving at 1.5 m/s. The lens is 16 mm focal length and the sensor pixel pitch is 10 μm. Calculate: (i) the required line rate, (ii) the field of view per pixel, and (iii) the working distance (assume image fits the sensor).

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Functional Architecture (7 marks):**
- **Block diagram (3 marks):** A flowchart showing the five blocks — Illumination → Camera/Lens → Image Capture → Processing (PC/Embedded) → Sorting Actuator (pneumatic jet/diverter).
- **Explanation of each block (2 marks):** Each block described with food-specific function.
- **Illumination strategies (1 mark):**
  - **Dome lighting** for diffuse, shadow-free illumination of curved glossy fruit.
  - **Backlighting** for precise silhouette/size measurement.
  - **Ring lighting** for general purpose.
- **Justification for apple (1 mark):** An apple is **specular and convex**; a **dome light** provides uniform diffuse illumination, eliminating specular hotspots that would otherwise mask bruising.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $N = 2048$ pixels/line
- $W = 200 \text{ mm}$ (FOV)
- $v_c = 1.5 \text{ m/s} = 1500 \text{ mm/s}$
- $f = 16 \text{ mm}$
- Pixel pitch $p = 10 \text{ μm} = 0.01 \text{ mm}$

**(i) Line rate (2 marks):**
$$f_{\text{line}} = \frac{v_c}{R_p}$$
where $R_p = W/N = 200/2048 = 0.0977 \text{ mm/pixel}$.

$$f_{\text{line}} = \frac{1500}{0.0977} = 15{,}352 \text{ lines/s} \approx 15.4 \text{ kHz}$$

**(ii) Field of view per pixel (2 marks):**
$$R_p = 0.0977 \text{ mm/pixel} \approx 98 \text{ μm/pixel}$$

**(iii) Working distance (3 marks):**
- Sensor active length (assumed full width) = $N \cdot p = 2048 \times 0.01 = 20.48 \text{ mm}$
- Magnification $m = 20.48 / 200 = 0.1024$

From $m = v/u$ → $v = 0.1024 \cdot \vert u \vert$. Substituting into the lens equation:

$$\frac{1}{16} = \frac{1}{v} + \frac{1}{\vert u \vert} = \frac{1}{0.1024 \cdot \vert u \vert} + \frac{1}{\vert u \vert}$$

$$\frac{1}{16} = \frac{1 + 0.1024}{0.1024 \cdot \vert u \vert} = \frac{1.1024}{0.1024 \cdot \vert u \vert}$$

$$\vert u \vert = \frac{1.1024 \times 16}{0.1024} = 172.2 \text{ mm}$$

**Working distance ≈ 172 mm** from the lens to the apple.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the relationship between focal length, working distance, and field of view for a thin-lens imaging system. State the assumptions made.

**(b) [7 marks]** A dairy plant uses a vision system to detect cracks in eggs on a 50 mm wide lane. The camera sensor is 1/2" (diagonal 8 mm) with a 12 mm lens. Compute: (i) the working distance, (ii) pixel resolution if the camera has 1280 × 1024 pixels, and (iii) the maximum conveyor speed for a 1-pixel blur tolerance.

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**
*Assumptions (1 mark):* Thin lens, paraxial approximation, real inverted image, object distance $u < 0$ (sign convention), image distance $v > 0$.

*Derivation (5 marks):*

From similar triangles formed by object and image planes through the optical center:

$$m = \frac{v}{u} = \frac{h'}{h}$$

For the FOV geometry, $W$ (object width) projects onto sensor width $w_s$:

$$\frac{W}{w_s} = \frac{\vert u \vert}{v} = \frac{1}{m}$$

Using the thin-lens equation:
$$\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{v} - \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{v} + \frac{1}{\vert u \vert}$$

Substitute $v = w_s \cdot \vert u \vert / W$:

$$\frac{1}{f} = \frac{W}{w_s \cdot \vert u \vert} + \frac{1}{\vert u \vert} = \frac{W + w_s}{w_s \cdot \vert u \vert}$$

Therefore the working distance is:
$$\boxed{\vert u \vert = \frac{f \cdot (W + w_s)}{w_s}}$$

And the field of view in terms of focal length:
$$W = w_s \left( \frac{\vert u \vert}{f} - 1 \right)$$

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $W = 50 \text{ mm}$, $w_s = 8 \text{ mm}$ (diagonal used as effective), $f = 12 \text{ mm}$, $1280 \times 1024$ pixels.

**(i) Working distance (2 marks):**
$$\vert u \vert = \frac{12 \cdot (50 + 8)}{8} = \frac{12 \cdot 58}{8} = 87 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Pixel resolution (2 marks):**
$$R_p = \frac{50 \text{ mm}}{1280} = 0.0391 \text{ mm/pixel} \approx 39 \text{ μm/pixel}$$

**(iii) Max conveyor speed for 1-pixel blur (3 marks):**
The maximum distance the egg can travel in one frame is 1 pixel = 0.0391 mm.
Assuming exposure time $t_{exp} = 1/1000 \text{ s} = 1 \text{ ms}$ (typical industrial value):

$$v_{c,\max} = \frac{R_p}{t_{exp}} = \frac{0.0391 \text{ mm}}{1 \text{ ms}} = 39.1 \text{ mm/s}$$

(Students may also solve this using the line-scan formula $f_{\text{line}} = v_c / R_p$ for a chosen frame rate; both approaches are accepted.)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU ESE Module 2, "Basics of Computer Vision"**

- ✅ **Computer vision = non-contact sensing** that automates human visual inspection on food lines.
- ✅ A CV station always has **five blocks**: Illumination → Optics → Sensor → Processing → Actuation.
- ✅ The **thin-lens equation** is $\dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{v} - \dfrac{1}{u}$ (Cartesian sign convention).
- ✅ **Magnification** $m = v/u = h'/h$ — always a dimensionless number; **never carry units** on $m$.
- ✅ **Field of View** depends on both **focal length** and **sensor size** — short lens = wide FOV, long lens = narrow FOV.
- ✅ **Pixel resolution** = FOV / number of pixels — drives the smallest detectable defect size.
- ✅ **Frame rate** must be matched to **conveyor speed** to avoid motion blur (1-pixel blur rule).
- ✅ **Line-scan cameras** are mandatory for high-speed webs (grain, sheet) — required for $\geq 1 \text{ m/s}$ lines.
- ✅ **Area-scan cameras** are sufficient for discrete item inspection (fruits, eggs, packaged goods) at lower speeds.
- ✅ **Illumination choice** is governed by **surface properties**:
  - Glossy / convex → **dome light**
  - Transparent → **backlight**
  - Specular / reflective → **coaxial / polarized light**
  - High-speed → **structured laser line**
- ✅ **Luminance** for grayscale conversion: $Y = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B$ — **memorize the weights**.
- ✅ Always quote **units explicitly** in derivations (mm, mm/s, fps, mm/pixel).
- ✅ For 14-mark questions, **draw a labelled block diagram** before writing any derivation.
- ✅ Always state the **sign convention** you are using in any lens-equation derivation.
- ✅ Industrial benchmarks to remember: high-speed sorter ≈ **4–15 kHz** line rate; typical food vision pixel resolution ≈ **30–100 μm/pixel**.

---

**End of Notes — Module 2, Topic: Basics of Computer Vision**
**PEFTT864 — Automation in Food Industry | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications in Quality Control and Inspection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT864 - AUTOMATION IN FOOD INDUSTRY > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Applications in Quality Control and Inspection ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Quality Control (QC)** in the food industry is the operational set of procedures and activities employed to fulfil requirements relating to the quality of a food product, encompassing sampling, testing, and the use of measurement systems to monitor and record the results of these activities. **Quality Inspection** is the systematic examination of a food product, its packaging, and its raw materials to determine its conformity with predefined specifications and food safety standards. In the context of **Automation in Food Industry**, QC and Inspection are executed through **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)** that combine: * **Sensors** (transducers) for data acquisition (vision, spectral, chemical, mechanical). * **Signal Processing Units** (PLCs, edge AI processors) for data conditioning. * **Actuators and Reject Mechanisms** for physical segregation of non-conforming products. * **Feedback Loops** integrated with **MES (Manufacturing Execution Systems)** for real-time lot traceability. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** This topic directly maps to the application of *Machine Vision*, *Near-Infrared (NIR) Spectroscopy*, *Hyperspectral Imaging*, and *Computer Tomography* in ensuring food safety and quality, in alignment with **FSSC 22000** and **HACCP** protocols. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Invisible Inspector") Imagine a conveyor belt at a biscuit factory carrying thousands of biscuits per minute. A human eye can inspect maybe 30-40 biscuits per minute with a fatigue-induced error rate of ~10%. Now imagine replacing that eye with a **high-speed camera coupled to a super-fast brain (an AI processor)** that can: 1. Take 5,000 images per second. 2. Measure the precise diameter, colour shade (using **L\*a\*b\* colorimetry**), surface crack density, and topping distribution of every single biscuit. 3. Instantly push an air jet to divert the imperfect ones into a rejection bin. This is **Automated Quality Inspection**. It never blinks, never gets bored, and evaluates **100% of the production**, rather than the tiny **AQL (Acceptable Quality Level)** samples used in traditional manual inspection. ### 1.3 Core Performance Metrics (Standardized in KTU Curriculum) For any automated inspection system, KTU examiners expect students to understand these four metrics. Let: * $TP$ = True Positives (Defective correctly identified as defective) * $TN$ = True Negatives (Good correctly identified as good) * $FP$ = False Positives (Good wrongly identified as defective) * $FN$ = False Negatives (Defective wrongly identified as good) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sensitivity (Recall):** $\text{Sensitivity} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ — the system's ability to *catch every defect*. Critical for food safety. > **Specificity:** $\text{Specificity} = \dfrac{TN}{TN + FP}$ — the system's ability to *not over-reject* good products. Critical for yield and profit. > **Precision:** $\text{Precision} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ > **Accuracy:** $\text{Accuracy} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ ### 1.4 Foundational Quality Standards Referenced in Food QC > [!NOTE] > **HACCP (Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Points):** A systematic preventive approach to hazard control. > **ISO 22000 / FSSC 22000:** International food safety management system standards. > **GMP (Good Manufacturing Practice):** Baseline operational requirements. > **AQL (Acceptable Quality Limit):** Statistical sampling standard (e.g., AQL 1.0 = 1% defectives allowed in a sample). > **Six Sigma Quality:** A target of **3.4 Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Four Pillars of Automated Food Quality Inspection | Pillar | Sensor / Technology | Physical Principle | Typical Food Application | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Pillar 1: Optical Vision** | RGB CMOS/CCD Cameras, 3D Laser Profilometers | Reflected visible light, structured light triangulation | Shape sorting, size grading, surface defect detection in fruits. | | **Pillar 2: Spectral Sensing** | NIR (Near-Infrared) Spectrometers, FTIR, Raman | Molecular overtone vibrations (C-H, O-H, N-H bonds) | Moisture, fat, protein, sugar content measurement in dairy/grains. | | **Pillar 3: Hyperspectral Imaging (HSI)** | Push-broom or snapshot HSI cameras | Combines spatial (imaging) + spectral (chemistry) data | Detecting faecal contamination on chicken carcasses, bruising in apples. | | **Pillar 4: Volumetric Sensing** | X-ray, CT (Computed Tomography) | Differential X-ray attenuation by density | Foreign object detection (bone in meat, glass, metal), internal insect infestation. | ### 2.2 The Generic Machine Vision Workflow (Core Concept) Every automated visual inspection follows this exact pipeline. KTU questions frequently test this sequential logic. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Step 1 — Image Acquisition:** The product is illuminated (LED ring lights, dome lights, backlights) and imaged by a sensor. The illumination is critical; wrong lighting invalidates everything downstream. > **Step 2 — Pre-processing:** Operations like **Gaussian filtering** (noise removal), **Histogram Equalization** (contrast enhancement), and **Binarization** prepare the image. > **Step 3 — Segmentation:** Isolating the **Region of Interest (ROI)** from the background using techniques like **Otsu's Thresholding** or **Edge Detection (Canny/Sobel)**. > **Step 4 — Feature Extraction:** Mathematical descriptors are computed, e.g., **Area**, **Perimeter**, **Circularity**, **Centroid**, **Mean HSL values**. > **Step 5 — Classification:** A trained AI model (**SVM, Random Forest, CNN**) maps the features to a class: "Grade A," "Grade B," or "Reject." ### 2.3 Statistical Process Control (SPC) in Food Manufacturing SPC is the mathematical backbone of automated QC. KTU expects students to derive and apply control limits. > [!NOTE] > **Concept:** A process is "in control" if its variation is due only to **Common Cause Variation** (random, inherent). "Out of control" means **Special Cause Variation** (assignable, like a faulty ingredient). SPC charts detect this. The control limits are set at **$\mu \pm 3\sigma$** because of the empirical rule (**68-95-99.7 rule**) — capturing 99.73% of all common-cause variation. The 0.27% probability of a point falling outside purely by chance is treated as a statistically significant signal of an assignable cause. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are exam-critical for the PEFTT864 Module 2 question paper.** | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Application Note | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Mean (X-double bar)** | $\bar{\bar{X}} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \bar{X}_i}{k}$ | $k$ = number of sub-groups | Grand average of sample means. | | **Average Range** | $\bar{R} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} R_i}{k}$ | $R_i$ = sub-group range | Used in R-chart. | | **X-bar Chart UCL** | $UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \cdot \bar{R}$ | $A_2$ = constant from table (for $n=5$, $A_2 = 0.577$) | Upper Control Limit for means. | | **X-bar Chart LCL** | $LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \cdot \bar{R}$ | $A_2$ = same constant | Lower Control Limit for means. | | **R-Chart UCL** | $UCL_R = D_4 \cdot \bar{R}$ | $D_4$ = constant (for $n=5$, $D_4 = 2.114$) | Upper Control Limit for range. | | **R-Chart LCL** | $LCL_R = D_3 \cdot \bar{R}$ | $D_3$ = constant (for $n \le 6$, $D_3 = 0$) | Lower Control Limit for range. | | **Process Capability Index** | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | $USL/LSL$ = Spec limits; $\sigma$ = std. dev. | Measures process spread vs tolerance. | | **Process Capability (shifted)** | $C_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | $\mu$ = process mean | Penalises off-center processes. | | **Six Sigma DPMO** | $DPMO = 10^6 \cdot \dfrac{\text{Defects}}{\text{Opportunities}}$ | Opportunities = units $\times$ defect types | Industry gold standard for "zero defect." | | **NIR Absorbance (Beer-Lambert)** | $A = \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right) = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$ | $A$ = absorbance; $I_0$ = incident; $I$ = transmitted; $\varepsilon$ = molar absorptivity; $c$ = concentration; $l$ = path length | Foundation of spectroscopic QC. | | **Machine Vision Resolution** | $\text{Res} = \dfrac{\text{FOV}}{\text{Pixels}}$ | FOV = Field of View (mm) | Smallest detectable feature size. | | **Hyperspectral Data Cube** | $I(x, y, \lambda)$ | $x,y$ = spatial; $\lambda$ = wavelength | 3D tensor: two spatial, one spectral. | | **Confusion Matrix Accuracy** | $\text{Accuracy} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Categorical outcomes | Overall classification correctness. | | **Standard Deviation (sample)** | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}{N-1}}$ | $N$ = sample size | Foundation of all SPC limits. | ### 2.5 Why This System Matters in the Food Industry (Real-World Utility) Automated QC systems address the **Three Pillars of Food Quality**: **Safety** (no pathogens, allergens, foreign objects), **Integrity** (correct weight, seal, label), and **Aesthetic/Organoleptic** quality (colour, texture, freshness). Manual inspection is limited by human physiological bottlenecks — the average human eye has a contrast sensitivity of ~2%, a temporal resolution of ~24 Hz, and suffers from cognitive fatigue. Automated systems overcome these, enabling **100% inline inspection** at line speeds exceeding **600 units per minute**, which is the standard throughput in modern beverage and bakery plants (e.g., Nestlé, Mondelez automated lines). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Why Control Limits are set at $\mu \pm 3\sigma$ (3-Sigma Rule) This is a high-frequency KTU question. The derivation is based on the **Central Limit Theorem (CLT)** and the **Empirical Rule** for a Normal Distribution. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pre-requisite:** If a quality characteristic $X$ of a food product (e.g., the weight of a 500g bread loaf) is influenced by many small, independent, random factors (flour moisture, ambient humidity, machine vibration), then by the **Central Limit Theorem**, $X$ follows a **Normal (Gaussian) Distribution**: $X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$. The **Probability Density Function (PDF)** of this distribution is: $$ f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{(x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) $$ The probability that a measurement $x$ falls within any interval $[a, b]$ is the area under the PDF: $$ P(a \le x \le b) = \int_{a}^{b} f(x) \, dx $$ **Step 1: Evaluate the integral over one standard deviation.** For $a = \mu - \sigma$ and $b = \mu + \sigma$: $$ P(\mu - \sigma \le x \le \mu + \sigma) = \int_{\mu - \sigma}^{\mu + \sigma} \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{(x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) \, dx $$ **Step 2: Apply the substitution $z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$**, so $dz = \frac{dx}{\sigma}$.
