$$F_4 = 1.07 \times 10^{-3} \times 4 = 4.28 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$F_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{4} F_i = (2.14 + 6.63 + 5.55 + 4.28) \times 10^{-3} = 18.6 \times 10^{-3}$$

---

#### Step 4 — Convert to Equivalent Shelf-Life at Reference 4 °C

The equivalent time at 4 °C that produces the same spoilage:

$$t_{eq} = \frac{F_{total}}{k_{1}} = \frac{18.6 \times 10^{-3}}{1.07 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 17.4\ \text{h}$$

---

#### Step 5 — Interpretation

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The 12 actual hours of storage consumed an amount of shelf-life equivalent to **17.4 hours at the recommended 4 °C**. The temperature excursions to 7 °C during hours 2–5 accelerated spoilage by ~45 %. This is precisely the type of insight IoT analytics delivers that manual logs never could.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: IoT Protocols Comparison

| Feature | Wi-Fi | ZigBee | BLE | LoRaWAN | NB-IoT |
|---------|-------|--------|-----|---------|--------|
| Range | 50 m | 100 m | 30 m | 10 km | 15 km |
| Data rate | 100 Mbps | 250 kbps | 2 Mbps | 50 kbps | 200 kbps |
| Power | High | Low | Very low | Very low | Medium |
| Use case | Plant floor | Cold room | Wearables | Silo/truck | National fleet |
| KTU frequency asked | Often | Often | Sometimes | Sometimes | Rare |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 IoT Architecture Block Diagram (Food Processing Context)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[FOOD ENVIRONMENT\nSilos, Tanks, Ovens, Cold Rooms, Trucks] --> B[PERCEPTION LAYER\nTemp, Humidity, pH, Gas, NIR, RFID, Load Cell]
    B --> C[EDGE GATEWAY\nRaspberry Pi / PLC / Industrial Router]
    C --> D{NETWORK LAYER\nWi-Fi / ZigBee / BLE / LoRa / 5G}
    D --> E[CLOUD PLATFORM\nAWS IoT / Azure / Google Cloud]
    E --> F[ANALYTICS ENGINE\nML, Spoilage Models, HACCP]
    F --> G[APPLICATION LAYER\nDashboard, Mobile App, Alerts, ERP]
    G --> H[ACTUATORS\nCompressor, Valve, Conveyor, Alarm]
    H --> A
    F --> I[BLOCKCHAIN LEDGER\nEnd-to-End Traceability]
    I --> J[CONSUMER QR SCAN\nOrigin, Temp Log, Cert]
```

### 4.2 Cold-Chain Monitoring Flow (Dairy Industry)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    raw[Raw Milk Collection\nat Farm 4C] --> t1[IoT Temp Logger\nBLE Enabled]
    t1 --> truck[Refrigerated Truck\nGPS + Temp]
    truck --> plant[Plant Reception Bay\nAuto Sampling]
    plant --> pasteurize[HTST Pasteurization\n72C 15s]
    pasteurize --> coldstore[Cold Store 4C\nContinuous IoT Monitoring]
    coldstore --> retail[Distribution\nQR Code Attached]
    retail --> consumer[Consumer Smartphone\nSees Full Temp History]
    coldstore -.Alert.-> mgr[Plant Manager\nSMS and Email]
    mgr -.Recall Decision.-> retail
```

### 4.3 Decision Logic for Smart Cold Room

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Sensor Reads Temp T]) --> check{T in 2C to 6C}
    check -- YES --> normal[Log Normal\nGreen Status]
    check -- NO --> warn{T above 6C}
    warn -- YES --> alert[Send SMS Alert\nStart Backup Compressor]
    warn -- NO --> critical{T above 10C}
    critical -- YES --> recall[Trigger Recall\nLock Dispatch Bay]
    critical -- NO --> monitor[Continue Monitoring\n5 min interval]
    normal --> wait[Wait 10 min]
    monitor --> wait
    alert --> wait
    recall --> wait
    wait --> start
```

### 4.4 Data Flow in IoT-Enabled Bakery Oven

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    flour[Flour Hopper\nLoad Cell Sensor] --> ctrl[Bakery PLC\nSiemens S7-1200]
    dough[Dough Feeder\nVision Camera] --> ctrl
    ctrl --> zone1[Zone 1 Temp\nTop Bottom]
    ctrl --> zone2[Zone 2 Temp\nTop Bottom]
    ctrl --> zone3[Zone 3 Temp\nTop Bottom]
    zone1 --> mqtt[MQTT Broker\nLocal Edge]
    zone2 --> mqtt
    zone3 --> mqtt
    mqtt --> cloud[AWS IoT Core]
    cloud --> ml[ML Model\nPredict Browning Index]
    ml --> dash[Baker Dashboard\nTablet and Phone]
    dash -.Manual Override.-> ctrl
    cloud --> erp[ERP MES System\nProduction Records]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define the Internet of Things (IoT). List any four sensor types commonly used in food processing IoT systems.

**Model Answer:**
The Internet of Things (IoT) is a network of physical objects embedded with sensors, software, and connectivity that enables them to collect, exchange, and act on data over the internet without human intervention. In food processing, IoT enables real-time monitoring of process and product variables.

Four sensor types:
1. **Temperature sensors** (RTD, thermocouple) — for cold chain and pasteurization.
2. **Humidity sensors** (DHT22, SHT31) — for dry storage and bakery proofing.
3. **pH sensors** — for fermentation and dairy.
4. **Gas sensors** (MQ series) — for detecting spoilage gases like ammonia, ethanol, H₂S.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
What is the role of MQTT in IoT-based food monitoring systems? Mention any two advantages.

**Model Answer:**
**MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)** is a lightweight, publish-subscribe messaging protocol designed for low-bandwidth, high-latency, or unreliable networks. It is widely used in food IoT because sensors continuously *publish* temperature/humidity data to a *broker*, and multiple clients (dashboard, alarm system, ERP) *subscribe* to relevant topics.

Two advantages:
1. **Low bandwidth consumption** — ideal for remote silos, refrigerated trucks, and rural dairy collection centres.
2. **Reliable message delivery** with three Quality-of-Service (QoS) levels, ensuring critical alerts (e.g., temperature breach) are never lost.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Describe the four-layer architecture of IoT in food processing with a suitable block diagram. Explain the function of each layer in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A frozen seafood storage is maintained at −18 °C. Due to a power failure, the temperature rises to −5 °C over 4 hours, then drops back to −18 °C over the next 2 hours. Using the Arrhenius equation with $A = 5 \times 10^{14}\ \text{h}^{-1}$, $E_a = 90\ \text{kJ/mol}$, and $R = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$, estimate the cumulative spoilage fraction and comment on whether the batch is fit for consumption (assume rejection threshold $F_{total} > 0.05$). **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The four-layer IoT architecture for food processing is:

**1. Perception Layer (Sensors and Actuators):**
This is the physical contact layer. Sensors convert physical/chemical variables (temperature, humidity, gas, pH, weight) into electrical signals. Actuators (valves, compressors, conveyors) receive control commands to alter the process.

**2. Network Layer (Communication):**
Data is transmitted using short-range (BLE, ZigBee) or long-range (LoRa, NB-IoT, 4G/5G) protocols. The MQTT protocol is preferred for low-power, high-frequency sensor data.

**3. Middleware Layer (Edge and Cloud):**
Edge gateways preprocess data locally to reduce bandwidth. The cloud performs big-data analytics, ML-based predictions, and stores historical records for traceability and regulatory audits.

**4. Application Layer:**
Provides user-facing interfaces such as dashboards, mobile apps, automated alerts, ERP integration, and consumer-facing QR traceability systems.

**Block diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the full Mermaid architecture.)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- Initial temp: $T_i = -18\ ^{\circ}C = 255.15\ K$
- Peak temp: $T_p = -5\ ^{\circ}C = 268.15\ K$
- Phase 1 (ramp up): $t_1 = 4$ h
- Phase 2 (cool down): $t_2 = 2$ h

**Average temperatures:**
- Phase 1 average: $T_1 = (-18 + -5)/2 = -11.5\ ^{\circ}C = 261.65\ K$
- Phase 2 average: $T_2 = (-5 + -18)/2 = -11.5\ ^{\circ}C = 261.65\ K$

(Same average by symmetry.)

**Step 1 — Compute k:**

$$\frac{E_a}{RT} = \frac{90{,}000}{8.314 \times 261.65} = \frac{90{,}000}{2175.4} = 41.37$$

$$k = 5 \times 10^{14} \cdot e^{-41.37} \approx 5 \times 10^{14} \times 1.06 \times 10^{-18} = 5.30 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{h}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Spoilage fraction:**

$$F_1 = k \cdot t_1 = 5.30 \times 10^{-4} \times 4 = 2.12 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$F_2 = k \cdot t_2 = 5.30 \times 10^{-4} \times 2 = 1.06 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$F_{total} = F_1 + F_2 = 3.18 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 3 — Comparison with threshold:**
$F_{total} = 0.00318 < 0.05$ → **Batch is fit for consumption.**

**Comment:** Although IoT alerts would have triggered immediate operator intervention, the integrated spoilage remains well below the rejection threshold because the excursion duration was short and the average temperature stayed low.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Explain the application of IoT in cold-chain monitoring of dairy products. Discuss at least four sensors and the data analytics used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare MQTT and CoAP protocols with respect to architecture, message size, and suitability for food IoT applications. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Cold-chain dairy monitoring** ensures milk remains within 2 °C–6 °C from farm to retail. IoT enables this without manual checks.

**Sensors used:**

1. **Digital temperature sensors (DS18B20 / RTD PT100)** — placed inside milk cans, truck tanks, and cold-room racks. Accuracy ±0.5 °C, logging every 10–60 s.
2. **Humidity sensors (DHT22)** — monitor cold-room humidity to prevent condensation on packaging.
3. **pH sensors** — inline optical pH probes detect lactic acid buildup, an early indicator of spoilage.
4. **RFID tags** — attached to each milk crate, scanned at every handover point to log location and timestamp.

**Data analytics used:**

- **Real-time threshold alerts** when temperature crosses 6 °C.
- **Time-Temperature Indicators (TTI):** cumulative spoilage modeling using Arrhenius or Bigelow models.
- **Anomaly detection** using ML (Isolation Forest, LSTM) to flag unusual patterns.
- **Predictive analytics** for compressor failure based on temperature trend.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

| Parameter | MQTT | CoAP |
|---|---|---|
| Architecture | Publish-Subscribe (broker-based) | Request-Response (client-server) |
| Transport | TCP | UDP |
| Message header | 2 bytes minimum | 4 bytes minimum |
| Typical use | Continuous telemetry, dashboards | Constrained devices, REST-like queries |
| Suitability for food IoT | Excellent for streaming temperature/humidity data from many sensors to many consumers | Good for individual sensor queries, e.g., fetching current pH from a probe |
| Power consumption | Low | Very low |
| QoS | 3 levels (0, 1, 2) | Confirmable / non-confirmable |
| KTU takeaway | Preferred for cloud dashboards | Preferred for edge device polling |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing IoT layers** — writing "cloud" under perception or "sensors" under application. Memorize the *S⁴* mnemonic strictly.
> 2. **Forgetting units in Arrhenius calculations** — Temperature *must* be in Kelvin. Examiners deduct 1 mark for every missed unit conversion.
> 3. **Mixing MQTT with HTTP** — they are at different OSI layers and serve different purposes. Don't claim "MQTT is faster than HTTP" without qualification.
> 4. **Omitting real-world examples** — KTU examiners reward industry-linked answers (Nestlé, Amul, Maersk). Mention at least one.
> 5. **Sketching diagrams without labels** — a block diagram without arrows or labels scores zero in the 7-mark design sub-question.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ IoT = **SENSE → SEND → STORE → SHOW** (S⁴ mnemonic)
- ✅ Four layers: **Perception → Network → Middleware → Application**
- ✅ Always convert **°C to Kelvin** before applying the Arrhenius equation
- ✅ **Q₁₀ rule:** microbial growth rate roughly *doubles* per 10 °C rise
- ✅ **MQTT** = lightweight, broker-based, ideal for telemetry
- ✅ **CoAP** = REST-like, UDP-based, ideal for constrained devices
- ✅ Key sensors in food IoT: **temperature, humidity, pH, gas, NIR, RFID**
- ✅ Cold-chain temperature zones: **Chilled 0–4 °C**, **Frozen ≤ −18 °C**
- ✅ Cloud platform examples: **AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub, Google Cloud IoT**
- ✅ HACCP integration: IoT automates Critical Control Point (CCP) logging
- ✅ Traceability: **RFID + blockchain + QR code** is the KTU-preferred combo
- ✅ Cybersecurity note: food IoT systems must use **TLS/DTLS** encryption
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design and Implementation of Automated Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT864 - AUTOMATION IN FOOD INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design and Implementation of Automated Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Automated System Design** in the food industry is the structured engineering process of specifying, integrating, and commissioning electromechanical, instrumentation, and software-based control architectures to execute a sequence of food-processing operations — such as mixing, pasteurization, packaging, and quality inspection — with minimal human intervention, while satisfying the constraints of **Good Manufacturing Practice (GMP)**, **Hazard Analysis Critical Control Point (HACCP)**, product safety, throughput, and regulatory compliance (FSSAI, ISO 22000, FDA 21 CFR Part 11).

An automated food processing system is a **cyber-physical system (CPS)** composed of three interacting layers:
1. **Sensing Layer** — physical/chemical sensors (temperature, pH, viscosity, vision).
2. **Control Layer** — Programmable Logic Controllers (PLC), Distributed Control Systems (DCS), Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA).
3. **Actuation / Mechanical Layer** — valves, pumps, conveyors, robotic arms, drives.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: The "Smart Kitchen Restaurant"**
> Imagine you walk into a fully automated restaurant. The moment you place an order on a tablet (the **HMI**), the kitchen receives it. Sensors in the oven (temperature probes) measure heat, a controller (the **PLC**) decides when to switch off the heating coil (the **actuator**), and a robotic arm (the **mechanical layer**) plates the dish. Meanwhile, the manager watches a screen showing every station's status — that is the **SCADA** layer. Designing such a system is exactly the job of a food-industry automation engineer: defining *what* must happen (recipes, sequences), choosing *which* instruments will sense and act, and writing *how* they coordinate.

### 1.3 Key Design Goals (KTU 2024 Course Outcome Mapping)

| Design Goal | CO Mapping | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Functional correctness | CO1 | System performs the required process steps |
| Hygienic design | CO2 | Compliance with 3-A, EHEDG standards |
| Reliability & uptime | CO3 | Achieves >95% OEE |
| Food safety integration | CO4 | HACCP CCPs monitored in real-time |
| Scalability & flexibility | CO5 | Supports new SKUs without redesign |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this topic is high-weightage in KTU ESE**: Questions from "Design and Implementation" almost always appear as 14-mark questions in the end-semester exam because they test the student's ability to *integrate* sensors, controllers, actuators, and safety into one coherent answer — a true reflection of Outcome-Based Education (OBE).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Structured Design Methodology (V-Model for Automation)

The **V-Model** is the industry-standard for designing automated systems. It maps each design phase on the left to a corresponding validation phase on the right.

**Left Arm (Decomposition):**
1. **User Requirements Specification (URS)** — *What does the user need?*
2. **Functional Specification (FS)** — *What functions must the system perform?*
3. **Design Specification (DS)** — *How will the functions be realized?*
4. **Hardware/Software Detailed Design** — *Component selection, ladder/P&ID drafting.*

**Right Arm (Verification & Validation):**
1. **Module Testing (FAT)** — Factory Acceptance Test at vendor site.
2. **Integration Testing (SAT)** — Site Acceptance Test at customer plant.
3. **System Validation (IQ/OQ/PQ)** — Installation, Operational, Performance Qualification.
4. **Process Validation** — Confirmed against URS.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight**: When asked to "design an automated system for X", examiners expect the answer to *follow* the V-Model or a similar waterfall model — never a haphazard listing of components.

### 2.2 Component Selection Logic

The designer must answer four sub-questions for every subsystem:
- **WHAT** to sense? (pH, T, P, level, flow, vision)
- **WHICH** sensor? (RTD vs. thermocouple; capacitive vs. ultrasonic level)
- **HOW** to act? (pneumatic, hydraulic, electric, stepper, servo)
- **WHO** coordinates? (PLC for discrete; DCS for continuous; SCADA for supervision)

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following metrics are **must-know** for any KTU numerical or descriptive question on automation design.

$$
\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q
$$

$$
A = \frac{\text{Planned Production Time} - \text{Downtime}}{\text{Planned Production Time}}
$$

$$
P = \frac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces Produced}}{\text{Operating Time}}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces Produced}}
$$

$$
\text{ROI (\%)} = \frac{\text{Net Annual Benefit}}{\text{Total Investment Cost}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Payback Period (years)} = \frac{\text{Total Investment}}{\text{Annual Cash Inflow}}
$$

$$
\text{MTBF} = \frac{\text{Total Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}}
$$

$$
\text{Availability} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}
$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Food-Industry Target |
|---|---|---|
| OEE | Overall Equipment Effectiveness | **>85% (World Class)** |
| A | Availability | >90% |
| P | Performance | >95% |
| Q | Quality | >99% |
| MTBF | Mean Time Between Failures | **>500 h** |
| MTTR | Mean Time To Repair | <1 h |
| ROI | Return on Investment | >20% in 3 yrs |

**Engineering Utility**: These formulas let the automation engineer justify a CAPEX (Capital Expenditure) request to management using quantitative metrics. KTU examiners frequently test OEE and ROI because they bridge mechanical, instrumentation, and managerial economics.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Example 1 — OEE Calculation for a Milk Bottling Line

**Given Data:**
- Planned Production Time = 480 min/day
- Downtime = 30 min/day
- Ideal Cycle Time = 1.2 sec/bottle
- Total Pieces Produced = 22,000 bottles
- Good Pieces = 21,560 bottles

**Step 1 — Compute Operating Time**

$$
\text{Operating Time} = \text{Planned Production Time} - \text{Downtime}
$$

$$
\text{Operating Time} = 480 - 30 = 450 \text{ min} = 27{,}000 \text{ sec}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Availability (A)**

$$
A = \frac{480 - 30}{480} = \frac{450}{480} = 0.9375 \quad \text{(93.75%)}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Performance (P)**

$$
P = \frac{1.2 \times 22{,}000}{27{,}000} = \frac{26{,}400}{27{,}000} = 0.9778 \quad \text{(97.78%)}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Quality (Q)**

$$
Q = \frac{21{,}560}{22{,}000} = 0.9800 \quad \text{(98.00%)}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute OEE**

$$
\text{OEE} = 0.9375 \times 0.9778 \times 0.9800 = 0.8982 \quad \text{(89.82%)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation**: An OEE of **89.82%** is approaching World Class. The bottleneck is **Availability** (downtime losses). The automation design recommendation would be to install a **predictive-maintenance sensor suite** (vibration + current monitoring on conveyors and filler motors) to eliminate unplanned stoppages.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 2 — ROI for a Pasteurizer Automation Retrofit

**Given Data:**
- New PLC + SCADA + sensors cost = ₹ 18,00,000
- Installation and validation cost = ₹ 4,00,000
- Annual labour saving = ₹ 6,00,000
- Annual quality-rejection cost reduction = ₹ 3,00,000
- Annual maintenance saving = ₹ 1,00,000

**Step 1 — Total Investment**

$$
\text{Investment} = 18{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 = 22{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
$$

**Step 2 — Net Annual Benefit**

$$
\text{Net Benefit} = 6{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 = 10{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR/yr}
$$

**Step 3 — ROI**

$$
\text{ROI (\%)} = \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{22{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 45.45\%
$$

**Step 4 — Payback Period**

$$
\text{Payback} = \frac{22{,}00{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000} = 2.2 \text{ years}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision Rule (KTU industry standard)**: A food-industry automation project is considered **economically viable** if **ROI > 20%** AND **Payback < 5 years**. This project clears both hurdles comfortably.

---

### 3.3 Design Selection Matrix (Analytical Tool)

The table below is the standard "Pugh Matrix" used by automation designers to choose between competing architectures.

| Selection Criterion | Weight | Conventional Relay Logic | PLC-Based | DCS-Based | SCADA + Edge AI |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Initial Cost | 0.15 | +2 | +1 | -2 | -2 |
| Flexibility / Re-programmability | 0.20 | -3 | +2 | +2 | +3 |
| HACCP Data Logging | 0.25 | -3 | 0 | +2 | +3 |
| Scalability | 0.15 | -2 | +1 | +2 | +3 |
| Ease of Validation (IQ/OQ/PQ) | 0.15 | -1 | +2 | +1 | 0 |
| Mean Score | 1.00 | **-1.45** | **+1.10** | **+0.95** | **+1.45** |

*Scoring convention: +3 best, -3 worst.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineer's Interpretation**: For a high-throughput food plant requiring **HACCP data logging**, the SCADA + Edge AI architecture wins. For a small-scale dairy, the PLC alone is the cost-effective choice. This matrix frequently appears in 14-mark KTU questions.

---

### 3.4 Functional Flow of a Typical Automated Batch Pasteurizer

1. **Raw milk** enters via inlet valve V1 (open on START + level sensor L1 HIGH).
2. **Flow meter (F1)** measures volume → sent to PLC.
3. **Heater** raises T from 4 °C to 72 °C using a PID loop driven by **RTD Pt100** sensor.
4. **Holding tube** maintains 72 °C for 15 s (computed via flow & tube volume).
5. **Outlet valve V2** opens to packaging tank if T > 72 °C AND holding time met.
6. **CIP (Clean-In-Place)** sequence runs automatically after batch — controlled by dedicated PLC subroutine.
7. **SCADA** logs T, flow, pH every 5 s to a SQL database with **21 CFR Part 11**-compliant electronic signatures.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — The Design & Implementation Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[URS User Requirements] --> B0[FS Functional Spec]
    B0 --> C0[DS Design Spec]
    C0 --> D0[Hardware Selection]
    C0 --> E0[Software PLC Code]
    D0 --> F0[Panel Wiring FAT]
    E0 --> F0
    F0 --> G0[SAT Site Acceptance]
    G0 --> H0[IQ Installation Qualif]
    H0 --> I0[OQ Operational Qualif]
    I0 --> J0[PQ Performance Qualif]
    J0 --> K0[Production Handover]
    K0 --> L0[Continuous Improvement OEE Tracking]
    L0 --> A0
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Automation Architecture Layers

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph L1[Level 1 Field Layer]
      S1[Temp Sensor Pt100]
      S2[Flow Meter]
      S3[pH Probe]
      S4[Vision Camera]
    end

    subgraph L2[Level 2 Control Layer]
      PLC1[PLC Siemens S7 1500]
      DCS1[DCS for Continuous]
      VFD1[Variable Freq Drive]
    end

    subgraph L3[Level 3 Supervisory Layer]
      SCADA1[SCADA WinCC]
      HMI1[HMI Touchscreen]
      DB1[SQL Historian DB]
    end

    subgraph L4[Level 4 Enterprise Layer]
      ERP1[ERP SAP]
      MES1[MES]
    end

    S1 --> PLC1
    S2 --> PLC1
    S3 --> PLC1
    S4 --> SCADA1
    PLC1 --> VFD1
    PLC1 --> SCADA1
    DCS1 --> SCADA1
    SCADA1 --> HMI1
    SCADA1 --> DB1
    DB1 --> MES1
    MES1 --> ERP1
```

### 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — PLC Sequential Control for CIP

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> State1Idle
    State1Idle --> State2PreRinse : START pressed
    State2PreRinse --> State3Caustic : level high
    State3Caustic --> State4Rinse1 : timer 20 min done
    State4Rinse1 --> State5Acid : conductivity OK
    State5Acid --> State6FinalRinse : timer 15 min done
    State6FinalRinse --> State7Sterile : conductivity low
    State7Sterile --> State1Idle : operator OK
    State3Caustic --> State9Alarm : T exceeds 85 C
    State9Alarm --> State1Idle
```

### 4.4 Hierarchical Function Decomposition for an Automated Bakery Line

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SYS0[Automated Bakery System]
    SYS0 --> SUB1[Dough Preparation]
    SYS0 --> SUB2[Proofing Oven]
    SYS0 --> SUB3[Cooling Conveyor]
    SYS0 --> SUB4[Packaging Robot]

    SUB1 --> TASK1A[Flour Hopper with Load Cell]
    SUB1 --> TASK1B[Water Dosing Pump]
    SUB1 --> TASK1C[Mixer with VFD]

    SUB2 --> TASK2A[Temperature PID]
    SUB2 --> TASK2B[Humidity Sensor]
    SUB2 --> TASK2C[Conveyor Speed]

    SUB3 --> TASK3A[Fan Speed Control]
    SUB3 --> TASK3B[IR Temp Scanner]

    SUB4 --> TASK4A[Pick and Place Arm]
    SUB4 --> TASK4B[Vision QC]
    SUB4 --> TASK4C[Date Coding Inkjet]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q1]**
> **Q1.** Define OEE and state its three components.
>
> **Model Answer:**
> OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness) is a metric used in the food industry to quantify the *true* productive effectiveness of an automated manufacturing system.
>
> It is computed as:
>
> $$
> \text{OEE} = \text{Availability} \times \text{Performance} \times \text{Quality}
> $$
>
> - **Availability (A)** — captures losses due to unplanned downtime and setup.
> - **Performance (P)** — captures losses due to reduced speed and minor stoppages.
> - **Quality (Q)** — captures losses due to rejected product and rework.
>
> World-class OEE benchmark in food industry is **>85%**.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q2]**
> **Q2.** Differentiate between PLC, DCS, and SCADA.
>
> **Model Answer:**
>
> | Feature | PLC | DCS | SCADA |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | Primary Use | Discrete & sequential control | Continuous process control | Supervisory monitoring |
> | Architecture | Standalone or networked | Distributed controllers | PC-based HMI software |
> | Redundancy | Optional | **Built-in** | Software level only |
> | Typical Food Use | Packaging, sorting | Pasteurizer, fermenter | Plant-wide dashboard |
> | Data Historian | Limited | Advanced | **Yes** |
>
> In practice, a modern food plant uses **all three** in an integrated hierarchy (ISA-95 model).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Dec 2023]**

### ✦ Question A — 14 Marks (Choice 1)

**Design and explain an automated PLC-based system for HTST pasteurization of milk. Include the URS, sensor/actuator selection, control loop, and validation steps.**

#### (a) User Requirements & Functional Specification (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**URS:**
- Process 5000 L/h of raw milk.
- Pasteurize at **72 °C for 15 s** with ±1 °C accuracy.
- Auto CIP after every batch.
- Log all CCP data for FSSAI compliance.
- Alarm on T > 75 °C or flow failure.

**FS breakdown:**
1. **Inlet Section** — Flow control via VFD pump; level switch in balance tank.
2. **Heater Section** — PID control of plate heat exchanger using steam valve.
3. **Holding Tube** — Time computed from flow rate and tube volume.
4. **Outlet Section** — Divert-to-waste if T < 72 °C using 3-way valve.
5. **CIP Section** — Sequential caustic + acid + rinse.

**Sensor & Actuator Selection Table:**

| Function | Sensor / Actuator | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | RTD Pt100 Class A | High accuracy (±0.15 °C), hygienic Tri-clamp |
| Flow | Electromagnetic flowmeter | Sanitary, no moving parts |
| Level | Capacitive probe | Works with milk foam |
| Steam valve | Pneumatic modulating | Fast response for PID |
| Diversion valve | 3-way pneumatic | Failsafe spring-return |
| Controller | Siemens S7-1500 PLC | Common in dairy industry |

#### (b) Control Loop, Software, and Validation (7 Marks)

**Control Loop Architecture:**
- **Primary loop (T)** — PLC reads Pt100 → compares with setpoint 72 °C → PID output to steam control valve.
- **Secondary loop (Flow)** — VFD drives product pump; flow signal feeds forward to bias the T-PID.
- **Safety loop** — Independent safety PLC (SIL-2) trips steam on over-temperature.

**PLC Program Structure (Ladder Logic Overview):**
- OB1 — Main cyclic scan
- FC10 — PID_Temp block
- FC20 — FlowCalc
- FC30 — CIP_Sequencer (state machine as in Section 4.3)
- DB100 — Recipe storage (UHT, HTST, ESL)
- HMI Tags — Tags_DB

**Validation Sequence:**
1. **IQ (Installation Qualification)** — Verify sensor calibration certificates, wiring per loop diagram.
2. **OQ (Operational Qualification)** — Step-response tests on each loop; challenge alarms.
3. **PQ (Performance Qualification)** — Run 3 consecutive batches at 72 °C ±1 °C, verify phosphatase test negative.

**Expected OEE after commissioning:** 88–92% based on Section 3.1 example.

---

### ✦ Question B — 14 Marks (Choice 2)

**Explain in detail the design methodology for an automated food packaging line using the V-Model. Discuss the components of OEE and compute OEE from given data.**

#### (a) V-Model & Design Methodology (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The **V-Model** ensures every design level has a matching test level. For an automated packaging line:

| Left Arm (Design) | Right Arm (Validation) |
|---|---|
| URS — 5000 packs/h, 200 g each | Process Validation against URS |
| FS — Fill, seal, label, case-pack | System Validation IQ/OQ/PQ |
| DS — Architecture, P&ID, network | Integration Testing (SAT) |
| Detailed — Panel drawing, PLC code | Module Testing (FAT) |

**Implementation Steps:**
1. Concept & feasibility (ROI > 20% target).
2. URS drafted with Quality, Production, Maintenance inputs.
3. P&ID developed → I/O list generated → PLC hardware sized.
4. Panel fabricated, FAT performed at vendor.
5. Site installation, SAT, IQ/OQ/PQ performed.
6. Production handover with operator training.
7. Continuous improvement via OEE tracking dashboards.

#### (b) OEE Computation Problem (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Planned Production Time = 600 min
- Downtime = 45 min
- Ideal Cycle Time = 2 sec/pack
- Total Packs = 16,500
- Good Packs = 16,170

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Operating Time**
$$
\text{Operating Time} = 600 - 45 = 555 \text{ min} = 33{,}300 \text{ sec}
$$

**Step 2 — Availability**
$$
A = \frac{600 - 45}{600} = \frac{555}{600} = 0.9250
$$

**Step 3 — Performance**
$$
P = \frac{2 \times 16{,}500}{33{,}300} = \frac{33{,}000}{33{,}300} = 0.9910
$$

**Step 4 — Quality**
$$
Q = \frac{16{,}170}{16{,}500} = 0.9800
$$

**Step 5 — OEE**
$$
\text{OEE} = 0.9250 \times 0.9910 \times 0.9800 = 0.8984 = 89.84\%
$$

**Interpretation:** Availability is the weakest link. Recommendation: install **predictive maintenance vibration sensors** on the form-fill-seal machine.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in 14-mark "Design" Answers — Where students lose marks:**
>
> 1. **Skipping URS/FS** — Students directly list PLC and sensors. KTU rubric awards 2–3 marks specifically for URS. **Always** start with a numbered URS list.
> 2. **No justification for sensor choice** — Writing "use temperature sensor" loses marks; "RTD Pt100 Class A in hygienic Tri-clamp fitting because ±0.15 °C accuracy is required" earns full credit.
> 3. **Missing safety loop** — A 14-mark design answer without an independent SIL-rated safety instrumented function is considered *incomplete* and loses 2 marks.
> 4. **OEE numerical errors** — Forgetting to convert minutes to seconds in the Performance formula. Always state units explicitly.
> 5. **No validation discussion** — IQ/OQ/PQ is mandatory in any food-industry automation answer; omitting it loses 2 marks.
> 6. **Ignoring hygienic design** — Food automation is *not* general automation; 3-A / EHEDG hygienic standards are expected knowledge.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Module 3: Design and Implementation of Automated Systems**

- ☐ **V-Model** is the *de facto* methodology — memorize the 4 left-arm and 4 right-arm stages.
- ☐ **Three automation layers** — Sensing (field), Control (PLC/DCS), Supervisory (SCADA/HMI).
- ☐ **OEE = A × P × Q**; World-class threshold is **>85%**.
- ☐ **Availability formula** uses *Planned* time; Performance uses *Ideal Cycle Time* (must be in **seconds** consistently).
- ☐ **Quality** uses *Good Pieces / Total Pieces*, not rejects only.
- ☐ **ROI > 20%** and **Payback < 5 years** = project economically justified.
- ☐ **MTBF & MTTR** define reliability; Availability = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR).
- ☐ **HTST pasteurization** is 72 °C / 15 s — most common 14-mark question topic.
- ☐ **Sensor hygiene**: Tri-clamp fittings, CIP compatibility, FDA-approved elastomers.
- ☐ **Control hierarchy**: ISA-95 / Purdue model — Field → Control → Supervisory → MES → ERP.
- ☐ **Validation trilogy**: IQ (installed correctly) → OQ (operates as designed) → PQ (produces acceptable product).
- ☐ **Safety**: Independent SIL-rated trip loop is mandatory for steam, refrigeration, and ammonia systems.
- ☐ **21 CFR Part 11** = electronic records & signatures compliance for export-oriented plants.
- ☐ **Network protocols**: Profinet/Modbus TCP for PLC; OPC UA for SCADA-MES integration.
- ☐ **Always** convert time units before substituting into OEE formulas — examiners test this.
- ☐ **Always** include a sketch or Mermaid-style diagram — visually-marked answers earn +1 to +2 grace marks in KTU valuation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: System Design Principles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT864 - AUTOMATION IN FOOD INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** System Design Principles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **System Design Principles in Food Industry Automation** refer to the disciplined set of engineering methodologies, hierarchical control philosophies, modular architectures, and regulatory-compliant frameworks used to specify, integrate, validate, and commission automated process systems across the food manufacturing value chain — from raw material intake to packaged product dispatch.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)** curriculum for *PEFTT864 – Automation in Food Industry*, *System Design Principles* constitute the foundational Module-3 learning block that bridges instrumentation theory (Module-2) with real plant-floor implementation (Module-4). The subject covers the architectural blueprints by which a **SCADA/PLC/DCS-based control solution** is engineered to satisfy the **food industry’s unique drivers**: hygienic operation, traceability, regulatory compliance (**FSSAI, ISO 22000, FDA 21 CFR Part 11, GAMP 5, IEC 61508**), and product-quality consistency.

### 1.2 Key Sub-Topics Inside "System Design Principles"

The topic is conventionally sub-divided into the following sub-modules:

1. **Hierarchical Control Models** — ISA-95 / Purdue Reference Model (Levels 0 → 4).
2. **Functional Specification & Design Documents** — FDS, P&ID, PFD, I/O lists, loop diagrams.
3. **Design Philosophies** — Modular, Scalable, Redundant, Fail-Safe, Sanitary.
4. **System Architecture Selection** — PLC vs. DCS vs. SCADA vs. Hybrid.
5. **HMI / MMI Design Principles** — ISA-101 High-Performance HMI guidelines.
6. **Communication & Network Design** — Fieldbus, Industrial Ethernet, OPC UA.
7. **Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS)** — SIL classification, IEC 61511.
8. **Hygienic & Sanitary Design Integration** — EHEDG, 3-A Sanitary Standards.
9. **Validation Lifecycle** — V-Model, FAT, SAT, IQ, OQ, PQ (GAMP 5).

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — The "Smart Kitchen Restaurant"**
> Think of designing a fully-automated food plant as designing a *robotic, hyper-hygienic restaurant kitchen*:
>
> - The **physical cooking vessels, conveyors, tanks, and pumps** are the *Field Devices* (analogous to pots, pans, and burners).
> - The **sensors** (temperature, flow, level, pH) are the *thermometers and timers* monitoring each station.
> - The **PLCs** are the *head chefs* running individual stations (frying, boiling, packaging) — fast, deterministic, and repetitive.
> - The **SCADA system** is the *restaurant manager* sitting on top, watching all stations on big screens, recording every order, and generating daily reports.
> - The **DCS** is the *centralized head office* coordinating multiple kitchens, with shared recipes, ingredients, and quality audits.
> - The **ERP / MES layer** is the *headquarters finance and supply-chain system* that decides how much raw material to procure, schedules production, and dispatches the final trucks.
> - The **Safety Interlock System** is the *fire-suppression and gas-shutoff system* — it does NOT cook; it just stops everything when something is wrong.
> - The **Hygienic Design** is the rule that every pot, surface, and pipe must be **stainless steel (SS 304/316)** with no crevices — the same logic EHEDG applies to food-contact equipment.

Just as a *good restaurant designer* balances cooking speed, food safety, staff ergonomics, and cost — a *good food-automation system designer* balances throughput, product quality, sanitary compliance, operator usability, and capital cost.

---

## 1.4 Core Vocabulary Used by KTU Examiners

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize these KTU-favoured terms (they appear verbatim in board papers):**
> - **P&ID** – Piping & Instrumentation Diagram
> - **PLC** – Programmable Logic Controller
> - **SCADA** – Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition
> - **DCS** – Distributed Control System
> - **HMI** – Human-Machine Interface
> - **SIS** – Safety Instrumented System
> - **SIL** – Safety Integrity Level (1 → 4)
> - **FDS** – Functional Design Specification
> - **GAMP 5** – Good Automated Manufacturing Practice (Category 1 → 5)
> - **PAT** – Process Analytical Technology (FDA initiative)
> - **CIP** – Cleaning-In-Place
> - **EHEDG** – European Hygienic Engineering & Design Group

---

## 1.5 Standard Metrics a KTU Student Must Know

- **Response time of a control loop:** typically **< 1 second** for critical loops (e.g., pasteurization).
- **HMI refresh rate:** **250 ms – 1 s** as per ISA-101.
- **Acceptable SIS PFD (Probability of Failure on Demand):** SIL-1 → 10⁻¹ to 10⁻², SIL-4 → 10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁵.
- **Sanitary surface roughness (Ra):** **Ra ≤ 0.8 µm** for food-contact surfaces (3-A / EHEDG).
- **PLC scan time:** **5 – 20 ms** for discrete, **20 – 100 ms** for process loops.
- **Standard plant availability target:** **> 95 %** (with **OEE > 85 %** as world-class benchmark).
- **Validation documentation depth:** typically **5–8 % of total project cost** in regulated food/pharma plants.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Purdue / ISA-95 Reference Model — *The Backbone of Every KTU Question*

The **Purdue Reference Model** (now formalized as **ANSI/ISA-95**) divides a manufacturing enterprise into **six hierarchical levels**. Every KTU question on "system design" essentially tests the student's ability to identify *which function belongs to which level*.

| Level | Name | Typical Devices / Functions | KTU Example in Food Industry |
|------|------|------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| **L0** | Process (Physical) | Sensors, valves, motors, pumps, tanks | RTD in milk pasteurizer, butterfly valve on CIP line |
| **L1** | Basic Control (PLC / DCS Controller) | PID loops, interlocks, batch logic | PLC controlling pasteurization temperature at 72 °C / 15 s |
| **L2** | Supervisory (SCADA / HMI) | Operator screens, alarms, trending | SCADA mimic of a dairy evaporator showing Brix trend |
| **L3** | Manufacturing Operations (MES) | Recipe management, batch records, OEE | MES tracking each cheese batch's start/end time, yield |
| **L4** | Business Planning (ERP) | Procurement, sales, finance | SAP forecasting weekly demand for orange juice |
| **L5** | Enterprise Network | Internet, vendor portals | Cloud dashboard for remote maintenance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Conduit / 5½-Level" rule:** KTU papers often draw a **clear boundary** between L2 and L3 called the **"Manufacturing Conduit"**. *Real-time control traffic (L0–L2) must NEVER traverse the corporate LAN (L4).* This separation is a **board-favourite conceptual question**.

---

## 2.2 The V-Model for Automation System Design (GAMP 5)

The **V-Model** is the de-facto design-then-validate lifecycle used in regulated food/pharma plants. It is *symmetrical*: each design step on the left has a corresponding test on the right.

**Left arm (Specification → Design):**
1. User Requirement Specification (URS)
2. Functional Specification (FS)
3. Design Specification (DS) — hardware & software
4. Module / Code Configuration
5. **Coding ends at the bottom of the V**

**Right arm (Verification → Validation):**
1. Module Testing
2. **Factory Acceptance Test (FAT)**
3. **Site Acceptance Test (SAT)**
4. **Installation Qualification (IQ)**
5. **Operational Qualification (OQ)**
6. **Performance Qualification (PQ)** ← proves the *product* meets spec

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trap:** Students frequently confuse **OQ with PQ**. Rule of thumb:
> - **OQ** = Does the *system* do what the *Functional Spec* says? (Software & hardware-focused.)
> - **PQ** = Does the system produce *food that meets FSSAI specs*? (Product-focused, uses real raw material.)

---

## 2.3 PLC vs. SCADA vs. DCS — Selection Decision Table

This is one of the **highest-weightage 14-mark KTU questions**. The selection is driven by **plant size, complexity, and redundancy need**.

| Parameter | PLC-Based System | SCADA System | DCS |
|-----------|------------------|--------------|-----|
| **Architecture** | Single / networked PLCs | Centralized supervisory software | Distributed controllers + operator stations |
| **Best For** | Discrete, machine-level (packaging line) | Multi-site monitoring (cold chain) | Continuous, integrated process (refinery, dairy) |
| **Redundancy** | Optional, often single | Mostly in servers | **Hot-redundant controllers standard** |
| **Speed / Determinism** | **Highest** (ms scan) | Lower (s-dependent) | Medium (loop-specific) |
| **HMI** | Local HMI panel | Multi-screen, multi-user | Engineered consoles (per ISA-101) |
| **Typical Food Plant Use** | Bottle-filling, check-weighing, labeling | Cold-storage, fleet tracking | **Dairy, brewing, sugar, edible-oil refineries** |
| **CAPEX** | Low | Medium | **High** |
| **KTU Position** | Module 2 / 3 | Module 3 / 4 | Module 3 / 4 |

---

## 2.4 Safety Integrity Level (SIL) — Numerical Foundation

The **IEC 61508 / IEC 61511** standards define a Safety Integrity Level based on the **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** of the safety function.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PFDavg is the average probability that a safety function fails to operate when demanded.** Lower PFDavg ⇒ higher SIL ⇒ higher safety.

### 2.4.1 SIL Probability Table (Kerala Board Favourite)

| SIL | PFDavg Range (Low-Demand Mode) | RRF (Risk Reduction Factor) | Typical Food-Industry Application |
|------|--------------------------------|------------------------------|------------------------------------|
| **No SIL** | ≥ 10⁻¹ to < 1 | 1 to 10 | Conveyor start/stop, cosmetic interlocks |
| **SIL 1** | ≥ 10⁻² to < 10⁻¹ | 10 to 100 | Pasteurizer over-temperature cut-out |
| **SIL 2** | ≥ 10⁻³ to < 10⁻² | 100 to 1000 | Ammonia refrigeration shut-off on leak |
| **SIL 3** | ≥ 10⁻⁴ to < 10⁻³ | 1 000 to 10 000 | Sterilizer over-pressure rupture disc + interlock |
| **SIL 4** | ≥ 10⁻⁵ to < 10⁻⁴ | 10 000 to 100 000 | **Rare in food industry**; nuclear/aviation territory |

### 2.4.2 The SIL Determination Formula (KTU Formula Sheet Star)

The classical **risk-graph / safety-layer** method reduces to:

$$ \text{SIL Required} = f\!\left( \text{Severity},\ \text{Frequency},\ \text{Probability of Avoidance},\ \text{Probability of Occurrence} \right) $$

A more quantitative KTU-friendly form is the **Hazard Frequency × Consequence → SIL** lookup. The probability of dangerous failure per hour for **high-demand / continuous mode** is given by **PFH (Probability of Failure per Hour)**:

$$ \text{PFH} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \lambda_{DU,i} $$

where $\lambda_{DU,i}$ is the dangerous-undetected failure rate of the *i*-th component in the safety path.

| SIL (High-Demand) | PFH (per hour) |
|--------------------|-----------------|
| SIL 1 | $\geq 10^{-6}\ \text{to}\ < 10^{-5}$ |
| SIL 2 | $\geq 10^{-7}\ \text{to}\ < 10^{-6}$ |
| SIL 3 | $\geq 10^{-8}\ \text{to}\ < 10^{-7}$ |
| SIL 4 | $\geq 10^{-9}\ \text{to}\ < 10^{-8}$ |

---

## 2.5 Availability, MTBF, MTTR & OEE — Reliability Mathematics

> [!NOTE]
> KTU questions often ask: *"A packaging line has MTBF = 500 h and MTTR = 5 h. Calculate availability."*

### 2.5.1 Availability Formula

$$ A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} $$

where:
- **MTBF** = Mean Time Between Failures (h)
- **MTTR** = Mean Time To Repair (h)
- **A** = steady-state availability (0 < A < 1, often expressed as a **percentage**).

### 2.5.2 Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)

The food-industry gold standard for plant productivity (**Six Sigma / TPM framework**):

$$ \text{OEE} = \text{Availability} \times \text{Performance} \times \text{Quality} $$

with:

$$ \text{Availability} = \dfrac{\text{Planned Production Time} - \text{Downtime}}{\text{Planned Production Time}} $$

$$ \text{Performance} = \dfrac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces Produced}}{\text{Operating Time}} $$

$$ \text{Quality} = \dfrac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces Produced}} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **World-class OEE = 85 %** (industry benchmark in dairy & beverage plants). Below **60 %** ⇒ immediate capex loss; above **85 %** ⇒ TPM / Lean practitioner.

### 2.5.3 Unavailability and PFD Relationship

For low-demand mode:

$$ \text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = 1 - A \approx \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2} $$

where:
- $\lambda_{DU}$ = dangerous-undetected failure rate (per hour)
- $T_{proof}$ = proof-test interval (h)

This is *the* formula KTU examiners love for **SIS design** questions.

---

## 2.6 Control-Loop Bandwidth & Sampling Theorem

A KTU-favourite. For a digital (PLC) implementation of a continuous PID loop:

$$ f_{s} \geq 10 \times f_{c} \quad \text{(rule of thumb)} $$

where $f_{s}$ = sampling frequency (Hz), $f_{c}$ = closed-loop bandwidth (Hz). The Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem states the strict mathematical lower bound:

$$ f_{s} \geq 2 \times f_{\max} $$

Practical experience in **food process loops** (e.g., milk pasteurization with $\tau \approx 30$ s):

$$ f_{c} \approx \dfrac{1}{2\pi\tau} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; f_{c} \approx 5.3 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{Hz} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; T_{c} \approx 188\ \text{s} $$

The PLC scan time of 50 ms is therefore **4000× faster than loop time-constant** → discretization error is negligible. This is *why PLCs can control slow food processes comfortably*.

---

## 2.7 Sanitary Design — Quantitative Constraints

| Parameter | Threshold | Standard |
|-----------|-----------|----------|
| Surface roughness | **Ra ≤ 0.8 µm** | EHEDG Doc. 2, 3-A 63-03 |
| Slope for self-draining | **≥ 1 : 100 (1 %)** | EHEDG |
| Butt-weld vs. socket-weld | Butt only (no crevice) | ASME BPE |
| Dead-leg length | **L ≤ 2 × D** (branch) | EHEDG |
| Material | **SS 316L** (product contact) | 3-A, FDA |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The *2-D rule* is a **board-exam guarantee** — it is the maximum permissible dead-leg length to prevent microbial harborage. Students who forget this lose 2–3 marks instantly.

---

## 2.8 Communication-Bus Decision Matrix

| Bus | Speed | Distance | Food Industry Use |
|-----|-------|----------|--------------------|
| 4–20 mA + HART | 1.2 kbps | ≤ 3 km | Legacy field instruments |
| **Profibus PA** | 31.25 kbps | ≤ 1.9 km | Process automation (dairy, brewing) |
| **Foundation Fieldbus** | 31.25 kbps | ≤ 1.9 km | Same as Profibus PA |
| **Profibus DP** | 12 Mbps | ≤ 100 m | PLC ↔ remote I/O |
| **Profinet** | 100 Mbps | ≤ 100 m | Modern Ethernet-based |
| **EtherNet/IP** | 100 Mbps | ≤ 100 m | Rockwell / Packaging Machinery |
| **OPC UA** | 100 Mbps+ | Enterprise-wide | L2 ↔ L3/L4 data exchange |
| **IO-Link** | 230 kbps | ≤ 20 m | Smart sensor point-to-point |

> [!NOTE]
> The **2024 trend (and a KTU expected question)** is **OPC UA over TSN (Time-Sensitive Networking)** — it unifies fieldbus and IT on a single deterministic Ethernet. Worth 3–5 marks in any modern KTU paper.

---

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles covered above are used by **every food-plant EPC contractor** worldwide — Tetra Pak, GEA, SPX, Krones, Alfa Laval — and are codified by **FSSAI, ISO 22000, BRCGS Issue 9, SQF Edition 9, and FSSC 22000 v6**. A KTU student who masters these principles can immediately work as a:
- **Control & Instrumentation (C&I) Engineer** in a dairy / brewery / bakery plant.
- **SCADA / PLC Programmer** in a food packaging line.
- **Validation Engineer** in a FSSAI-audited plant.
- **Functional Safety Engineer** for ammonia refrigeration.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section presents **exhaustive, line-by-line derivations** of every formula you must know for a KTU 14-mark numerical on System Design. **No steps are skipped.**

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Availability from MTBF and MTTR

### Statement
A **bottling line in a beverage plant** has:
- Mean Time Between Failures, MTBF = **600 h**
- Mean Time To Repair, MTTR = **4 h**

Determine: (i) steady-state availability, (ii) annual downtime, (iii) OEE if performance and quality are 0.95 and 0.98 respectively.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Write the availability definition.**
Availability is the fraction of time the system is *operational* versus the *total time*:

$$ A = \dfrac{\text{Time Operational}}{\text{Time Operational} + \text{Time in Repair}} $$

**Step 2 — Substitute MTBF and MTTR.**
By definition, *MTBF = average operational time between failures*, and *MTTR = average time spent in repair*. Therefore:

$$ A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} $$

**Step 3 — Plug the numerical values.**

$$ A = \dfrac{600}{600 + 4} = \dfrac{600}{604} $$

**Step 4 — Compute the ratio.**

$$ A = 0.993377\ \ldots \approx 0.9934 $$

Expressed as a percentage:

$$ A\ \% \ = 99.34\ \% $$

**Step 5 — Compute annual downtime (in hours).**

$$ \text{Annual time} = 365 \times 24 = 8760\ \text{h} $$

$$ \text{Downtime per year} = 8760 \times (1 - A) = 8760 \times (1 - 0.9934) $$

$$ \text{Downtime per year} = 8760 \times 0.006623 = 58.0\ \text{h/year} $$

**Step 6 — Compute the OEE using the given performance and quality factors.**

$$ \text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q = 0.9934 \times 0.95 \times 0.98 $$

$$ \text{OEE} = 0.9934 \times 0.9310 = 0.9248 $$

$$ \text{OEE}\ \% = 92.48\ \% $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A 92.5 % OEE is **world-class** (world-class benchmark = 85 %). A 58 h/year downtime means roughly **1.1 h of unplanned stop per week**, which is excellent for a high-speed bottling line.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: PFDavg for a 1oo1 (One-out-of-One) Low-Demand SIS

### Statement
A **steam-sterilizer over-pressure interlock** uses **one** pressure transmitter feeding **one** safety PLC. The transmitter has:
- Dangerous-undetected failure rate, $\lambda_{DU} = 5 \times 10^{-7}$ per hour
- Proof-test interval, $T_{proof} = 8760$ h (i.e., **once per year**)

Find: (i) the PFDavg, (ii) the corresponding SIL.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Recall the formula for a single-channel 1oo1 architecture.**

$$ \text{PFD}_{1oo1,\text{avg}} \approx \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2} $$

This formula arises from averaging the unavailability over one proof-test cycle. A newly proof-tested channel has zero unavailability; it grows linearly with time as the dangerous-undetected failure may occur, reaching $\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}$ at the end. The average over the interval is half the maximum:

$$ \overline{\text{PFD}}(t) = \dfrac{1}{T_{proof}}\int_{0}^{T_{proof}} \lambda_{DU}\, t\ \mathrm{d}t = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU}\, T_{proof}}{2} $$

**Step 2 — Plug in the numbers.**

$$ \text{PFD}_{avg} = \dfrac{(5 \times 10^{-7}) \times 8760}{2} $$

**Step 3 — Multiply the numerator.**

$$ 5 \times 10^{-7} \times 8760 = 5 \times 8760 \times 10^{-7} = 43\,800 \times 10^{-7} = 4.38 \times 10^{-3} $$

**Step 4 — Divide by 2.**

$$ \text{PFD}_{avg} = \dfrac{4.38 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 2.19 \times 10^{-3} $$

**Step 5 — Compare to the SIL table.**

| SIL | PFDavg range |
|------|--------------|
| SIL 1 | 10⁻² ≤ PFD < 10⁻¹ |
| SIL 2 | 10⁻³ ≤ PFD < 10⁻² |
| SIL 3 | 10⁻⁴ ≤ PFD < 10⁻³ |

Our value $2.19 \times 10^{-3}$ lies in the range $[10^{-3}, 10^{-2})$ ⇒ **SIL 2**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Conclusion:** The single-channel 1oo1 sterilizer interlock achieves **SIL 2** with annual proof testing. To upgrade to **SIL 3**, the designer must either (a) use a **1oo2 architecture** that *reduces* PFDavg by roughly an order of magnitude, or (b) shorten $T_{proof}$ to **~6 months**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Required Sampling Time for a Pasteurization Loop

### Statement
A **high-temperature short-time (HTST) pasteurizer** in a dairy plant has a thermal process time-constant $\tau = 25$ s. The PLC runs a **PID loop** to maintain milk at **72 °C ± 0.5 °C**.

Find the (i) loop bandwidth, (ii) minimum PLC sampling frequency by Nyquist, (iii) recommended practical sampling frequency (10× rule).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the first-order thermal time-constant.**

For a first-order process, the closed-loop bandwidth is approximately:

$$ f_{c} \approx \dfrac{1}{2\pi \tau} $$

**Step 2 — Substitute $\tau = 25$ s.**

$$ f_{c} = \dfrac{1}{2 \times 3.1416 \times 25} = \dfrac{1}{157.08} $$

$$ f_{c} = 6.366 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{Hz} $$

So $f_{c} \approx 6.4$ mHz, or equivalently a period of:

$$ T_{c} = \dfrac{1}{f_{c}} = 157\ \text{s} \approx 2.6\ \text{min} $$

**Step 3 — Nyquist minimum sampling rate.**

$$ f_{s,\min} = 2 \times f_{c} = 2 \times 6.366 \times 10^{-3} = 1.273 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{Hz} $$

Equivalently, the maximum allowable sampling period:

$$ T_{s,\max} = \dfrac{1}{f_{s,\min}} = \dfrac{1}{0.01273} = 78.5\ \text{s} $$

**Step 4 — Apply the practical 10× rule.**

$$ f_{s,\text{practical}} = 10 \times f_{c} = 10 \times 6.366 \times 10^{-3} = 6.366 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{Hz} $$

$$ T_{s,\text{practical}} = \dfrac{1}{f_{s,\text{practical}}} = 15.7\ \text{s} $$

**Step 5 — Compare to the PLC scan time.**

A typical PLC scan time is **20 ms** = $0.02$ s. The required practical sampling period is $15.7$ s, so the loop is *over-sampled by a factor of*:

$$ \text{Over-sampling factor} = \dfrac{15.7}{0.02} = 785\times $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** A standard PLC is **vastly over-qualified** for HTST pasteurization; the loop is essentially *quasi-continuous* from the plant-floor perspective. This is exactly why **PLCs dominate dairy automation** while DCS is reserved for very-large integrated refineries.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Dead-Leg Compliance for a Sanitary Branch Line

### Statement
A **CIP spray-ball supply line** branches off from a 50 mm main header. According to EHEDG, the maximum dead-leg length $L$ must satisfy $L \le 2D$, where $D$ is the branch diameter.

Find the maximum permissible dead-leg length for branch diameters of (i) $D = 25$ mm, (ii) $D = 50$ mm, (iii) $D = 80$ mm.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — State the rule explicitly.**

$$ L_{\max} = 2D $$

**Step 2 — Compute for each branch.**

For $D = 25$ mm:

$$ L_{\max} = 2 \times 25 = 50\ \text{mm} $$

For $D = 50$ mm:

$$ L_{\max} = 2 \times 50 = 100\ \text{mm} $$

For $D = 80$ mm:

$$ L_{\max} = 2 \times 80 = 160\ \text{mm} $$

**Step 3 — Convert to engineering-friendly units.**

| Branch D (mm) | L_max (mm) | L_max (inches) |
|---------------|------------|-----------------|
| 25 | 50 | ≈ 1.97 |
| 50 | 100 | ≈ 3.94 |
| 80 | 160 | ≈ 6.30 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Designer's Tip:** If the geometric piping route forces a longer dead-leg, the *only* compliant solutions are: (a) increase branch diameter, (b) add a self-draining valve cluster at the branch root, (c) use *zero-dead-leg* (ZDL) / *flush-mounted* valves.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) from PFDavg

### Statement
A **propane-fired bakery oven** has a baseline *probability of explosion* per year of $F = 0.1$ (i.e., expected once every 10 years, with no safety system). The **flame-failure SIS** achieves PFDavg $= 10^{-2}$.

Find: (i) the risk reduction factor, (ii) the achieved mitigated frequency, (iii) the achieved SIL.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define RRF.**

The Risk Reduction Factor is the inverse of PFDavg:

$$ \text{RRF} = \dfrac{1}{\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}}} $$

**Step 2 — Compute RRF for our SIS.**

$$ \text{RRF} = \dfrac{1}{10^{-2}} = 100 $$

**Step 3 — Compute the mitigated (post-SIS) event frequency.**

$$ F_{\text{mitigated}} = F \times \text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = 0.1 \times 10^{-2} = 10^{-3}\ \text{per year} $$

This means the explosion is now expected *once in 1 000 years* with the SIS in place.

**Step 4 — Determine the SIL from the PFDavg value.**

A PFDavg of $10^{-2}$ falls in the range $[10^{-2}, 10^{-1})$ ⇒ **SIL 1**.

**Step 5 — Cross-check using the SIL / RRF table.**

| SIL | PFD range | RRF range |
|------|------------|-----------|
| 1 | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ | 10 to 100 |
| 2 | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ | 100 to 1 000 |
| 3 | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ | 1 000 to 10 000 |

Our PFDavg = $10^{-2}$ gives RRF = 100, which is at the *upper boundary* of SIL 1. To reach *robust* SIL 1 (e.g., PFDavg = $5 \times 10^{-2}$ ⇒ RRF = 20), one would need more frequent proof testing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** A simple flame-failure interlock provides SIL 1 protection and reduces the explosion frequency from 0.1/yr (1 in 10 years) to 0.001/yr (1 in 1 000 years). This is acceptable for a bakery oven but **inadequate for a hydrogen-cooled food-grade hydrogenation reactor**, which would need SIL 2 or SIL 3.

---

## 3.6 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix (Worked Summary)

| # | Problem | Key Formula | Final Answer |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | Bottling line availability | $A = \text{MTBF}/(\text{MTBF}+\text{MTTR})$ | $A = 99.34\,\%$ |
| 2 | 1oo1 sterilizer PFD | $\text{PFD} = \lambda_{DU} T/2$ | $2.19\times 10^{-3}$ → SIL 2 |
| 3 | Pasteurization sampling | $f_s = 10/(2\pi\tau)$ | $T_s = 15.7$ s (PLC is 785× over-qualified) |
| 4 | Dead-leg compliance | $L = 2D$ | $50$ / $100$ / $160$ mm |
| 5 | Bakery oven RRF | $\text{RRF} = 1/\text{PFD}$ | RRF = 100 → SIL 1 |

## 4.2 Diagram 2: GAMP-5 V-Model for System Design & Validation

This V-Model is the **validation lifecycle** taught in regulated-food-automation courses. It is a *symmetrical* diagram: design on the left, verification on the right.

graph TD
