
> [!NOTE]
> The dashed lines indicate **traceability matrices** — every requirement in the URS must map to exactly one test in the PQ. Auditors (FSSAI, US-FDA, BRC) routinely check this.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3: System Design Decision Flow — PLC vs. SCADA vs. DCS

```mermaid
graph TD
    START["NEW FOOD PLANT AUTOMATION PROJECT"]
    Q1{"PLANT TYPE"}
    START --> Q1
    Q1 -->|DISCRETE MACHINES PACKAGING LABELING| PLC["USE PLC BASED SYSTEM"]
    Q1 -->|CONTINUOUS DAIRY BREWERY OIL| Q2{"PLANT SIZE"}
    Q2 -->|SMALL lt 500 I O| DCSLOW["DCS LIGHT OR HYBRID PLC SCADA"]
    Q2 -->|MEDIUM 500 TO 3000 I O| DCSCORE["FULL DCS WITH REDUNDANT CONTROLLERS"]
    Q2 -->|LARGE gt 3000 I O| DCSHI["ENTERPRISE DCS WITH MES INTEGRATION"]
    Q1 -->|MULTI SITE COLD CHAIN| SCADA["USE SCADA FOR SUPERVISORY TELEMETRY"]

    PLC --> HMI1["LOCAL HMI PANEL"]
    DCSCORE --> HMI2["ENGINEERED OPERATOR CONSOLES"]
    SCADA --> HMI3["CENTRAL CONTROL ROOM"]
```

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Functional Block Architecture of a Food-Plant Automation System

This single diagram captures **all design layers** a KTU student must discuss in a 14-mark design question.

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph LVL4["ENTERPRISE LAYER"]
        ERP["ERP MODULE - PROCUREMENT SALES FINANCE"]
    end

    subgraph LVL3["OPERATIONS LAYER"]
        MES["MES - RECIPE BATCH RECORD OEE"]
        LIMS["LIMS - LAB QC INTEGRATION"]
    end

    subgraph LVL2["SUPERVISORY LAYER"]
        SCADA["SCADA SERVER"]
        HMI["HMI CONSOLES PER ISA 101"]
    end

    subgraph LVL1["CONTROL LAYER"]
        DCSPLC["DCS OR PLC CONTROLLERS"]
        SIS["SAFETY INSTRUMENTED SYSTEM SIS"]
    end

    subgraph LVL0["FIELD LAYER"]
        SENS["SENSORS RTD PT PH FLOW LEVEL"]
        ACTU["ACTUATORS VALVES VFD MOTORS"]
    end

    ERP --- MES
    MES --- SCADA
    LIMS --- SCADA
    SCADA --- HMI
    SCADA --- DCSPLC
    SCADA --- SIS
    DCSPLC --- SENS
    DCSPLC --- ACTU
    SIS --- ACTU
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SIS must be physically separate** from the Basic Process Control System (BPCS) in a KTU design answer — separate I/O, separate controller, separate wiring, and ideally separate power. This is the *single most-lost* mark in design questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> Questions below are modeled strictly on **KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech End-Semester Examination (ESE)** patterns: 2-mark / 3-mark short answer in **Part A**, 14-mark full-question in **Part B with internal choice**.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**
**Define the term "System Design Principles" as applied to automation in the food industry. List any FOUR key design considerations unique to food plant automation.**

**Model Answer (verbatim-ready):**

> System Design Principles in food-industry automation refer to the structured engineering methodology used to plan, specify, integrate, validate, and commission automated process control systems across the food manufacturing value chain. The objective is to achieve consistent product quality, regulatory compliance, traceability, and operator safety.
>
> Four key design considerations unique to food plants are:
> 1. **Hygienic / sanitary design** — SS 316L surfaces, self-draining slopes, Ra ≤ 0.8 µm, dead-leg ≤ 2D.
> 2. **Traceability & batch records** — every raw-material lot, process parameter, and finished product must be linked via MES / SCADA.
> 3. **CIP integration** — the control system must seamlessly drive Cleaning-In-Place sequences.
> 4. **Regulatory compliance** — FSSAI, ISO 22000, FDA 21 CFR Part 11 (electronic records), GAMP 5, and BRCGS Issue 9.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**
**Compare PLC-based and DCS-based automation with respect to (i) architecture, (ii) redundancy, and (iii) typical food-industry application.**

**Model Answer (table form for 3-mark full marks):**

| Aspect | PLC-Based | DCS-Based |
|--------|-----------|-----------|
| **Architecture** | Standalone or networked PLCs with separate SCADA | Distributed controllers + engineered operator consoles + system server |
| **Redundancy** | Optional, mostly single | Hot-redundant controllers, servers, networks as standard |
| **Food-Plant Use** | Packaging lines, labelers, fillers (discrete) | Dairy, brewing, edible-oil refinery (continuous, integrated) |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Question (14 Marks, Module-3 Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Most Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing OQ with PQ** — remember: OQ tests the *system*, PQ tests the *product*.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention SIS separation** from the BPCS — losing up to 2 marks.
> 3. **Skipping the formula derivation** in numerical questions — board examiners award partial marks for each correct algebraic step.
> 4. **Citing outdated standards** (e.g., IEC 61508 only, without IEC 61511) — always mention *both*.
> 5. **Not drawing a labeled block diagram** in design questions — without a diagram, full 14 marks are virtually impossible.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — *Numerical + Design*

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 / Dec 2024 Expected Pattern]**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**
A **UHT milk processing line** has the following reliability data:
- MTBF = 800 hours
- MTTR = 6 hours
- Performance factor = 0.92
- Quality factor = 0.97

**(i)** Calculate the steady-state availability of the line.
**(ii)** Calculate the OEE of the line.
**(iii)** If the **sterilizer over-temperature safety loop** uses a single-channel pressure transmitter with $\lambda_{DU} = 3 \times 10^{-7}$ per hour and a proof-test interval of **4 380 hours (6 months)**, determine the achieved SIL.

**Model Answer — Part (a):**

**(i) Availability:**

$$ A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \dfrac{800}{800 + 6} = \dfrac{800}{806} $$

$$ A = 0.9926 = 99.26\ \% $$

**(ii) OEE:**

$$ \text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q = 0.9926 \times 0.92 \times 0.97 $$

$$ \text{OEE} = 0.9926 \times 0.8924 = 0.8858 = 88.58\ \% $$

This is **world-class performance** (> 85 %).

**(iii) PFD and SIL:**

$$ \text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \times T_{proof}}{2} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^{-7} \times 4380}{2} $$

$$ \text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{1.314 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 6.57 \times 10^{-4} $$

Comparing to the SIL table:

- $6.57 \times 10^{-4}$ lies in the range $[10^{-3}, 10^{-4})$? — *No, it lies in $[10^{-4}, 10^{-3})$*.
- Therefore the achieved **SIL = 3**.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**
With the help of a **labeled block diagram**, describe the **hierarchical (ISA-95) design** of a fully automated **dairy processing plant**. Briefly state the function of EACH level.

**Model Answer — Part (b):**

Refer to **Section 4.1 diagram** and explain **each level** with one specific dairy-plant example:

| Level | Name | Function | Dairy Example |
|-------|------|----------|---------------|
| L0 | Process | Physical sensing & actuation | PT-100 RTD in pasteurizer, pneumatic divert valve on filler |
| L1 | Basic Control | Real-time PID & interlocks | Allen-Bradley ControlLogix PLC holding milk at 72 °C for 15 s |
| L2 | Supervisory | Operator screens, alarms, trends | Wonderware SCADA showing Brix trend on evaporator mimic |
| L3 | Operations | Recipe, batch, OEE, genealogy | MES tracking each cheese vat's start time, pH profile, yield |
| L4 | Business | Procurement, finance, dispatch | SAP generating daily milk-collection schedule for chilling centres |
| L5 | Enterprise | Cloud & vendor connectivity | Remote OEM support via secure VPN |

**Mandatory inclusions for full 7 marks:**
- Diagram (3 marks)
- One specific example per level (2 marks)
- Mention of **"Manufacturing Conduit"** boundary between L2 and L3 (1 mark)
- Mention of **SIS separation** from BPCS (1 mark)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Design + Theory*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / May 2025 Expected Pattern]**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**(i)** Explain the **GAMP-5 V-Model** for automation-system design in a regulated food plant. Label each design stage and its corresponding verification/validation stage.
**(ii)** Differentiate between **IQ, OQ, and PQ** with a food-industry example for each.

**Model Answer — Part (a):**

**(i) V-Model Stages (refer to Section 4.2 diagram):**

| Left Arm (Design) | Right Arm (Verification) |
|--------------------|----------------------------|
| URS (User Requirements) | PQ (Performance Qualification) |
| FS (Functional Spec) | OQ (Operational Qualification) |
| DS (Design Spec) | IQ (Installation Qualification) |
| Configuration / Coding | FAT → SAT → Module Test |

**Traceability rule:** Every URS line must be linked to a PQ test. Every FS line must be linked to an OQ test. Auditors check this matrix first.

**(ii) IQ / OQ / PQ — Food Industry Examples:**

- **IQ (Installation Qualification):** *Documented verification that the SCADA server, PLC panel, and field wiring are installed as per the design drawings and component list.* Example: verifying that the panel incoming power is 230 V AC ± 10 %, all cable glands are IP 65, and the SS 304 enclosure is earthed < 1 Ω.
- **OQ (Operational Qualification):** *Documented verification that the system performs as per the Functional Specification under simulated conditions.* Example: forcing the pasteurizer RTD to read 90 °C in software and confirming the steam valve fully closes within 5 s.
- **PQ (Performance Qualification):** *Documented verification that the system produces food meeting FSSAI/product spec using real raw material.* Example: running three consecutive batches of 5 000 L of toned milk and confirming the *product* fat % is 3.0 ± 0.1 and SNF is 8.5 ± 0.2 in the lab.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**(i)** Define **SIL**. List the **four SIL levels** and their **PFDavg ranges** (low-demand mode).
**(ii)** A safety instrumented function in a **cold-storage ammonia plant** must reduce the risk of a refrigerant release. The unprotected event frequency is **0.05 per year**, and the required mitigated frequency is **≤ 10⁻⁴ per year**. Determine the *minimum* required SIL and the *minimum acceptable* PFDavg.

**Model Answer — Part (b):**

**(i) SIL Definition and PFDavg Table:**

> **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** is a discrete level (1 to 4) used to specify the reliability of a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF), defined in **IEC 61508 / IEC 61511** as a range of *probability of failure on demand (PFDavg)* values for low-demand mode of operation.

| SIL | PFDavg range (low-demand) |
|------|---------------------------|
| 1 | $10^{-2} \le \text{PFD} < 10^{-1}$ |
| 2 | $10^{-3} \le \text{PFD} < 10^{-2}$ |
| 3 | $10^{-4} \le \text{PFD} < 10^{-3}$ |
| 4 | $10^{-5} \le \text{PFD} < 10^{-4}$ |

**(ii) Required SIL for the Ammonia SIS:**

$$ \text{Required PFD} \le \dfrac{F_{\text{mitigated}}}{F_{\text{unprotected}}} = \dfrac{10^{-4}}{0.05} $$

$$ \text{Required PFD} \le 2 \times 10^{-3} $$

Comparing to the SIL table: $2 \times 10^{-3}$ lies in $[10^{-3}, 10^{-2})$ ⇒ **Required SIL = 2**, with $\text{PFD}_{\max} = 10^{-2}$ and a target of $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} \le 2 \times 10^{-3}$.

**Design consequence:** A single-channel architecture is insufficient; the designer must select either a **1oo2 (one-out-of-two) voted architecture** with annual proof testing, *or* a 1oo1 architecture with **proof-test interval ≤ 6 months** to halve the PFDavg.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this checklist verbatim before the KTU exam — it is the high-density summary of the entire topic.**

### A. Conceptual Pillars
- ☐ **ISA-95 / Purdue Model** has **6 levels (L0–L5)**. Memorize one food-industry example per level.
- ☐ The **"Manufacturing Conduit"** is the **forbidden** boundary between real-time control (L0–L2) and IT (L3–L4).
- ☐ **GAMP-5 V-Model** maps URS→PQ, FS→OQ, DS→IQ — symmetrical design-then-validate.
- ☐ **PLC** = fast & discrete; **SCADA** = supervisory & multi-site; **DCS** = continuous & integrated.
- ☐ **SIS** must be **physically separate** from BPCS.

### B. Numerical Formulas (must know)
- ☐ $A = \text{MTBF}/(\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$
- ☐ $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$
- ☐ $\text{PFD}_{1oo1} = \lambda_{DU}\, T_{proof}/2$
- ☐ $\text{RRF} = 1/\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}}$
- ☐ $f_{s,\text{practical}} = 10\, f_{c}$; $f_{c} = 1/(2\pi\tau)$

### C. Safety Standards
- ☐ **IEC 61508** = generic functional safety; **IEC 61511** = process-industry sector standard.
- ☐ **SIL 1** = PFD 10⁻² to 10⁻¹; **SIL 2** = 10⁻³ to 10⁻²; **SIL 3** = 10⁻⁴ to 10⁻³; **SIL 4** = 10⁻⁵ to 10⁻⁴.
- ☐ **High-demand mode** uses **PFH (per hour)** instead of PFDavg (per demand).

### D. Sanitary / Hygienic Design Numbers
- ☐ **Surface roughness Ra ≤ 0.8 µm** for food-contact (3-A / EHEDG).
- ☐ **Self-draining slope ≥ 1 %** (1 in 100).
- ☐ **Dead-leg L ≤ 2D** (branch length rule).
- ☐ **Material: SS 316L** (product contact), **SS 304** (non-contact framing).

### E. Communication & Network Selection
- ☐ **4–20 mA + HART** = legacy, point-to-point.
- ☐ **Profibus PA / Foundation Fieldbus** = 31.25 kbps, multi-drop, intrinsically safe.
- ☐ **OPC UA** = the modern L2↔L3/L4 standard, supports encryption + AC.
- ☐ **OPC UA + TSN** = future of unified, deterministic Ethernet (2024 trend).

### F. Validation Documents (GAMP 5 Categories)
- ☐ **Cat 1** = infrastructure OS (non-configured).
- ☐ **Cat 3** = off-the-shelf software (e.g., Windows).
- ☐ **Cat 4** = configured product (e.g., SCADA with CIs).
- ☐ **Cat 5** = custom-coded (e.g., custom PLC blocks).

### G. KTU Examiner's "Mark-Loser" Traps
- ☐ Confusing **OQ vs. PQ**.
- ☐ Forgetting **SIS separation**.
- ☐ Skipping the **block diagram** in a 14-mark design answer.
- ☐ Using **outdated standards** (always cite current revisions: IEC 61511:2016, ISA-101.01-2015, BRCGS Issue 9).
- ☐ Not converting **% OEE** explicitly — write both decimal AND percentage.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Key Considerations in Designing Automated Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT864 - AUTOMATION IN FOOD INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Key Considerations in Designing Automated Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Considerations in Designing Automated Food Systems** refer to the systematic, multidisciplinary set of engineering, economic, hygienic, regulatory, and operational factors that must be evaluated, weighted, and integrated during the conceptual, detailed, and commissioning stages of an automation project to ensure the system is **safe, sanitary, reliable, cost-effective, flexible, and compliant** with food-industry standards.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEFTT864 – Automation in Food Industry)**, these considerations are the *decision framework* that connects a food-process requirement (e.g., pasteurisation, aseptic filling, sorting, packaging) to a working automated system comprising **sensors, actuators, controllers (PLC/DCS/SCADA), HMIs, and material-handling equipment**.

The principal categories adopted by global food-automation standards (ISO 22000, FSSC 22000, GAMP 5, ISA-95, ISA-88, 3-A Sanitary Standards, FDA 21 CFR Part 11) are:

1. **Product & Process Considerations** – nature of the food (solid, liquid, particulate, sticky, abrasive, heat-sensitive).
2. **Hygienic & Sanitary Design** – CIP/SIP capability, surface finish, drainage, dead-legs, material grade (**SS 304 / SS 316 / SS 316L**).
3. **Safety Considerations** – personnel safety, machine safety, food safety (HACCP, allergen control).
4. **Economic Considerations** – CAPEX, OPEX, **ROI**, **Payback Period**, life-cycle cost.
5. **Flexibility & Scalability** – changeover time, modularity, future capacity expansion.
6. **Maintainability & Reliability** – **MTBF**, **MTTR**, predictive maintenance readiness.
7. **Regulatory & Compliance** – FSSAI (India), FDA, USDA, EU CE-Marking, BIS, ISO standards.
8. **Control Architecture** – PLC vs. DCS vs. SCADA, network topology, redundancy.
9. **Sensor & Instrumentation** – hygienic sensor selection (IP69K), accuracy, calibration.
10. **Human-Machine Interface (HMI)** – ergonomic design, role-based access, traceability.
11. **Environmental Factors** – ambient temperature (**–5 °C to +55 °C**), humidity, wash-down zones.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — “Designing an Automated Kitchen vs. Designing a Home Kitchen”**
>
> Imagine you are asked to design a kitchen for a 5-star hotel chain.
> - You cannot copy your *home kitchen* layout — the *scale*, *food type*, *speed*, and *cleanliness rules* are different.
> - You must ask: **What is being cooked? (Product)** — A bakery needs dough-handling robots; a beverage plant needs CIP-friendly tanks.
> - **Who will use it? (Operator skill)** — The HMI buttons must match the operator’s literacy and training level.
> - **What if the menu changes next year? (Flexibility)** — The line should be re-configurable in hours, not months.
> - **How easy is it to clean? (Hygiene)** — Every pipe, valve, and sensor must be designed so that no food residue can hide.
> - **Will it pass the FSSAI inspector? (Regulatory)** — Documentation and traceability are non-negotiable.
>
> **Key Insight:** A food-automation system that *technically works* but cannot be *cleaned, validated, or documented* is **not a valid system**. The "key considerations" are precisely this checklist that prevents such failures.

### 1.3 Typical Design Parameters at a Glance

| Parameter | Typical Range / Standard |
|---|---|
| Surface roughness (Ra) of product-contact surfaces | **Ra ≤ 0.8 µm** (3-A / EHEDG) |
| Sloped self-draining surfaces | **≥ 3°** slope |
| Minimum bend radius of product piping | **≥ 1.5 × pipe diameter** |
| Dead-leg length in sanitary piping | **≤ 2 × pipe diameter** |
| Operating ambient temperature | **0 °C to +50 °C** |
| Humidity | **5 % to 95 % RH, non-condensing** |
| Wash-down zone ingress protection | **IP66 / IP67 / IP69K** |
| PLC scan time (typical) | **5 ms to 20 ms** |
| Ethernet cycle time (Profinet/EtherNet/IP) | **≤ 1 ms** |
| Valve response time (pneumatic) | **≤ 50 ms** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Eleven Pillars of Design — Structured Logic

**Pillar 1 — Product & Process Characterisation**
- Identify physical state (solid, liquid, slurry, powder), viscosity, abrasiveness, particle size, and temperature sensitivity.
- Map **Critical Control Points (CCPs)** in the HACCP plan; every CCP requires a sensor and a final control element.
- *Why?* Abrasive foods (e.g., sugar, rice) need hardened-steel components; sticky foods (chocolate, honey) need self-cleaning geometries.

**Pillar 2 — Hygienic & Sanitary Design**
- Use **SS 316L** for product contact, **SS 304** for non-contact frame.
- Specify **electropolished surfaces** (Ra ≤ 0.8 µm), **tri-clamp fittings**, **sanitary gaskets (EPDM / silicone / FKM)**.
- Ensure **self-draining geometry** to avoid microbial harborage.
- Design for **CIP (Clean-in-Place)** and **SIP (Sterilise-in-Place)** with minimum 1.5 m/s turbulent flow.

**Pillar 3 — Safety Engineering**
- **Machine Safety:** EN ISO 12100, EN 60204-1, ISO 13849-1 (PL ≥ d for food machinery).
- **Process Safety:** Pressure-relief valves, flame arrestors, inert-gas blanketing for powders.
- **Food Safety:** Allergen segregation, foreign-body detection (metal detector, X-ray), traceability via batch codes.

**Pillar 4 — Economic Justification**
- **CAPEX** (Capital Expenditure) — equipment, installation, validation, training.
- **OPEX** (Operating Expenditure) — energy, consumables, labour, maintenance, sanitation.
- Compute **ROI** and **Payback Period** (see Formula Sheet below).

**Pillar 5 — Flexibility & Changeover**
- Design for **SMED (Single-Minute Exchange of Die)** principles.
- Use **modular skids**, **quick-disconnect couplings**, recipe-based PLC programming (ISA-88 batch model).
- Aim for changeover time **< 15 minutes** for multi-product lines.

**Pillar 6 — Reliability & Maintainability**
- Specify **MTBF ≥ 20,000 h** for critical instrumentation.
- Plan **MTTR ≤ 30 minutes** through modularity and good documentation.
- Include condition-monitoring sensors (vibration, temperature, current) for **predictive maintenance**.

**Pillar 7 — Regulatory & Documentation Compliance**
- FSSAI (India), FDA 21 CFR Part 11 (electronic records/signatures), EU 1935/2004 (food contact materials), 3-A Sanitary Standards, EHEDG.
- Maintain a **Design History File (DHF)**, **FAT/SAT reports**, IQ/OQ/PQ validation protocols.

**Pillar 8 — Control Architecture**
- **PLC** for discrete/fast logic (packaging, sorting).
- **DCS** for continuous processes (dairy, beverage, brewing).
- **SCADA** for supervisory visualisation and data acquisition.
- Adopt **ISA-95** levels (Level 0: field, Level 1: control, Level 2: supervisory, Level 3: MES, Level 4: ERP).

**Pillar 9 — Sensor & Instrumentation Selection**
- Hygienic sensors: **Pt-100 Class A RTDs**, **Hygienic pressure transmitters**, **Capacitive level probes**.
- Ingress protection: **IP69K** for wash-down areas.
- Calibration interval: typically **annually** for critical CCP sensors.

**Pillar 10 — HMI / Operator Interface**
- Role-based access (Operator / Supervisor / Engineer).
- Colour conventions: **Red = Stop / Fault, Green = Run, Yellow = Warning, Blue = Mandatory action**.
- Include **trend graphs, alarm history, batch reports** on every screen.

**Pillar 11 — Environmental & Site Factors**
- **Ambient temperature, humidity, dust, water-jet exposure, chemical exposure (CIP detergents: 1–3 % NaOH @ 80 °C, 1 % HNO₃)**.
- Plan cable routing, cabinet cooling, and enclosure ratings accordingly.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Return on Investment | $$ROI \;=\; \frac{\text{Net Annual Benefit}}{\text{Initial Investment}} \times 100\,\%$$ | Benefit in INR/yr, Investment in INR |
| 2 | Payback Period | $$PP \;=\; \frac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Net Cash Inflow}}$$ | Years |
| 3 | Net Present Value | $$NPV \;=\; \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{CF_{t}}{(1+r)^{t}} \;-\; C_{0}$$ | r = discount rate, CF = cash flow |
| 4 | Overall Equipment Effectiveness | $$OEE \;=\; A \times P \times Q$$ | World-class OEE ≥ 85 % |
| 5 | Availability | $$A \;=\; \frac{\text{Operating Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}}$$ | Dimensionless (0–1) |
| 6 | Performance | $$P \;=\; \frac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces}}{\text{Operating Time}}$$ | Dimensionless (0–1) |
| 7 | Quality | $$Q \;=\; \frac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces}}$$ | Dimensionless (0–1) |
| 8 | Mean Time Between Failures | $$MTBF \;=\; \frac{\text{Total Uptime}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$$ | Hours |
| 9 | Mean Time To Repair | $$MTTR \;=\; \frac{\text{Total Downtime}}{\text{Number of Repairs}}$$ | Hours |
| 10 | Reynolds Number (CIP) | $$Re \;=\; \frac{\rho \, v \, D}{\mu}$$ | Re ≥ 4000 → Turbulent (required for CIP) |
| 11 | Pressure Drop (CIP loop) | $$\Delta P \;=\; f \,\frac{L}{D}\,\frac{\rho v^{2}}{2}$$ | f = Darcy friction factor |
| 12 | Heat Transfer (Pasteurisation) | $$Q \;=\; \dot{m} \, c_{p} \, \Delta T$$ | Watts |
| 13 | F₀ Pasteurisation Value | $$F_{0} \;=\; \int_{0}^{t} 10^{(T(t)-T_{ref})/z} \, dt$$ | z = 10 °C, T_ref = 121.1 °C |
| 14 | Line Balancing Efficiency | $$\eta_{LB} \;=\; \frac{\sum t_{i}}{n \times t_{max}} \times 100\,\%$$ | t_i = station time |
| 15 | Cost per Unit | $$CPU \;=\; \frac{C_{total}}{N_{units}}$$ | INR / unit |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX RULE for KTU Boards:** Never use the vertical pipe symbol `|` inside a markdown table row (e.g., writing `|x|` for absolute value). Use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the table grid during PDF rendering.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility

These formulas and considerations drive every food-automation project from concept to commissioning. In a **dairy plant**, Re ≥ 4000 dictates pump sizing; in a **bakery**, OEE determines whether a robot cell is justified; in a **beverage filler**, F₀ validates the pasteuriser; in a **frozen-food line**, the payback period decides if cobot palletising is viable.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Payback Period and ROI for a CIP Automation Upgrade

**Statement.** A food plant spends **₹ 50,00,000** (₹ 5 million) to automate a CIP loop. Annual labour saving = **₹ 12,00,000**, chemical saving = **₹ 4,00,000**, water-and-energy saving = **₹ 2,00,000**, but new annual maintenance cost = **₹ 3,00,000**. Compute **Payback Period (PP)** and **ROI**.

**Step 1 — Compute Net Annual Benefit.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Annual Benefit} &= (\text{Labour} + \text{Chemical} + \text{Energy-Water}) - \text{Maintenance} \\
&= (12{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000) - 3{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 18{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 15{,}00{,}000 \;\text{INR / year}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* All savings are positive cash inflows; the maintenance cost is a recurring outflow. We net them because ROI is a measure of *net* yearly benefit relative to the initial outlay.

**Step 2 — Compute Payback Period.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PP &= \frac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Net Annual Benefit}} \\
&= \frac{50{,}00{,}000}{15{,}00{,}000} \\
&= 3.33 \;\text{years} \;\;\approx\; 3\;\text{years and } 4\;\text{months}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* PP is the time for the cumulative net cash inflow to recover the capital outlay. A PP below 5 years is generally acceptable for the food industry.

**Step 3 — Compute ROI (over a 5-year horizon, ignoring discounting).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ROI_{5yr} &= \frac{5 \times 15{,}00{,}000 - 50{,}00{,}000}{50{,}00{,}000} \times 100\,\% \\
&= \frac{75{,}00{,}000 - 50{,}00{,}000}{50{,}00{,}000} \times 100\,\% \\
&= \frac{25{,}00{,}000}{50{,}00{,}000} \times 100\,\% \\
&= 50\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* ROI here is total-return-on-investment over 5 years (5 × annual net benefit minus capital) divided by capital. A 50 % cumulative ROI is excellent.

**Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation.**
- A payback of **3.33 years** is well within food-industry norms (typical benchmark ≤ 5 years).
- The decision is **ACCEPT** the automation project, provided the hygienic-design and validation costs have already been included in the ₹ 50 L figure.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — OEE Computation for a Biscuit Packaging Line

**Statement.** A biscuit packaging line operates **2 shifts × 8 h = 16 h/day**, 25 days/month. Daily recorded data for one month: **Planned Production Time = 400 h**, **Operating Time = 340 h** (downtime = 60 h), **Ideal Cycle Time = 1.2 s/pack**, **Total Pieces Produced = 800,000**, **Rejected Pieces = 8,000**. Compute **OEE**.

**Step 1 — Availability.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\text{Operating Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}} \\
  &= \frac{340}{400} \\
  &= 0.85 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 85\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* 60 h lost in stops, changeovers, breakdowns. 340 h of actual running out of 400 h planned.

**Step 2 — Performance.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces}}{\text{Operating Time}} \\
  &= \frac{1.2 \times 800{,}000}{340 \times 3600} \\
  &= \frac{9{,}60{,}000}{12{,}24{,}000} \\
  &= 0.7843 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 78.43\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* 340 h × 3600 s/h = 12,24,000 s available. If the line ran at ideal 1.2 s/pack, it would have produced 12,24,000 / 1.2 = 10,20,000 packs. But it produced only 8,00,000, hence minor stops + reduced speed caused a 21.6 % loss.

**Step 3 — Quality.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \frac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces}} \\
  &= \frac{800{,}000 - 8{,}000}{800{,}000} \\
  &= \frac{7{,}92{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000} \\
  &= 0.99 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 99\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* 1 % rejection, typical of a well-tuned packaging line.

**Step 4 — OEE.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
OEE &= A \times P \times Q \\
    &= 0.85 \times 0.7843 \times 0.99 \\
    &= 0.6600 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 66.00\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* The line is *typical* of a food packaging operation; world-class is ≥ 85 %. The bottleneck is **Performance**, indicating that speed losses (e.g., worn-out sealer belts, inefficient servo tuning) are the prime target for improvement.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix (Weighted Scoring Method)

A practical method to short-list automation alternatives is the **Weighted-Score Decision Matrix**.

**Step 1 — List criteria** and assign weights (sum to 100 %).

| Criterion | Weight (%) |
|---|---|
| Hygienic Design | 20 |
| Cost / ROI | 18 |
| Reliability (MTBF) | 15 |
| Flexibility / Changeover | 12 |
| Safety (PL rating) | 12 |
| Regulatory Compliance | 10 |
| Maintainability | 8 |
| Energy Efficiency | 5 |
| **Total** | **100** |

**Step 2 — Score each alternative (1–10).**

**Step 3 — Compute Weighted Score.**

$$
\text{Weighted Score}_{i} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_{j} \times s_{ij}
$$

where $w_j$ is weight of criterion j, $s_{ij}$ is score of alternative i on criterion j.

*Example outcome (illustrative):* Alternative A (Turnkey OEM skid) → 8.4 / 10; Alternative B (In-house build) → 6.9 / 10; Alternative C (Retrofit) → 7.2 / 10. **Alternative A wins.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Design Considerations Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Food Product Brief] --> B1[Product and Process Analysis]
    A0 --> B2[Regulatory Mapping]
    B1 --> C1[Pillar 1: Product Specs]
    B1 --> C2[Pillar 2: Hygienic Design]
    B1 --> C3[Pillar 3: Safety Engineering]
    B1 --> C4[Pillar 4: Economic Analysis]
    B1 --> C5[Pillar 5: Flexibility Plan]
    B1 --> C6[Pillar 6: Reliability Plan]
    B2 --> C7[Pillar 7: Compliance Docs]
    C1 --> D1[Sensor Selection]
    C2 --> D2[Material Selection]
    C3 --> D3[Safety Circuits]
    C4 --> D4[ROI and PP Calculation]
    C5 --> D5[Modular Skid Design]
    C6 --> D6[Predictive Maintenance]
    C7 --> D7[IQ OQ PQ Validation]
    D1 --> E1[Control Architecture: PLC DCS SCADA]
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
    D4 --> E2[Go No Go Decision]
    D5 --> E1
    D6 --> E1
    D7 --> E2
    E1 --> F1[Pillar 8: Control HMI Design]
    F1 --> G1[Detailed Engineering]
    E2 --> H1[Approved: Commissioning]
    E2 --> H2[Rejected: Re Design]
    G1 --> H1
    H1 --> I1[Production with OEE Monitoring]
```

*Reading the diagram:* The design process begins with the *product brief* and *regulatory map*, branches into the eleven pillars, converges at the **control-architecture layer**, and is finally gated by a **Go/No-Go** decision based on economic and compliance results.

### 4.2 Pillar-to-Deliverable Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Pillar 1 Product] --> DL1[Process Flow Diagram P and ID]
    P2[Pillar 2 Hygiene] --> DL2[Hygienic Layout 3 D Model]
    P3[Pillar 3 Safety] --> DL3[Safety Risk Assessment SRA]
    P4[Pillar 4 Economics] --> DL4[Capex Opex Payback Sheet]
    P5[Pillar 5 Flexibility] --> DL5[Changeover Matrix]
    P6[Pillar 6 Reliability] --> DL6[RAM Analysis]
    P7[Pillar 7 Compliance] --> DL7[Design History File]
    P8[Pillar 8 Control] --> DL8[I O List and FDS]
    P9[Pillar 9 Sensors] --> DL9[Instrument Index]
    P10[Pillar 10 HMI] --> DL10[HMI Mockup]
    P11[Pillar 11 Environment] --> DL11[Site Survey Report]
    DL1 --> MERGE[Integrated Engineering Package]
    DL2 --> MERGE
    DL3 --> MERGE
    DL4 --> MERGE
    DL5 --> MERGE
    DL6 --> MERGE
    DL7 --> MERGE
    DL8 --> MERGE
    DL9 --> MERGE
    DL10 --> MERGE
    DL11 --> MERGE
```

*Interpretation:* Every pillar produces a **design deliverable**. These deliverables, when merged, form the *Engineering Package* that the OEM/integrator hands over to the food manufacturer for review and approval.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)**

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] List any three hygienic-design requirements for product-contact surfaces in a food-automation system.**

**Model Answer (3 × 1 = 3 marks):**

1. **Material of construction** — Food-grade **stainless steel SS 316L** (or SS 304 for non-contact) to prevent corrosion and metal leaching.
2. **Surface finish** — Electropolished to a roughness of **Ra ≤ 0.8 µm** to inhibit microbial adhesion.
3. **Self-draining geometry** — All surfaces sloped at **≥ 3°**, no dead-legs, **tri-clamp joints** to avoid residue accumulation.

*Marking Note:* 1 mark each. A simple list without justification earns full marks.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define MTBF and MTTR. Why are both important when designing an automated food line?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** is the average operating time between two consecutive failures, computed as
$$MTBF = \frac{\text{Total Uptime}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$$

- **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair)** is the average downtime to restore a failed system, computed as
$$MTTR = \frac{\text{Total Downtime}}{\text{Number of Repairs}}$$

- **Importance:** Together they give **Availability** ($A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$). High MTBF and low MTTR mean fewer production losses, lower contamination risk during restart, and higher OEE — all critical for a food line where downtime translates into product-spoilage, sanitation-cycle misses, and revenue loss.

*Marking Note:* 1 mark for each definition, 1 mark for relevance to food line.

---

### **Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Internal Choice)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the **economic considerations** in designing an automated food-processing system. With a suitable example, derive the formula for **Payback Period** and comment on the typical benchmark for the food industry.

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the **hygienic-design and material-selection** considerations for product-contact parts in a dairy automation line, mentioning the relevant Indian and international standards.

##### **Model Solution**

**(a) Economic Considerations [7 marks]**

Economic considerations decide whether the proposed automation is *worth* its technical merit. The major components are:

1. **CAPEX (Capital Expenditure)** — Includes equipment, installation, instrumentation, PLC/SCADA hardware, software licences, civil work, validation (IQ/OQ/PQ), and operator training. (1 mark)
2. **OPEX (Operating Expenditure)** — Energy, labour, consumables (CIP chemicals, lubricants, gaskets), spares, maintenance, software updates, insurance. (1 mark)
3. **Revenue uplift** — Increased throughput, reduced rejection, lower wastage, consistent quality, premium pricing, and export-grade certification. (1 mark)
4. **Risk-adjusted cost** — Cost of *not* automating: product recall cost, brand damage, regulatory penalties. (1 mark)

**Derivation of Payback Period [3 marks]:**

Let $C_0$ = initial investment, $CF_t$ = net cash inflow in year $t$.

The payback period $PP$ is the smallest integer $T$ such that
$$C_0 \;\le\; \sum_{t=1}^{T} CF_{t}$$

For a *uniform* cash inflow (CF = constant) — a common assumption in food-industry feasibility studies — this simplifies to
$$PP = \frac{C_0}{CF}$$

**Example:** A ₹ 80 L bottling-line PLC upgrade yields ₹ 25 L/year saving. $PP = 80/25 = 3.2$ years. (1 mark)

**Benchmark:** Indian food industry typically accepts $PP \le 5$ years; FMCG/high-volume lines accept $PP \le 3$ years. Anything > 7 years is generally rejected. (1 mark)

---

**(b) Hygienic & Material Considerations in a Dairy Line [7 marks]**

1. **Material of construction:** **SS 316L** for all product-contact surfaces (superior chloride resistance from milk salts and CIP detergents), **SS 304** for non-contact frames. (1 mark)
2. **Surface finish:** Electropolished to **Ra ≤ 0.8 µm**; mechanically polished **Ra ≤ 1.6 µm** acceptable for non-contact. (1 mark)
3. **Joint design:** **Tri-clamp (TC)** ferrules, **EPDM** or **silicone** food-grade gaskets; no threaded joints in product zone. (1 mark)
4. **Piping geometry:** Self-draining slope **≥ 3°**, bend radius **≥ 1.5 × D**, dead-legs **≤ 2 D**. (1 mark)
5. **Valves & instrumentation:** Sanitary butterfly valves, hygienic Pt-100 RTDs, **IP69K** pressure transmitters, in-line turbidity and conductivity sensors. (1 mark)
6. **Standards:** **3-A Sanitary Standards, EHEDG (Doc 2, 8, 10), FDA 21 CFR Part 11, EU 1935/2004, FSSAI Schedule 4, IS 14773** for hygienic design. (1 mark)
7. **Validation:** CIP test must achieve **Re ≥ 4000**, ≥ 80 °C, 1–3 % NaOH for 30 min, followed by 1 % HNO₃ rinse; final rinse conductivity ≤ 50 µS/cm. (1 mark)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the **eleven pillars** (or any eight, in detail) that an engineer must consider when designing an automated food-industry system.

**(b) [7 marks]** A sauce-bottling plant operates 16 h/day, 300 days/year. The line is being considered for a robotic palletiser costing **₹ 1,20,00,000**. Estimated annual saving: labour ₹ 40 L, ergonomic-injury-related cost reduction ₹ 8 L, and product-damage reduction ₹ 6 L. Annual AMC and energy = **₹ 14 L**. Compute the **Payback Period** and the **5-year ROI**. Comment on the decision.

##### **Model Solution**

**(a) Eleven Pillars [7 marks — 1 mark per pillar in detail, plus 1 mark for overview]**

1. **Product & Process Characterisation** — physical state, viscosity, abrasiveness, allergens, CCPs.
2. **Hygienic & Sanitary Design** — SS 316L, Ra ≤ 0.8 µm, self-draining, CIP/SIP.
3. **Safety Engineering** — machine safety (ISO 13849-1 PL ≥ d), process safety, food safety.
4. **Economic Justification** — CAPEX, OPEX, ROI, NPV, payback.
5. **Flexibility & Changeover** — SMED, modular skids, recipe management.
6. **Reliability & Maintainability** — MTBF, MTTR, predictive maintenance, OEE.
7. **Regulatory & Documentation** — FSSAI, FDA, FSSC 22000, GAMP 5, Design History File.
8. **Control Architecture** — PLC/DCS/SCADA, ISA-95/ISA-88, redundancy, cyber-security.
9. **Sensor & Instrumentation** — hygienic sensors, IP69K, calibration, redundancy.
10. **HMI / Operator Interface** — role-based access, ergonomics, colour code, alarm management (ISA-18.2).
11. **Environmental & Site Factors** — ambient temperature, humidity, wash-down zones, vibration, dust.

*(Any eight with explanations fetch full marks.)*

---

**(b) Payback Period and 5-Year ROI Computation [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Net Annual Cash Inflow.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Annual Benefit} &= (40 + 8 + 6) - 14 \\
&= 54 - 14 \\
&= 40\;\text{Lakhs / year} \\
&= \text{₹ } 40{,}00{,}000 \;\text{per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* All three savings are positive cash inflows; the AMC and energy are operating costs. Net = 40 L/yr. (2 marks)

**Step 2 — Payback Period.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PP &= \frac{C_0}{CF} = \frac{1{,}20{,}00{,}000}{40{,}00{,}000} \\
  &= 3.0\;\text{years}
\end{aligned}
$$

(2 marks)

**Step 3 — 5-Year ROI.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total 5-yr Net Benefit} &= 5 \times 40{,}00{,}000 = 2{,}00{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Net Profit over 5 yr} &= 2{,}00{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}20{,}00{,}000 = 80{,}00{,}000 \\
ROI_{5} &= \frac{80{,}00{,}000}{1{,}20{,}00{,}000} \times 100\,\% = 66.67\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

(2 marks)

**Step 4 — Engineering Decision [1 mark].**

- A payback of **3.0 years** is well within the food-industry benchmark of ≤ 5 years.
- A 5-year ROI of **66.67 %** is excellent.
- **Decision: ACCEPT** the robotic palletiser, provided the line has been validated for the additional floor-load and the HMI complies with the safety standards (ISO 13849-1 PL d).

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Areas in this Topic (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Unit-mismatch in ROI** — writing ROI in INR instead of %. Always multiply by 100.
> 2. **Mixing up MTBF and MTTR** — MTBF is a *reliability* metric; MTTR is a *maintainability* metric. Examiners deduct 1 mark if confused.
> 3. **Ignoring discounting in NPV/ROI for > 5-year horizons** — for a 14-mark problem, examiners expect at least a mention of *discount rate*.
> 4. **Listing standards without context** — naming "3-A" or "FSSAI" without saying *what* clause/parameter is satisfied loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 5. **Forgetting to convert hours to seconds** in OEE Performance — a frequent 1-mark loss.
> 6. **Hygienic design without numerical thresholds** — "polished surface" without quoting **Ra ≤ 0.8 µm** is treated as incomplete.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Key considerations = the multi-disciplinary checklist (11 pillars) ensuring safe, sanitary, economic, compliant, reliable, and flexible automation.
- **Top 3 critical pillars** (highest weight in KTU exam): **Hygienic Design, Economic Justification, Safety Engineering**.
- **Numerical must-knows:**
  - $ROI = \dfrac{\text{Net Annual Benefit}}{\text{Initial Investment}} \times 100\,\%$
  - $PP = \dfrac{C_0}{CF}$
  - $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ (World-class ≥ 85 %)
  - $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu} \ge 4000$ for CIP turbulence
  - $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$
- **Numerical thresholds to memorise:**
  - Surface finish **Ra ≤ 0.8 µm** (product contact)
  - Self-drain slope **≥ 3°**
  - Dead-leg **≤ 2D**
  - Bend radius **≥ 1.5D**
  - PLC scan time **5–20 ms**
  - IP rating for wash-down **IP66 / IP67 / IP69K**
  - CIP temperature **80 °C**, time **30 min**, detergent **1–3 % NaOH**
- **Standards grid to remember:**
  - **India** → FSSAI, BIS (IS 14773, IS 15495)
  - **USA** → FDA 21 CFR Part 11, USDA, 3-A
  - **Europe** → EU 1935/2004, EHEDG, CE Machinery Directive
  - **Global** → ISO 22000, FSSC 22000, GAMP 5, ISO 13849-1, ISA-95, ISA-88, ISA-18.2
- **Decision-rule of thumb:**
  - Accept project if $PP \le 5$ years.
  - World-class target: $OEE \ge 85\,\%$, $A \ge 90\,\%$, $Q \ge 99\,\%$.
- **Exam tip:** Always draw a **P&ID or block diagram** in 14-mark questions; it guarantees 1–2 extra marks in KTU valuation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Integration of Various Components
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT864 - AUTOMATION IN FOOD INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Integration of Various Components

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** curriculum for *Automation in Food Industry (PEFTT864)*, **Integration of Various Components** refers to the systematic interconnection of heterogeneous hardware and software elements — namely **sensors, actuators, controllers (PLC/DCS), communication networks, Human–Machine Interfaces (HMI), Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA) systems, and Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) layers** — into a unified, real-time, closed-loop automation architecture capable of monitoring, controlling, and optimizing food processing operations.

According to the *ISA-95 / IEC 62264* standard adopted in food process automation, integration is realized across five functional levels:

| Level | Functional Layer | Food Industry Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Level 0** | Field Devices (Sensors & Actuators) | PT-100 temperature probe in a pasteurizer |
| **Level 1** | Basic Control (PLC / DCS) | Allen-Bradley PLC controlling CIP skids |
| **Level 2** | Supervisory (SCADA / HMI) | Wonderware InTouch panel for a dairy plant |
| **Level 3** | Manufacturing Operations (MES) | Batch tracking in a brewery |
| **Level 4** | Enterprise (ERP) | SAP integration with finished-goods inventory |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Integration is *not* merely wiring components together — it requires harmonized **signal conditioning, communication protocols (e.g., Modbus, Profibus, EtherNet/IP, OPC-UA), data standardization, and cybersecurity layering**, all of which are examinable under the revised Bloom's Taxonomy levels *Apply, Analyze, and Evaluate*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine an orchestra. Each musician (violin, flute, drums) is like an individual component — the **sensor** listens (microphone), the **actuator** plays (loudspeaker), the **PLC** is the conductor's brain reading the sheet music (program), the **SCADA** is the conductor gesturing to the audience (HMI), and the **ERP** is the concert hall's administration handling tickets and finances.

**Integration** is the *sheet of music* — the standardized language (protocol) that ensures every instrument plays the right note at the right time, in harmony. In a **food processing plant**, if the temperature sensor detects overheating in a retort sterilizer, it must *tell* the PLC, which must *command* the steam valve (actuator) to close, while simultaneously *informing* the SCADA screen so the operator knows, and *logging* the event in the ERP for batch traceability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics to Remember:**
> - **Latency** (signal delay): typically **< 100 ms** for Level 0–1 integration
> - **Update rate** for SCADA: **1–5 seconds** typical
> - **MTBF** (Mean Time Between Failures) for integrated PLC systems: **≥ 50,000 hours**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Layer Integration Architecture

The integration of components in food industry automation follows the **Purdue Model** (also adopted by ISA-95). The breakdown is as follows:

### 2.1.1 Level 0 – Field Instrumentation
- **Sensors**: Temperature (RTD, Thermocouple), Pressure (Strain gauge), Flow (Coriolis, Magnetic), Level (Capacitive, Ultrasonic), pH, Conductivity, Humidity, Near-Infrared (NIR) for moisture/fat content.
- **Actuators**: Pneumatic valves, Motorized dampers, Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs) for conveyors, Heater cartridges, Servo-driven filling nozzles.
- **Signal Types**: 4–20 mA current loop, 0–10 V voltage, digital pulses, HART (Highway Addressable Remote Transducer).

### 2.1.2 Level 1 – Basic Control (PLC / DCS)
- **Programmable Logic Controllers (PLC)** execute Ladder Logic (LD), Function Block Diagram (FBD), or Structured Text (ST).
- **Distributed Control System (DCS)** is preferred for continuous processes (e.g., evaporation in sugar refining).
- **PID Control Loop** is the heart of analog control:
  - P (Proportional) – reduces error
  - I (Integral) – eliminates steady-state error
  - D (Derivative) – anticipates future error

### 2.1.3 Level 2 – Supervisory Control
- **SCADA** provides centralized monitoring, alarming, and trending.
- **HMI** offers graphical operator interaction.
- **Historian** databases (e.g., OSIsoft PI) store time-series data for analytics.

### 2.1.4 Level 3 – Manufacturing Execution System (MES)
- Recipe management, batch traceability, OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness) calculation, genealogy tracking — critical for **FSSAI 2006** and **ISO 22000** compliance.

### 2.1.5 Level 4 – Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP)
- Business-level planning: procurement, sales, invoicing, and regulatory reporting.

## 2.2 Communication Protocols

The integration backbone relies on standardized protocols. For KTU exam purposes, the following table is **high-yield**:

| Protocol | Type | Typical Use in Food Industry | Layer (OSI) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **4–20 mA** | Analog | Sensor-to-PLC signal transmission | Physical |
| **HART** | Hybrid (Analog + Digital) | Smart transmitter tuning | Data Link |
| **Modbus RTU / TCP** | Master–Slave Serial | PLC-to-VFD communication | Application |
| **Profibus DP / PA** | Token-passing | Fieldbus for dairy CIP systems | Fieldbus |
| **Profinet** | Real-time Ethernet | High-speed packaging lines | Application |
| **EtherNet/IP** | CIP over Ethernet | CIP skid automation | Application |
| **OPC-UA** | Service-oriented | MES / ERP data exchange | Application |
| **MQTT** | Pub/Sub over TCP/IP | IoT cloud integration for cold chain | Application |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why OPC-UA matters for KTU 2024**: It is platform-independent, supports **end-to-end encryption (AES-256)**, and is the de-facto standard for **Industry 4.0** integration of food plants.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Relationship | Description | Typical Units | Application in Food Industry |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{out} = 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{P_{meas} - P_{min}}{P_{max} - P_{min}}$ | 4–20 mA loop linearization to voltage | mA, bar, °C | Pressure transmitter in retort |
| 2 | $u(t) = K_p \cdot e(t) + K_i \int e(t)\,dt + K_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ | Standard PID controller output | dimensionless | Pasteurization temperature control |
| 3 | $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1} e^{-L s}$ | First-Order Plus Dead Time (FOPDT) model | — | Thermal process identification |
| 4 | $\text{OEE} = A \cdot P \cdot Q$ | Overall Equipment Effectiveness | dimensionless | Bottling line efficiency |
| 5 | $K_p = \dfrac{\tau}{K \cdot L}$ | Ziegler–Nichols P-gain tuning | dimensionless | Initial PID tuning |
| 6 | $K_i = \dfrac{K_p}{T_i}$ ; $K_d = K_p \cdot T_d$ | Integral & Derivative gain forms | s⁻¹, s | PI, PD loop design |
| 7 | $f_{Nyquist} = \dfrac{f_{sample}}{2}$ | Minimum sampling frequency | Hz | Digital sensor acquisition |
| 8 | $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | dB | NIR sensor accuracy |
| 9 | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{N_{units}}{t_{cycle}}$ | Line throughput | units/min | Packaging line integration |
| 10 | $\Delta T_{lag} = \dfrac{m \cdot c_p}{h \cdot A}$ | Thermal lag time constant | s | Heat exchanger response |

> [!WARNING]
> When transcribing the **4–20 mA formula** or any absolute-value expression in your KTU answer scripts, board examiners specifically look for correct units and clear delimitation. Avoid writing **|x|** — instead write **\vert x \vert** or **mod x** to maintain mathematical clarity.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

The integrated architecture is the backbone of:
- **Cold-chain traceability** (dairy, frozen foods) — temperature data logged every **5 seconds** via MQTT to cloud ERP.
- **CIP (Clean-In-Place) automation** — recipe-driven sequencing of valves, pumps, and conductivity sensors.
- **Batch pasteurization** — PLC controls temperature ramp/hold, SCADA logs F₀ lethality value, MES generates compliance report.
- **Robotic pick-and-place in packaging** — Vision system + Servo + PLC + HMI synchronized via EtherCAT or Profinet.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: 4–20 mA Loop Calibration

Consider a **temperature sensor** in a UHT (Ultra-High Temperature) processing line with measurement range **$T_{min} = 0\,°C$** and **$T_{max} = 150\,°C$**. The sensor outputs a current loop signal. We must derive the relationship between measured current and actual temperature.

**Step 1:** Define the linear mapping principle.
The 4–20 mA standard defines a linear mapping from physical variable to current:
$$I_{out} = 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{P_{meas} - P_{min}}{P_{max} - P_{min}}$$

**Step 2:** Define the inverse mapping (current to temperature).
Rearranging for $P_{meas}$:
$$P_{meas} = P_{min} + \dfrac{(I_{out} - 4)}{16} \cdot (P_{max} - P_{min})$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values for the UHT sensor.
With $P_{min} = 0\,°C$, $P_{max} = 150\,°C$:
$$T_{meas} = 0 + \dfrac{(I_{out} - 4)}{16} \cdot (150 - 0)$$
$$T_{meas} = \dfrac{(I_{out} - 4)}{16} \cdot 150$$

**Step 4:** Verification at end-points.
\begin{aligned}
I_{out} = 4 \text{ mA} &\Rightarrow T_{meas} = \dfrac{(4-4)}{16} \cdot 150 = 0\,°C \quad \checkmark \\
I_{out} = 20 \text{ mA} &\Rightarrow T_{meas} = \dfrac{(20-4)}{16} \cdot 150 = 150\,°C \quad \checkmark \\
I_{out} = 12 \text{ mA} &\Rightarrow T_{meas} = \dfrac{(12-4)}{16} \cdot 150 = 75\,°C \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 4 mA and not 0 mA?** The **4 mA "live zero"** allows detection of a broken wire (0 mA = fault), increasing system reliability — a key advantage for **food safety-critical** circuits.

## 3.2 Derivation: PID Controller Output for Pasteurization

A continuous stirred-tank pasteurizer is to be controlled at **$T_{set} = 72\,°C$** for HTST (High Temperature Short Time) milk processing. The PID output drives a steam control valve (0–100% opening).

**Step 1:** Define the error signal.
$$e(t) = T_{set} - T_{measured}(t)$$

**Step 2:** Write the standard parallel-form PID equation.
$$u(t) = K_p \cdot e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 3:** Discretize for PLC implementation (sampling period $\Delta t$).
$$u_n = K_p e_n + K_i \sum_{k=0}^{n} e_k \Delta t + K_d \dfrac{e_n - e_{n-1}}{\Delta t}$$

**Step 4:** Apply anti-windup correction (essential for batch food processes).
$$u_{sat} = \begin{cases} u_{max}, & u_{raw} > u_{max} \\ u_{raw}, & u_{min} \le u_{raw} \le u_{max} \\ u_{min}, & u_{raw} < u_{min} \end{cases}$$

**Step 5:** Numerical example.
Suppose $K_p = 2.5$, $K_i = 0.15\,\text{s}^{-1}$, $K_d = 0.8\,\text{s}$, and at instant $t = 10\,\text{s}$, the measured temperature is $T_{meas} = 70.2\,°C$. Also, $\int e\,dt \approx 18.5\,°C\cdot s$ and $\dfrac{de}{dt} \approx -0.05\,°C/s$ (temperature approaching setpoint).

\begin{aligned}
e(t) &= 72 - 70.2 = 1.8\,°C \\
u(t) &= (2.5)(1.8) + (0.15)(18.5) + (0.8)(-0.05) \\
     &= 4.50 + 2.775 - 0.04 \\
     &= 7.235\%
\end{aligned}

Therefore, the steam valve opens approximately **7.24 %**, indicating a small corrective action as the system is approaching setpoint.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Integration of Components vs. Process Requirements

| Process Requirement | Sensor | Actuator | Controller | Protocol | SCADA/HMI Function |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Temperature** (Pasteurizer) | RTD / TC | Steam valve | PLC PID loop | 4–20 mA + HART | Trend + Alarm |
| **Flow** (CIP) | Magnetic flowmeter | Pump VFD | PLC cascade loop | Profibus PA | Recipe execution |
| **Level** (Silo) | Ultrasonic | Rotary feeder | PLC on/off | Modbus TCP | Inventory dashboard |
| **Pressure** (Retort) | Strain gauge | Safety valve | DCS | 4–20 mA | F₀ calculation |
| **pH** (Yoghurt) | Glass electrode | Dosing pump | PLC PI loop | HART | Lot traceability |
| **Vision** (Sorting) | CMOS camera | Pneumatic ejector | PC + PLC | GigE Vision | Defect analytics |
| **Weight** (Filling) | Load cell | Servo valve | PLC + PID | EtherNet/IP | Net weight SQC |

## 3.4 Throughput Calculation for an Integrated Packaging Line

A beverage bottling line integrates a rinser, filler, capper, labeler, and shrink-wrapper. Each station has its own cycle time. The overall line throughput is governed by the **bottleneck**.

$$t_{cycle,\,line} = \max\{t_{rinse},\, t_{fill},\, t_{cap},\, t_{label},\, t_{wrap}\}$$

If $t_{cycle} = 4.5\,\text{s}$ (filler is the bottleneck), then:

$$\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{3600}{4.5} = 800 \text{ bottles/hour}$$

**OEE calculation:**
$$\text{OEE} = A \cdot P \cdot Q$$

If availability $A = 0.92$, performance $P = 0.88$, quality $Q = 0.98$:

$$\text{OEE} = 0.92 \cdot 0.88 \cdot 0.98 = 0.793 = 79.3\%$$

This OEE value falls in the *typical* range for **food industry** (60–85%), as confirmed by industry benchmarks from *ABB White Paper on Food & Beverage Automation (2022)*.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
