# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Cost-Benefit Analysis as applied to the food industry. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Cost-Benefit Analysis (CBA) is a quantitative economic evaluation technique used to assess the financial viability of an automation project in the food industry. It involves systematically identifying all **costs** (capital investment, operating expenses, maintenance) and **benefits** (labour savings, increased throughput, quality improvement, wastage reduction) over the project's life cycle, discounting them to present value using a suitable **discount rate**, and comparing them. A project is accepted if the **Net Present Value (NPV) > 0** and the **Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR) > 1**.

---

### Question 2: What is the difference between Net Present Value (NPV) and Internal Rate of Return (IRR)? [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | NPV | IRR |
|--------|-----|-----|
| Definition | Absolute monetary value created by the project | Discount rate at which NPV becomes zero |
| Unit | Rupees (₹) | Percentage (%) |
| Decision Rule | Accept if NPV > 0 | Accept if IRR > MARR (Minimum Attractive Rate of Return) |
| Reliability | Considered the most reliable single criterion | Can give multiple values for non-conventional cash flows |
| Use | Best for ranking mutually exclusive projects | Best for communicating to non-financial managers |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the step-by-step methodology of conducting a Cost-Benefit Analysis for installing an **automated aseptic packaging line** in a fruit juice processing plant. Clearly state the formulas for NPV, BCR, and Payback Period. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A food processing company is evaluating the installation of an **automated sorting and grading machine** for mangoes. The initial investment is **₹30,00,000** with a life of **6 years** and no salvage value. Annual labour cost saved is **₹7,00,000**, additional revenue from premium-graded mangoes is **₹2,00,000/year**, and annual operating and maintenance cost is **₹3,00,000**. Taking a discount rate of **12% p.a.**, calculate the **NPV** and **BCR**, and comment on the project's feasibility. **[7 marks]**

---

### Model Answer — Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Define the Project Boundary** — Identify the aseptic packaging line's scope, project life (typically 10 years for such capital equipment), and base year for discounting.

**Step 2: Identify Costs** —

- Capital cost (₹) of the aseptic filler, sterilizer, PLC, and conveyors.
- Installation, civil work, and commissioning.
- Annual operating costs: steam, electricity, CIP chemicals, spares.
- Annual labour for supervision and quality control.
- Annual maintenance contract.
- Training cost for operators.
- Decommissioning cost at end of life.

**Step 3: Identify Benefits** —

- Reduction in manual labour (number of workers × annual salary).
- Increased throughput (extra units × contribution margin).
- Reduced product wastage due to accurate aseptic filling.
- Extended shelf life → reduced recalls and rejections.
- Premium pricing for consistent aseptic quality.
- Possible salvage value at end of life.

**Step 4: Apply Discounting** — Convert each year's net cash flow to present value using:

$$
DF_t = \frac{1}{(1+r)^t}
$$

**Step 5: Apply Decision Criteria Formulas** —

$$
NPV = -C_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{B_t - C_t}{(1+r)^t}
$$

$$
BCR = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{B_t}{(1+r)^t}}{C_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{C_t}{(1+r)^t}}
$$

$$
PBP = \frac{C_0}{\text{Annual Net Cash Flow}}
$$

**Step 6: Sensitivity Analysis** — Test outcomes under ±20% variation in throughput, ±2% variation in discount rate, and ±15% variation in operating cost.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**

- $C_0 = 30,00,000$
- $n = 6$ years
- Salvage $S = 0$
- $B_t = 7,00,000 + 2,00,000 = 9,00,000$
- $C_t = 3,00,000$
- $r = 0.12$
- Net annual cash flow: $A = 9,00,000 - 3,00,000 = 6,00,000$

**Step 1 — Compute Discount Factors at 12%:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DF_1 &= 0.8929 \\
DF_2 &= 0.7972 \\
DF_3 &= 0.7118 \\
DF_4 &= 0.6355 \\
DF_5 &= 0.5674 \\
DF_6 &= 0.5066 \\
\text{Sum of DFs} &= 4.1114
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute PV of Net Cash Flows:**

$$
PV_{\text{net}} = 6,00,000 \times 4.1114 = 24,66,840
$$

**Step 3 — Compute NPV:**

$$
NPV = -30,00,000 + 24,66,840 = -5,33,160
$$

Since **NPV < 0**, the project is **NOT acceptable** on a strict NPV basis.

**Step 4 — Compute BCR:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV_{\text{benefits}} &= 9,00,000 \times 4.1114 = 37,00,260 \\
PV_{\text{costs}} &= 30,00,000 + (3,00,000 \times 4.1114) \\
&= 30,00,000 + 12,33,420 = 42,33,420 \\
BCR &= \frac{37,00,260}{42,33,420} = 0.874
\end{aligned}
$$

Since **BCR = 0.874 < 1**, the project returns only **₹0.87 per ₹1 invested** — confirming the NPV verdict.

**Step 5 — Verdict & Recommendation:**

The project is **not financially feasible** at a 12% discount rate. The management should either:

- Negotiate a lower capital cost (target initial investment < ₹24,67,000), OR
- Increase throughput assumptions to raise annual benefits, OR
- Extend project life to 8+ years to spread benefits over more discounting periods.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **key economic indicators** used to evaluate automation projects in the food industry, highlighting why NPV is considered the most reliable single criterion. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A small-scale cashew processing unit is investing in a **PLC-based roasting automation system** costing **₹12,00,000** with a life of **5 years** and salvage value of **₹1,00,000**. The annual cash inflows (savings) are **₹3,50,000** and annual outflows (expenses) are **₹50,000**. Using a discount rate of **10%**, compute the **NPV**, **BCR**, and **Simple Payback Period**. Advise the company on whether to invest. **[7 marks]**

---

### Model Answer — Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

The **key economic indicators** for evaluating food industry automation projects are:

1. **Net Present Value (NPV)** — The present value of all net cash flows minus the initial investment. It is considered the **most reliable single criterion** because:
   - It directly measures wealth creation in absolute monetary terms.
   - It accounts for the **time value of money**.
   - It considers **all cash flows** over the entire project life.
   - It allows correct ranking of **mutually exclusive** projects of different scales.

2. **Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR)** — Useful for comparing projects of different magnitudes; however, it cannot independently rank mutually exclusive alternatives.

3. **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** — Easy to communicate but suffers from potential multiple solutions for non-conventional cash flow patterns.

4. **Payback Period (PBP)** — A liquidity measure; ignores cash flows after payback and ignores time value of money (in its simple form).

5. **Return on Investment (ROI)** — A simple percentage efficiency metric, best used for quick screening of proposals.

> NPV is preferred because it is a **direct, additive, and theoretically sound** measure of project worth, while the others are relative or limited indicators.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**

- $C_0 = 12,00,000$, $n = 5$, $S = 1,00,000$, $r = 0.10$
- $B_t = 3,50,000$, $C_t = 50,000$
- Net annual cash flow: $A = 3,50,000 - 50,000 = 3,00,000$

**Step 1 — Discount Factors at 10%:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DF_1 &= 0.9091 \\
DF_2 &= 0.8264 \\
DF_3 &= 0.7513 \\
DF_4 &= 0.6830 \\
DF_5 &= 0.6209 \\
\text{Sum} &= 3.7907
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — NPV Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV_{\text{net CF}} &= 3,00,000 \times 3.7907 = 11,37,210 \\
PV_{\text{salvage}} &= 1,00,000 \times 0.6209 = 62,090 \\
NPV &= -12,00,000 + 11,37,210 + 62,090 = -865
\end{aligned}
$$

The NPV is **marginally negative (–₹865)**, indicating the project is on the borderline of acceptability.

**Step 3 — BCR Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV_{\text{benefits}} &= (3,50,000 \times 3.7907) + 62,090 = 13,26,745 + 62,090 = 13,88,835 \\
PV_{\text{costs}} &= 12,00,000 + (50,000 \times 3.7907) = 12,00,000 + 1,89,535 = 13,89,535 \\
BCR &= \frac{13,88,835}{13,89,535} = 0.9995
\end{aligned}
$$

Since **BCR = 0.9995 < 1**, on strict criterion the project is **not acceptable**, but is so close to 1 that it warrants renegotiation.

**Step 4 — Simple Payback Period:**

$$
PBP = \frac{12,00,000}{3,00,000} = 4 \text{ years}
$$

The payback of 4 years is exactly equal to the project life of 5 years (excluding salvage), which is **not attractive**.

**Step 5 — Verdict:**

The project is **not recommended in its current form**. The owner should:

- Seek a **5–10% reduction** in capital cost, OR
- Increase the **annual benefits** by improving throughput assumptions, OR
- Seek a **government subsidy** (e.g., MSME technology upgradation scheme) to lower the effective initial investment.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to include the salvage value** as a benefit in year $n$ — costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Using percentage in the discount factor** (e.g., `1.10^5` instead of `1 + 0.10` raised to 5) — major error.
> 3. **Confusing simple and discounted payback** — examiners want the **discounted** version for full credit in advanced questions.
> 4. **Not stating the decision rule clearly** (NPV > 0, BCR > 1) after computation — students often compute but forget to conclude.
> 5. **Ignoring the salvage value** in BCR denominator — it should only be in the numerator.
> 6. **Failing to convert discount rate from % to decimal** — a frequent calculation error worth 1 mark.
> 7. **Not recommending corrective action** (sensitivity analysis comment) — KTU examiners reward practical engineering judgment.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Scheme**

- **CBA = Monetary comparison of discounted costs vs. discounted benefits** over a project's life.
- **Time Value of Money:** ₹1 today > ₹1 in the future → always discount future cash flows.
- **Discount Factor Formula:** $DF_t = \dfrac{1}{(1+r)^t}$ — use $r$ as decimal.
- **NPV Decision Rule:** Accept if **NPV ≥ 0**; reject if NPV < 0.
- **BCR Decision Rule:** Accept if **BCR ≥ 1**; reject if BCR < 1.
- **IRR Decision Rule:** Accept if **IRR > MARR** (Minimum Attractive Rate of Return, usually 12–15%).
- **PBP Decision Rule:** Accept if **PBP ≤ target years** (often 3–5 years for SME food units).
- **Always include the salvage value** as a year-$n$ benefit in NPV and BCR calculations.
- **Always convert % to decimal** before substituting into formulas.
- **Sensitivity Analysis** is essential — vary discount rate, throughput, and operating cost to test robustness.
- **NPV is the gold standard** for ranking mutually exclusive projects; BCR and IRR are supportive metrics.
- **Common KTU food industry CBA applications:** PLC upgrades, robotic packaging, vision-based QC, CIP automation, cold chain automation, ASRS (Automated Storage & Retrieval Systems).
- **Mutually Exclusive vs. Independent Projects:** Use NPV for ranking mutually exclusive projects; BCR/IRR for screening independent ones.
- **Inflation effect:** Discount rate $r$ should ideally be a real (inflation-adjusted) rate or nominal rate with inflation included in cash flow projections — examiners may test this concept.
- **Memorize the SPPWF and Annuity formulas** — they are the foundation for solving **every** numerical problem in this topic.
- **Practical Engineering Insight:** A food unit should typically require **BCR > 1.25** and **PBP < 4 years** to comfortably proceed with automation in the Indian MSME context.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Studies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT864 - AUTOMATION IN FOOD INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Case Studies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Case Study** in the context of food industry automation is a systematic, in-depth investigation of a real-world food processing/manufacturing facility where automation technologies (PLCs, SCADA, DCS, robotics, sensors, vision systems, and industrial IoT) have been implemented. It involves documenting the **pre-automation baseline**, the **automation architecture implemented**, the **instrumentation and control philosophy**, and the **post-automation performance metrics** such as throughput, yield, quality, energy efficiency, and operator safety.

As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme** outcome-based framework for **PEFTT864**, case studies are pedagogical instruments designed to map the theoretical concepts of sensors, actuators, PLC programming, SCADA architecture, and process control onto actual industrial deployments, thereby validating **Course Outcomes (CO3 & CO4)** relating to the design and analysis of automated food systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEFTT864 – Module 3):**
> Case studies form the *integrative capstone* of the automation course. They require students to synthesise knowledge from Modules 1 (Sensors & Transducers), 2 (Actuators, PLC & SCADA), and 3 (Industrial Applications) into a unified engineering analysis.

### 1.2 Key Vocabulary of Food Industry Case Studies

| Term | Meaning in Food Automation Context |
|---|---|
| **Baseline (As-Is)** | Production metrics before automation (e.g., manual yield, labour hours, defect rate) |
| **Target (To-Be)** | Desired performance metrics post-automation |
| **Gap Analysis** | Quantified difference between baseline and target |
| **Control Philosophy** | Narrative + P&ID describing how sensors, controllers, and actuators interact |
| **CAPEX** | Capital Expenditure – one-time cost of automation hardware & installation |
| **OPEX** | Operational Expenditure – recurring cost of running the automated line |
| **OEE** | Overall Equipment Effectiveness – the gold-standard productivity metric |
| **Validation** | Documented proof that the automated system produces food meeting FSSAI/ISO standards |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a Case Study as a "Medical Case File" for a Factory.**
> Just as a doctor documents a patient's symptoms, diagnosis, treatment, and recovery, an automation case study documents a factory's *production symptoms* (low yield, high wastage), *diagnosis* (root cause – manual errors, inconsistent process), *treatment* (PLC + SCADA + Sensors), and *recovery* (improved OEE, lower rejection rate).
> The "patient" is a **milk pasteurisation line**; the "stethoscope" is a **temperature transmitter (RTD)**; the "surgeon" is a **PID controller in a PLC**; and the "ICU monitor" is the **SCADA HMI screen**.

### 1.4 Why Case Studies Matter in PEFTT864

1. **Bridging Theory-to-Practice Gap** – Theory on PID tuning becomes meaningful only when applied to a *real*烘焙 oven temperature loop.
2. **Exam Relevance** – KTU frequently tests whether students can read a process description and propose an automation architecture with justified component selection.
3. **Industry Readiness** – Food processing companies (Nestlé, Amul, Britannia, ITC Foods) hire engineers who can interpret and design such integrated systems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Standard Case Study Analysis Framework

A KTU-grade case study analysis must follow this **five-pillar structure** (this is exactly how examiners expect answers in the 14-mark question):

1. **Plant / Product Description** – What is being manufactured? What are the critical quality parameters?
2. **Process Flow Breakdown** – Unit operations from raw material receipt to finished goods dispatch.
3. **Automation Architecture** – Sensors → Signal Conditioning → PLC/DCS → Actuators → HMI/SCADA.
4. **Control Strategy** – Open-loop / Closed-loop / Sequential / Batch / Continuous, with justification.
5. **Performance Evaluation** – OEE, payback period, ROI, defect rate reduction, energy savings.

### 2.2 The "Why & How" Behind Each Pillar

#### Pillar 1: Plant / Product Description
**Why:** Without defining what we are making, we cannot identify *which* parameters must be controlled (e.g., *moisture* in biscuits, *Brix* in juice, *fat %* in ghee).
**How:** Identify the **Critical Quality Attributes (CQAs)** of the food product and trace them back to **Critical Process Parameters (CPPs)**.

#### Pillar 2: Process Flow Breakdown
**Why:** Each unit operation (cleaning, pasteurisation, homogenisation, filling, sealing, labelling) has its own automation need.
**How:** Draw a **block flow diagram (BFD)** and a **piping & instrumentation diagram (P&ID)**.

#### Pillar 3: Automation Architecture
**Why:** This is the *technical heart* of the case study – the answer to *"What components are used and why?"*
**How:** Build the **sensor–controller–actuator loop** for each CPP, following **ISA-88 / ISA-95** batch control standards where applicable.

#### Pillar 4: Control Strategy
**Why:** Choosing between ON-OFF, PID, Cascade, Feedforward, or Model-Predictive Control must be justified by the process dynamics.
**How:** Apply the **Three-Mode PID rule of thumb**:
   - Fast loop (flow) → **High P-gain, Low I, No D**
   - Slow loop (temperature) → **Moderate P, Moderate I, Optional D**
   - Noisy loop (level) → **Low P, High I**

#### Pillar 5: Performance Evaluation
**Why:** Automation is justified by **measurable improvement** – not just technology deployment.
**How:** Quantify using the formulas in the cheat-sheet below.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Metric / Equation | Formula | Variables & Units | Typical Food Industry Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **OEE** | $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | $A$ = Availability, $P$ = Performance, $Q$ = Quality (all decimals) | World-class ≥ **0.85** |
| 2 | **Availability** | $A = \dfrac{\text{Planned Production Time} - \text{Downtime}}{\text{Planned Production Time}}$ | Time in minutes/hours | 0.90 – 0.99 |
| 3 | **Performance** | $P = \dfrac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces Produced}}{\text{Run Time}}$ | Cycle time in seconds, pieces in count | 0.90 – 0.95 |
| 4 | **Quality** | $Q = \dfrac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces Produced}}$ | Dimensionless ratio | 0.95 – 0.999 |
| 5 | **Throughput** | $\text{TH} = \dfrac{\text{Total Output}}{\text{Total Time}}$ | Units $\vert$ kg $\vert$ L per hour | Plant-specific |
| 6 | **Payback Period** | $\text{PP} = \dfrac{\text{CAPEX}}{\text{Annual Net Cash Savings}}$ | Currency $\vert$ years | Target ≤ 3 years |
| 7 | **ROI** | $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{\text{Annual Savings} - \text{Depreciation}}{\text{CAPEX}} \times 100$ | Percentage | Target ≥ 25% |
| 8 | **PID Output (Velocity Form)** | $\Delta u(t) = K_c\!\left[\Delta e(t) + \dfrac{\Delta e(t)}{T_i} + T_d \dfrac{\Delta^2 e(t)}{\Delta t}\right]$ | $K_c, T_i, T_d$ tuning constants | Tuning-parameter-specific |
| 9 | **First-Order Process Response** | $y(t) = K\, u\!\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$ | $K$ = process gain, $\tau$ = time constant (s) | Pasteuriser $\tau \approx$ 30–120 s |
| 10 | **Heat Transfer (Pasteuriser)** | $Q = \dot{m}\, c_p\, \Delta T$ | $Q$ in W, $\dot{m}$ in kg/s | Used in energy balance |

> [!TIP]
> **Critical Reminder for Table Reading:** When examiners quote "OEE = 0.78", interpret it as **78%**, not 0.78 of 100. Many students lose 1–2 marks by missing this decimal placement.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Plant Engineers** use these formulas in *commissioning reports* and *annual productivity reviews*.
- **Food Safety Auditors (FSSC 22000, BRCGS)** use Availability and Quality indices as indirect *food safety indicators* (a broken-down filler is a *contamination risk*).
- **Management Consultants** use Payback Period and ROI to decide between two competing automation bids.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example: OEE Calculation for an Automated Dairy Plant

**Scenario (KTU-style):**
> An automated UHT milk packaging line at a dairy plant runs two shifts of 8 hours each, 6 days a week. The line is rated for an *ideal cycle time* of **3.0 seconds per 1-litre carton**. Last month's records show:
> - Total scheduled production time = 280 hours
> - Unplanned downtime (CIP, changeover, breakdown) = 32 hours
> - Total cartons produced = 245,000
> - Rejected cartons (leaking, misprinted, underweight) = 8,500
>
> **Calculate the OEE and interpret the result.**

---

#### Step 1 — Availability ($A$)

The *Planned Production Time* excludes scheduled breaks (already accounted for in the 8-hr shift), so we directly subtract downtime.

$$
A = \dfrac{\text{Planned Production Time} - \text{Downtime}}{\text{Planned Production Time}}
$$

Substitute values:

$$
A = \dfrac{280 - 32}{280} = \dfrac{248}{280}
$$

$$
A = 0.8857 \;\approx\; 88.57\%
$$

**Interpretation:** The line is *available* for production 88.57% of the time. The remaining ~11.4% is lost to CIP (Clean-in-Place) and changeovers.

---

#### Step 2 — Performance ($P$)

Performance measures *speed losses* — running slower than the ideal cycle time.

$$
P = \dfrac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces Produced}}{\text{Run Time}}
$$

**Run Time** = Planned Production Time − Downtime = 248 hours.

Convert units: Run Time = $248 \times 3600 = 892{,}800$ seconds.

$$
P = \dfrac{3.0 \;\text{s/carton} \times 245{,}000 \;\text{cartons}}{892{,}800 \;\text{s}}
$$

$$
P = \dfrac{735{,}000}{892{,}800} = 0.8233 \;\approx\; 82.33\%
$$

**Interpretation:** The line runs at 82.33% of its theoretical maximum speed. The 17.67% loss is due to *minor stops*, *reduced speed* during start-up, and operator-induced slowdowns.

---

#### Step 3 — Quality ($Q$)

$$
Q = \dfrac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces Produced}}
$$

Good Pieces = 245,000 − 8,500 = 236,500.

$$
Q = \dfrac{236{,}500}{245{,}000} = 0.9653 \;\approx\; 96.53\%
$$

**Interpretation:** 96.53% of the cartons produced meet quality specifications. The 3.47% rejection includes leaking cartons (seal-temperature control issue) and underweight cartons (filling-nozzle calibration issue).

---

#### Step 4 — OEE

$$
\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q
$$

$$
\text{OEE} = 0.8857 \times 0.8233 \times 0.9653
$$

Let us compute step by step:

$$
0.8857 \times 0.8233 = 0.7292
$$

$$
0.7292 \times 0.9653 = 0.7039
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{OEE} = 0.7039 \;\approx\; 70.4\%}
$$

---

#### Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation

The **KTU benchmark table** for OEE interpretation:

| OEE Range | World-Class Rating | Typical Action Required |
|---|---|---|
| ≥ 85% | **World-Class** | Maintain, fine-tune |
| 60% – 85% | **Typical** (most food plants) | Targeted improvement projects |
| < 60% | **Low** | Major intervention / re-design |

**Our OEE of 70.4%** falls in the *typical* range. To reach 85% (world-class), the dairy must:
1. **Reduce downtime** by upgrading CIP automation (add conductivity sensor-based auto-rinse termination).
2. **Improve Performance** by tuning the filler's servo drive ramp profile.
3. **Reduce rejections** by installing a **vision inspection system** with auto-reject diverter.

---

### 3.2 Payback Period Derivation (Cost-Benefit Extension)

**Scenario:** The same dairy invests ₹ 1.5 Crore in the suggested upgrades, expecting annual savings of ₹ 55 Lakh.

$$
\text{PP} = \dfrac{\text{CAPEX}}{\text{Annual Net Savings}}
$$

$$
\text{PP} = \dfrac{1{,}50{,}00{,}000}{55{,}00{,}000} = 2.727 \;\text{years}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Payback Period} \approx 2\text{ years }9\text{ months}}
$$

This is well within the **food industry benchmark of ≤ 3 years**, justifying the CAPEX approval.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Automation Case Study Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Material Receipt] --> B[Weighing and Sorting]

    subgraph SENSOR_LAYER["SENSOR LAYER"]
        S1[Load Cell] -.-> B
        S2[Vision Camera] -.-> E
        S3[Level Transmitter] -.-> D
        S4[Temperature RTD] -.-> D
        S5[Flow Meter] -.-> F
        S6[Pressure Sensor] -.-> F

```

### 4.2 Detailed Case Study Flow — Automated Bread/Bakery Plant

```mermaid

    subgraph BAKE["STAGE 4 BAKING"]
        BK1[Tunnel Oven]

    IA2 --> MB1
```
